member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression
member.summary: An Arcade expression object, containing an expression, a name, a return type and a title.
member.remarks:
An Arcade expression object gathers an Arcade script expression together with:
The expected return type.
The name that a map author uses to refer to it.
The title that the author uses to describe the expression.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression.#ctor(System.String)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class with an expression script.
member.param: The expression to be evaluated. If set to an empty expression string then the evaluation will return null.
member.remarks: The and will default to empty strings. The will default to .

member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class with an expression string, title, and name.
member.param: The expression to be evaluated. If set to an empty expression string then the evaluation will return null.
The title of the expression.
The name of the expression.
member.remarks: The will default to .

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression.#ctor(System.String,System.String,System.String,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class with an expression string, title, name, and return type.
member.remarks: If the Arcade expression evaluates to a result which differs from then the result will be cast to the expected return type. If the cast cannot be carried out, the result will be a type-specific error value. See for details.
member.param: The Arcade expression to be evaluated. If set to an empty expression string then the evaluation will return zero, false, an empty string, or null, depending on whether the is , , or anything else, respectively.
The title of the expression.
The name of the expression.
The type to cast the evaluated value to.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression.Expression
member.summary: Gets the Arcade expression string.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression.Title
member.summary: Gets the title that the author uses to describe the expression.
member.remarks: If not set explicitly, then the default is an empty title string.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression.Name
member.summary: Gets the name that the author uses to refer to the expression.
member.remarks: If not set explicitly, then the default is an empty name string.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression.ReturnType
member.summary: Gets the expected return type of the expression.
member.remarks: If the Arcade expression evaluates to a result which differs from then the result will be cast to the expected return type. If the cast cannot be carried out, the result will be a type-specific error value. See for details.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression.Equals(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression)
member.summary: Tests if two Arcade expression objects are equal.
member.param: The expression to compare to.
member.returns: True if this Arcade expression and the other Arcade expression are equivalent, false if they are not.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression.UnknownJson
member.summary: Gets a collection of unknown data from the source JSON.
member.remarks: Unknown JSON is a dictionary of values not defined in the ArcGIS specification used to create this object but found in the source JSON. If the object is written back to JSON, any unknown JSON data is not persisted. The ArcGIS specification may be for a web map, web scene, REST API, and so on.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression.UnsupportedJson
member.summary: Gets a collection of unsupported data from the source JSON.
member.remarks: Unsupported JSON is a dictionary of values defined in the ArcGIS specification used to create this object but not currently used in this API. If the object is written back to JSON, any unsupported JSON data is persisted. The ArcGIS specification may be from a web map, web scene, REST API, and so on.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression.FromJson(System.String)
member.summary: Creates an arcade expression from an ArcGIS JSON representation (expressionInfo).
member.param: The JSON string for the arcade expression.
member.returns: A new object.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpression.ToJson
member.summary: Converts this arcade expression to an ArcGIS JSON representation (expressionInfo).
member.returns: A string containing the JSON representation of the arcade expression.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType
member.summary: The expected type of the result calculated by an expression described within an

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType.Automatic
member.summary: The return type of the expression will be automatically determined based on the script calculation.
member.remarks: The return type is determined by the return value of the script. Different paths through the script might result in different result types. For example, depending on input values, it might return a number, a string, a date or an array.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType.Number
member.summary: The return type is expected to be a floating-point number.
member.remarks:
Arcade will convert the return type to a double via the following transformations:
booleans return 1 if true, 0 otherwise.
integral types are converted to double. This may be a narrowing conversion.
double values are returned unchanged.
null is converted to 0.
strings are parsed as double.
all other types are converted to NaN.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType.String
member.summary: The return type is expected to be a string.
member.remarks: Arcade will convert the return type to a string via the Arcade Text() function.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType.Dictionary
member.summary: The return type is expected to be a dictionary of key/value pairs.
member.remarks:
Arcade will convert the return type to a dictionary of key/value pairs via the following transformations:
dictionaries are returned unchanged.
all other types are converted to null.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType.Any
member.summary: The return type is expected to be any of the other types. 'Any' implies that the application evaluating the expression will need to check the result for its type and use the value appropriately.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType.Boolean
member.summary: The return type is expected to be a boolean value of true or false.
member.remarks:
Arcade will convert the return type to a boolean via the following transformations:
booleans are returned unchanged.
numbers are true if not equal to 0 or NaN, false otherwise.
strings are true if a lower-case comparison is equal to 'true', false otherwise.
all other types are false.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType.Date
member.summary: The return type is expected to be a value.
member.remarks:
Arcade converts the return type to a via the following transformations:
objects are returned unchanged.
objects are converted by assuming the time is midnight.
objects are converted to match local time.
strings are parsed if they match the ISO 8601 format, YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS.
all other types are converted to null.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType.DateOnly
member.summary: The return type is expected to be a value.
member.remarks:
Arcade converts the return type to a via the following transformations:
objects are returned unchanged.
objects are converted by removing their time component.
objects are converted by removing their time component and offset.
strings are parsed if they match the ISO 8601 format, YYYY-MM-DD.
all other types are converted to null.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType.TimeOnly
member.summary: The return type is expected to be a value.
member.remarks:
Arcade converts the return type to a via the following transformations:
objects are returned unchanged.
objects are converted by removing their date component.
objects are converted by removing their date component and offset.
strings are parsed if they match the ISO 8601 format, HH:MM:SS.
all other types are converted to null.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType.TimestampOffset
member.summary: The return type is expected to be a value.
member.remarks:
Arcade converts the return type to a via the following transformations:
objects are returned unchanged.
objects display their offset from UTC based on their timezone.
objects are converted assuming the time is midnight with no offset from UTC.
strings are parsed if they match the ISO 8601 format, YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS[+/-]HH::MM.
all other types are converted to null.

member.summary: The result from the successful evaluation of an Arcade expression.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Arcade.ArcadeEvaluationResult.CastTo(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcadeExpressionReturnType)
member.summary: Cast the result of an evaluation to another type using the Arcade casting rules.
member.remarks: Use to ensure that the final return value from a script is cast to an expected type in an Arcade-consistent manner. For more information on the casting behavior for each type, see . Only types to which a value can be cast are available in . Other types, such as , should be accessed via the result property. An exception is not thrown when casting fails. Instead, a type-specific error value is returned whose value depends on the type of cast attempted.
member.param: The type to cast to.
member.returns: An object of the requested type or null.

member.returns: true if this and the other are equivalent, false if they are not.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Arcade.ArcadeEvaluator
member.summary: Allows you to evaluate an under a given .

remarks.para: This object allows you to set up, execute and query information about an Arcade script. For more general information on Arcade and its usages we refer you to the Arcade online documentation.

To construct the object you need to supply an along with an . is an object that contains the Arcade script plus some additional metadata while specifies availability of profile variables and limits what functions are available.
The basic workflow is:

list.item: Construct an object using and .
Query information about a script.
Insert the required profile values into a dictionary.
Call .
Use the returned .

remarks.para: From an object you can query information to determine:

list.item: Which profile variables are accessed by the script.
Which attributes are used by a particular profile variable.
If the script is considered stable.

remarks.para: Not all scripts use all the profiles variables. returns an array of the names of the profile variables used in the current script. Possible uses include:

list.item: Informing dependency analysis to determine which scripts need to run when a value of a profile variable changes.
Minimizing expensive calculation of unneeded profile variables.

remarks.para: lets you dig deeper to determine which attributes are required on a specific profile variable. If you are querying a subset of attributes when querying a feature, use this information to ensure the profile variable will have the attributes it requires.

property indicates if the given script will return the same value for an identical set of profile variables. For this to be true a script must avoid using unstable builtin functions such as now() or random().

can be combined with the knowledge of the used profile variables to construct caches of results. This lets you avoid running scripts when repeated calls with the same profiles variables will not alter the evaluation result.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Arcade.ArcadeEvaluator.EvaluateAsync(System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{System.Collections.Generic.KeyValuePair{System.String,System.Object}})
member.summary: Evaluates the script using the supplied profile variables.

remarks.para: Each entry of the profile variables dictionary is placed into the interpreter as a value accessible throughout the script. The name and type of these should comply with the profile the has been constructed with. Values are not persisted across evaluations, meaning all required profile variables must be supplied with each evaluation.
Each value of a key/value pair in the dictionary is converted to a type understood by the interpreter.
Values in the dictionary can be any of the following types:

list.item: Numbers, integer and floating point types of various sizes. Arcade uses doubles for all internal calculations.
Boolean.
(or anything derived from it such as ).
, to supported types.
(or anything derived from it such as or ).

remarks.para: To be explicit on the conversions of compound types.

list.item: types are converted to an Arcade Feature type.
is converted to an Arcade Feature type.
types are converted to an Arcade FeatureSet type.
is converted to Arcade FeatureSetCollection type.
is converted to Arcade FeatureSetCollection type.
is converted to an Arcade FeatureSetCollection type.

member.param: The profile variables for the script as a collection of key/value pairs.
member.returns: A task object representing the asynchronous evaluation of an Arcade script. The value of the task result is an object.

member.summary: Returns a collection of attributes used in the script for a given profile variable.
member.remarks: To fully support the wildcard expansion in the expects statement, calculation of used attributes on a profile variable requires an object that can supply a full set of attribute names.

A from which we can obtain a list of all attribute names for the profile variable.
member.returns: A task object representing the asynchronous retrieval of the attributes looked up on a profile variable in the script. The value of the task result is a collection of objects.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Arcade.ArcadeEvaluator.IsStable
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the script is considered stable.

remarks.para: A script is considered stable if the script will return the same result given the same inputs. There are certain functions listed below that will return different values on each evaluation. Use of these functions means the script is not considered stable.
Arcade functions that stop a script being considered stable:

member.value: true if the script is considered stable; otherwise, false.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Arcade.ArcadeEvaluator.Profile
member.summary: Gets the Arcade profile under which the script should evaluate.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Arcade.ArcadeProfile
member.summary: The execution context a script will run under.

remarks.para: The Arcade profile specifies the context under which a script should be executed. This allows the script writer to program against a set of known profile variables and functionality restrictions that are appropriate for their use case. This includes the set of expected return types.
It is erroneous to try to use restricted functionality for instance the use of FeatureSets while working in the labeling profile. Often the restrictions are applied due to performance considerations.

This API does not check that the required profile variables are present for evaluation within a certain profile, nor does it check the return type is the expected type. The method converts the type of the evaluation result using Arcade casting rules.

member.summary: Provides an interface for the ArcGIS Maps SDK for .NET configuration.

member.summary: Provides methods for configuring the ArcGIS Maps SDK for .NET.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISConfigurationExtensions.UseApiKey(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.IArcGISConfiguration,System.String)
member.summary: Adds a default API key that allows your app to access ArcGIS location services and private portal items.

remarks.para: An API key is a unique long-lived access token that is used to authenticate and monitor requests to ArcGIS location services and private portal items. You can create and manage an API key using your portal when you sign in with an ArcGIS Location Platform account or an ArcGIS Online account with administrator access or a custom role that has the `Generate API keys` privilege. To learn how to create and manage API keys, go to the Create an API Key tutorial. You must ensure that your API key has the correct privileges to access secure resources.
If the user is authenticated with the ArcGIS.com Portal, an API key is not needed to access ArcGIS Online location services. See .

member.param: ArcGIS Configuration
A string containing the API key.
member.returns: A reference to this instance after the operation has completed.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISConfigurationExtensions.UseLicense(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.IArcGISConfiguration,System.String)
member.summary: Adds a license using an Esri license string.

remarks.para: A license key can also be obtained from an authenticated ArcGIS Portal instance using , and then set using .

member.param: ArcGIS Configuration
The Lite, Basic, Standard, or Advanced Esri license string.
member.returns: A reference to this instance after the operation has completed.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISRuntimeEnvironment.TempPath
member.summary: Gets or sets the temporary data path for the runtime deployment used by this application.
member.value: A representing the path to a folder on disk.

remarks.para: Any data created by the runtime deployment which is temporary and does not need to be persisted between application sessions will be written to the folder specified by this property for the lifetime of your application. To override the default, the property must be set each time your application starts but before the method is called. Once the runtime deployment is initialized, any attempt to set this property will result in an . To ensure the files in the folder specified by this property will be automatically deleted you should call the ShutdownAsync method on the LocalServer. However, if any problems are encountered during execution some files may be left in this folder. These are not essential to future usage of the runtime deployment and can safely be deleted during the shutdown phase of your application.

The default value for this property is: "C:\Users\{UserName}\AppData\Local\Temp". The default location will be shared by any other runtime deployment on the local machine which also uses the default location and should therefore be set if you require that the temporary data be differentiated from that created by another runtime deployment.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISRuntimeEnvironment.ServiceCurveGeometryMode
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether curve geometries are fetched from and sent to services that support them.

remarks.para:
By default, this API does not fetch curves in geometries or act as a true curve client, which means:
Densified versions of any curve geometries are fetched from services, resulting in client-side geometries where is always false.
Curve geometries stored in services where is true cannot be updated.

If your application preserves curve segments throughout all workflows, set this property to , which means:
Curve geometries are fetched from services, resulting in client-side geometries where is true.
Your app will inform the services that it is a true curve client, and therefore will be able to update curve geometries stored in services where is true.

Ensure that you change this property before any calls to services are made, as it cannot be changed after the first request has begun. Note that some geometry operations do not support curve geometries; see for details of curve support. does not support curve geometries. It is your responsibility to ensure data integrity by correctly preserving any curve segments throughout your application when using .
See the ArcGIS REST API doc for details of curve support in services.

For services that don't support curves, this value has no effect. It also has no effect on local data sources that may contain curve geometries, such as mobile geodatabases or mobile map packages created using ArcGIS Pro.

member.value: Determines if curve geometries are fetched from and sent to services that support them.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISRuntimeEnvironment.EnableTimestampOffsetSupport
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether TimestampOffset fields are supported, which necessarily changes the behavior of Date fields.

remarks.para: ArcGIS Maps SDK historically allowed using either DateTime or DateTimeOffset values for fields of type "Date". To support TimestampOffset fields and avoid ambiguity, the behavior of Date fields has changed to only use DateTime values. This new behavior is now enabled by default, so TimestampOffset fields are supported by default. For more information, see https://links.esri.com/DotNetDateTime.
To disable support for TimestampOffset fields and revert to the previous behavior, set this property to false when initializing the app.

When is false:
Values of Date attributes are returned as DateTimeOffset (UTC).
Values of TimestampOffset attributes are also returned as DateTimeOffset.
Either DateTime or DateTimeOffset values can be used when editing Date fields.
Editing data with TimestampOffset fields is not supported. A will be thrown.
Creating TimestampOffset fields, domains, renderers, or Arcade expressions is not supported.

When is true (default):
Values of Date attributes are returned as DateTime (UTC). Using a DateTimeOffset for a Date field will cause a "Geodatabase data type mismatch" exception.
Values of TimestampOffset attributes are returned as DateTimeOffset.
Only DateTime values can be used to edit Date attributes. They are converted to UTC.
DateTimeOffset values can be used to edit TimestampOffset fields.
Creating TimestampOffset fields, domains, renderers, and Arcade expressions is supported.

member.name: E:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISRuntimeEnvironment.ArcadeConsoleMessage
member.summary: Occurs when a new Arcade console function message is sent.
member.remarks: This is called when an Arcade Console Function is part of an Arcade script expression. The Arcade Console function can be used to send information like variable values at different places within an Arcade script. Use this callback to view messages from an Arcade script evaluation.

member.summary: The best quality antialiasing. This option takes the longest to render.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ArcGISFeatureServiceInfo.AllowTrueCurvesUpdates
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the service allows curve geometries to be updated.
member.remarks: This property can only be true if is true. If this property is true and is false, features with curve geometries can be updated by any client. This includes cases where the client provides a densified geometry to update the curve geometry. By default, this API is not a true curve-enabled client. Use to change this behavior.
member.value: true if the service allows curve geometries to be updated, otherwise false.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ArcGISFeatureServiceInfo.OnlyAllowTrueCurveUpdatesByTrueCurveClients
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the service only allows curve geometries to be updated by client code that identifies itself as a true curve-enabled client, indicating that client editing workflows account for curve segments correctly.
member.remarks: This property can only be true if is true. By default, this API is not a true curve-enabled client. Use to change this behavior.
member.value: true if the service only allows curve geometries to be updated by client code that identifies itself as a true curve capable client.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ArcGISFeatureServiceInfo.SupportsTrueCurve
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the service supports curve segments in feature geometries.
member.remarks: Some services support storing feature geometries with curve segments. By default, these services return densified versions of the feature geometries instead. Use to change this behavior.
member.value: true if the service supports curve segments in feature geometries, otherwise false.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ArcGISFeatureServiceInfo.CanUseServiceGeodatabaseApplyEdits
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether you can use ServiceGeodatabase.ApplyEditsAsync() to commit changes back to the service, false otherwise.

remarks.para: This value is true if the support for global IDs in all tables in the geodatabase is enabled, or support for global IDs in all tables in the geodatabase is disabled, false otherwise.

ServiceGeodatabase.ApplyEditsAsync() applies edits to all affected tables as a single transaction. This is the recommended approach for applying edits. However, if the service has been setup with mixed support for global IDs, then you must apply the edits individually to each affected table using ServiceFeatureTable.ApplyEditsAsync()
If ServiceGeodatabase.ApplyEditsAsync() is used when this property is set to false, it can fail with

member.value: true if you can use ServiceGeodatabase.ApplyEditsAsync() to commit changes back to the service, false otherwise.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ArcGISMapServiceInfo
member.summary: The metadata for an ArcGIS map service.

remarks.para: An ArcGIS map service is a data service that provides access to map images that can be rendered dynamically or pre-rendered and cached in a tile grid as static images. You can display dynamically rendered images using and pre-rendered static images using .

provides basic information about the map, including the layers that it contains, whether or not the map is cached, its spatial reference, initial and full extents, map units, and copyright text. It also provides some metadata associated with the service, such as the service description, its author, and keywords. If the map service has a tile cache, additional information about the tiling scheme is included, such as the levels of detail, tiling scheme origin, and tile dimensions.
You can obtain the from a loaded , , or .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ArcGISMapServiceInfo.ExportTileCacheCompactV2Allowed
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether exporting tiles in compact version 2 format (.tpkx) is supported.

remarks.para: The export tile cache format compact version 2 (.tpkx) now supersedes the compact format (.tpk) across ArcGIS software. For more information, see .

If this property is true, the map service supports exporting tiles as .tpkx format. If this property is false, the service does not support exporting .tpkx format and you should check to confirm whether it supports exporting the earlier .tpk format.

, 10.9 or greater, supports exporting tiles as .tpkx. Compact V2 tile caches can also be exported from export-enabled Esri image basemaps and elevation services hosted on ArcGIS Online. For more details on the specification of a .tpkx file format, see .

member.value: true if exporting tiles in compact version 2 format (.tpkx) is supported, false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ArcGISMapServiceInfo.ExportTilesAllowed
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether exporting tiles in compact format (.tpk) is supported.

remarks.para: This property indicates whether the map service supports exporting tiles as compact (.tpk) format. Exporting tiles allows you to use them as a basemap, operational layer, or elevation source, without network connectivity. See for information on creating a layer from a local tile cache.
To export tiles you can use either the or the .
There are two formats for exporting a tile cache:

list.item: Compact (.tpk) is the legacy format for a tile cache. All services that support exporting tiles, support the .tpk format. See .
Compact V2 (.tpkx) is the latest format used for exporting tiles. Check to confirm whether the service supports this format.

member.value: true if exporting tiles in compact format (.tpk) is supported, false otherwise.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ArcGISMapServiceSublayerInfo
member.summary: The metadata of an ArcGIS map service sublayer.

remarks.para: provides basic information about an individual map image sublayer (or map service sublayer), such as the description, geometry type, drawing information, fields, extent, capabilities, and layer type. You can obtain a collection of map image sublayers from and obtain the using the .

The specifies if the map image sublayer is a feature layer, table, raster layer, or group layer. If the sublayer type is , it will have its own collection of sublayers that you can access using . If one of these sublayers is a group layer it will also contain a collection of sublayers, and so on.

member.summary: Gets the layer's display field expression, or null if the default display field is used.
member.remarks: The display field expression contains a custom un-evaluated Arcade expression, a title, an empty name, and an return type.
member.value: The layer's display field expression, or null if the default display field is used.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ArcGISMapServiceSublayerInfo.DefinitionExpression
member.summary: Gets the sublayer's definition expression.
member.value: Definition expression, if specified. Otherwise, an empty string.
member.remarks: The definition expression string uses the SQL-92 WHERE clause syntax (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SQL-92). Be sure to escape special characters in the expression string as required for your platform. The DATE keyword expects the date format yyyy-mm-dd and the TIMESTAMP keyword expects the time stamp format yyyy-mm-dd hh:mm:ss. For more information, see the ArcGIS Blog article Querying Feature Services Date-Time Queries.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ServiceDocumentInfo
member.summary: Contains detailed metadata information about an ArcGIS Server MapServer when a map service is published. Some of the document information for the service include: title, author, keywords, whether to use anti-aliasing on graphics, etc..

remarks.para: Use either the ArcGISMapServiceInfo.ServiceDocumentInfo or FeatureServiceInfo.ServiceDocumentInfo Properties to obtain a ServiceDocumentInfo object.

TIP: You can verify that various Properties of the ServiceDocumentInfo by comparing it to what is displayed in the ArcGIS Server REST Services Directory page by copying the Url of the MapServer into the address bar of an Internet web browser and scrolling down to the Document Info section. See the following screen shot:

img.alt: Getting detailed metadata for ArcGIS Server MapServer web services.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.EditFieldsInfo
member.summary: The fields that record who adds or edits the features and when the edits are made.

remarks.para: These editing fields are defined when the data owner enables editor tracking on the feature service. For more information, see Editor tracking for feature services.
You can obtain the from . This information is recorded if is true.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.EditFieldsInfo.Realm
member.summary: Gets a string that uniquely identifies an editor and how they edited the data.
member.remarks: Realms can be used to differentiate edits made through a feature service as opposed to those made directly to the data in the geodatabase. If a realm string is present, it is appended to the username using the "@" character. For example, a realm string of "denver" is appended to the user "John" to give a user name of "John@denver". For more information, see Configure a realm for a feature service.
member.value: The realm is a string that uniquely identifies an editor and how they edited the data.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.EditorTrackingInfo
member.summary: Editor tracking information indicates who edited features of an ArcGIS feature service.

remarks.para: Editor tracking allows you to record who made edits and when the edits were applied. This can help you enforce accountability and quality control of the features you add to the geodatabase. If is true, this editing information is recorded against each edited feature using the fields specified in the . For more information, see Editor tracking for feature services.
You can obtain from the .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.EditorTrackingInfo.EditorTrackingEnabled
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether editor tracking is enabled.

remarks.para: If true, the feature service will record who added or edited the data and when the edit is made. This information is stored in fields that the data owner defined when they enabled editor tracking on the feature service. You can obtain these edit field names from . For more information, see Editor tracking for feature services.

member.value: true if editor tracking is enabled, false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.EditorTrackingInfo.OwnershipBasedAccessControlEnabled
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether ownership-based access control is enabled.

remarks.para: The data owner can restrict people from accessing features they do not own using Ownership-based access control. For more information, see Ownership-based access control for feature services.

member.value: true if ownership-based access control is enabled, false otherwise.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.FeatureServiceCapabilities
member.summary: The capabilities of an ArcGIS feature service.

remarks.para: ArcGIS feature service capabilities, such as Query, Create, Update, and Sync, are stated within the "Capabilities" tag of its ArcGIS REST Services Directory. Each of its capabilities are reflected in , , , and so on.

You can obtain an ArcGIS feature service's from its metadata using the . This is available from two sources:
a loaded .
a loaded that is downloaded from a feature service using either the or the .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.FeatureServiceSessionType
member.summary: An enumeration of the session types that control how multiple users access branch versioned data.

remarks.para: Session types — transient and persistent — let you control how multiple viewers and editors access branch versioned feature services. By default, a web map or web scene with branch versioned feature services (a version) loads as a type; you can change this type to at the map or scene level when you load the map or scene.

When you switch the version being used via , a read operation is started on the new version before releasing the previous version. If acquiring a shared lock on the new version fails (for example, if another user is editing the version), an error is raised. This behavior allows you to keep the user connected to the original version when the version they want is not yet available.

For descriptions of additional ways to manage multiple viewers and editors in branch versioned data, such as using multiple tiers of editors, see the ArcGIS Pro discussion on branch version scenarios.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.FeatureServiceSessionType.Transient
member.summary: A momentary session is used during queries and edit operations.
member.remarks: This is the default session type. The server creates an exclusive lock upon an editor's ServiceGeodatabase.ApplyEditsAsync(), ServiceGeodatabase.ApplyEditsAsync(CancellationToken) operation and holds the lock only for the duration that it takes that operation to occur. An exclusive lock prevents readers and editors from accessing (connecting to and viewing) the rows affected by the edit in the version during this time, but they can access (connect to, view, query, and edit) other rows in the version. Use the transient type when concurrency is more important than data consistency between queries or between concurrent users.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.FeatureServiceSessionType.Persistent
member.summary: A session is held open as long as tables are actively reading or writing.

remarks.para: When a user starts a read operation, a shared lock is placed on the version at the beginning of the read operation. A shared lock allows multiple users using read operations to access the service feature table at the same time.The shared lock is kept until or methods are called. If the is in , you can call to end a read session.

When a user starts an edit operation in the list below, an exclusive lock is placed on the affected at the beginning of the edit operation. An exclusive lock prevents all other users from connecting to the version. They cannot view, query, or edit that version.
The lock is held until one of the following methods is called:

If one or more read operations were already being performed on the when an edit operation began (if a shared lock was already in place), then the shared lock becomes an exclusive lock. When all edit operations are applied successfully, the exclusive lock returns to being a shared lock.
A call to will release an exclusive lock.
Note that if the version requested is the default version, no exclusive lock will be acquired.
Edit operations that trigger the locks described above:

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.LabelingInfo
member.summary: Defines the text, appearance and position of labels for features for a given scale range.

remarks.para: A describes a class of labels.
Each specifies:

list.item: Which features are covered by the definition, by applying the SQL constraint.
What text should be displayed, based on the individual feature's attributes and the formula.
where the text should appear, with respect to its feature, using the .

remarks.para: describes a very limited subset of the properties.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.LabelingInfo.LabelExpression
member.summary: Gets the simple expression describing how to build the text label for a feature, often using attributes of the feature.

remarks.para: The default is an empty expression and no labels will be produced.
The simple expression format is described in the document: Labeling objects. An example being: "Addr: " CONCAT [Name] CONCAT " Street" (note that quotes are needed around text literals).

member.value: The expression of the label.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.LabelingInfo.LabelPlacement
member.summary: Gets the preferred position of the text label, with respect to its feature geometry.
member.remarks:
The default depends on the type of feature geometry:
for point features.
for polygon features.
for line features.
member.value: The placement of the label.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.LabelingInfo.MaxScale
member.summary: Gets the largest viewing scale at which labels should be visible. If set, labels will disappear when the view is zoomed in beyond the maximum scale.

remarks.para: For example, if the viewing scale is 1:12000 and the is 1:10000, then the labels will be visible (because 1/12000 <= 1/10000).
A of 0, which is the default, indicates no upper limit on the viewing scale.

member.value: The maximum scale of the label.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.LabelingInfo.MinScale
member.summary: Gets the smallest viewing scale at which labels should be visible. If set, labels will disappear when the view is zoomed out beyond the minimum scale.

remarks.para: For example, if the viewing scale is 1:12000 and the is 1:20000, then the labels will be visible (because 1/12000 >= 1/20000).
A of 0, which is the default, indicates no lower limit on the viewing scale.

member.value: The maximum scale of the label.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.LabelingInfo.UseCodedValues
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether label should use coded values.
member.value: A value indicating whether label should use coded values.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.LabelingInfo.Where
member.summary: Gets the where clause used to filter which features will be labeled.

remarks.para: These are example SQL expressions that can refer to the attributes of the feature:

list.item: "" (no limitations)
NAME LIKE 'A%' (Only label features with NAME attribute values starting with 'A')
(LENGTH > CAPACITY) and (TYPE = 1) (Only label features whose attributes satisfy the SQL condition)

remarks.para: The default value is "".

member.value: The where clause used to filter which features will be labeled.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.MapServiceCapabilities
member.summary: Describes the capabilities of an ArcGIS map service, including whether it supports exporting map images, data and query operations.

remarks.para:
You can access the content of a map from an ArcGIS map service using one of its three main capabilities:
Data - find, query, and related records.
Map - export map images and list all layers and tables.
Query - identify and access attachment data.
These capabilities are reflected in , , and . For more information, see the Map Service help documentation.
You can obtain an ArcGIS map service's using . This is available from a number of loaded resources, such as , , or .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipCardinality
member.summary: An enumeration of the different cardinalities of feature table relationships.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipCardinality.ManyToMany
member.summary: Features in both tables may be related to any number of features in the other table.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipCardinality.OneToMany
member.summary: An origin feature can be related to any number of features in the destination table.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipCardinality.OneToOne
member.summary: An origin feature can be related to at most one feature in the destination table.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipInfo
member.summary: Defines information about the relationship between tables.
member.remarks: A relationship consists of two and only two tables, each having one of these roles in the relationship: Origin table, sometimes referred to as the source table or original table. Destination table. One table must have one of these roles and the other table must have the other role. A table can participate in more than one relationship. Further, a table may be an origin table in one relationship and also a destination table in another relationship. The relationship's cardinality describes whether features in a table are related to only one feature in the other table, or whether a feature may be related to multiple features. In a simple relationship, the features in the participating tables can exist without the other. For example, a transformer and an electric pole may be related but one's existence does not depend on the other. Deleting the origin features will simply disassociate the destination features from the origin features. In a composite relationship, the destination features cannot exist without the origin features. When the origin feature is deleted, the related destination feature is deleted as well. This is called a cascade delete. This dependency also means that each destination feature must be linked to a counterpart origin feature.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipInfo.Cardinality
member.summary: Gets the cardinality of this relationship.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipInfo.Id
member.summary: Gets the ID of this relationship.
member.remarks: The relationship ID is shared between the origin and destination tables.
member.value: The ID of this relationship.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipInfo.IsComposite
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether this is a composite relationship.
member.remarks: In a composite relationship, a feature in the destination table must always be associated with an origin feature. Orphan features in the destination tables are not permitted. Therefore, destination table features will be deleted when their associated origin table features are deleted (this is called a cascade delete).
member.value: true if this is a composite relationship; otherwise, false if it is a simple relationship.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipInfo.KeyField
member.summary: Gets the key field, the field that links the origin and destination tables of this relationship.
member.value: The key field, the field that links the origin and destination tables of this relationship.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipInfo.KeyFieldInRelationshipTable
member.summary: Gets the key field in the relationship table.
member.remarks: Used in many-to-many and attributed relationships. Values in this field will match values stored in the of features participating in the relationship.
member.value: The key field in the relationship table.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipInfo.Name
member.summary: Gets the name of the relationship.
member.value: The name of the relationship.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipInfo.RelatedTableId
member.summary: Gets the ID of the related table.
member.value: The ID of the related table.

member.summary: Gets the ID of the relationship table. The default value is -1, indicating that no table exists.
member.remarks: The relationship table is used in many-to-many and attributed relationships.
member.value: The ID of the relationship table.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipInfo.Role
member.summary: Gets the role that this table has in this relationship.
member.value: The role that this table has in this relationship.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ServiceCurveGeometryMode
member.summary: Specifies how curve geometries are fetched from and sent to services that support curves.
member.remarks: By default, curve geometries are not fetched from services that support curves, meaning that densified versions of any curve geometries are fetched from the service. By default, this API is it not a true curve-enabled client when sending updates to services that support curves. Use to change this default behavior. For details of curve support in services, see ArcGIS REST API documentation.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ServiceCurveGeometryMode.DensifyCurves
member.summary: Densified versions of geometries with curves are fetched. This is the default.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.ServiceCurveGeometryMode.TrueCurveClient
member.summary: Curve geometries are fetched, and this API acts as a true curve client when editing.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.SyncCapabilities.SupportsUtilityNetworkSystem
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the Utility Network System data is available.
member.remarks: Feature layers and tables are always included in the generation and synchronization of the geodatabase. This property indicates whether Utility Network System data is also available from the service, for generation of an offline geodatabase and for resynchronization.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.SyncCapabilities.SupportsUtilityNetworkTopology
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether utility network topology data is available.

remarks.para: When syncing Utility Network data, feature layers and tables are always included in the generation and synchronization of the geodatabase. This property indicates whether utility network topology data is also available from the service, for generation of an offline geodatabase and for resynchronization.

member.value: Indicates if utility network topology data is available.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.SyncCapabilities.SupportsUtilityNetworkTopologySync
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the available utility network topology data support synchronization in any direction, false otherwise.

remarks.para: If the value is true, utility network topology data can be taken offline with . The downloaded geodatabase supports editing and synchronization in any direction - , , .

If the value is false, utility network topology data can only be taken offline with if is true. The downloaded geodatabase cannot be edited directly but it can incorporate edits made on the service using .

If is false, the utility network topology data can only be taken offline with . In this situation, the geodatabase is read-only and does not support synchronization. To integrate updates from the service into the geodatabase, the current geodatabase needs to be replaced by downloading it again.

member.value: true if the available utility network topology data support synchronization in any direction, false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.SyncCapabilities.SupportsUtilityNetworkTopologySyncDownloadOnly
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the available utility network topology data support download-only synchronization, false otherwise.

remarks.para: If the value is true, utility network topology data can be taken offline with . The downloaded geodatabase cannot be edited directly. If you want to incorporate edits made on the service, you can synchronize the geodatabase using .
If the value is false, utility network topology data can only be taken offline with if is true. The downloaded geodatabase supports editing and synchronization in any direction - , , .

When is false, utility network topology data can only be taken offline with . In this situation, the geodatabase is read-only and does not support synchronization. To integrate updates from the service into the geodatabase, the current geodatabase needs to be replaced by downloading it again.

member.value: true if the available utility network topology data support download-only synchronization, false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.SyncCapabilities.SupportsContingentValues
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether Contingent Value data is available.
member.remarks: Feature layers and tables are always included in the generation and synchronization of the geodatabase. This property indicates whether Contingent Value data is also available from the service, for generation of an offline geodatabase and for resynchronization.
member.value: true if Contingent Value data is available.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.SyncCapabilities.SupportsAnnotations
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether Annotation data is available for downloading.
member.remarks: Feature layers and tables are always included in the generation and synchronization of the geodatabase. This property indicates whether Annotation data is also available from the service, for generation of, or downloading to, an offline geodatabase. Once this data is taken offline to a local geodatabase, any inserts, edits or deletions will NOT be applied to the server data when the geodatabase is re-synchronized. If features linked to the annotation have been changed, the server WILL apply the feature-linked-annotation updates. Another download from the server will replace any offline inserts, edits or deletions of Annotation features.
member.value: true if Annotation data is available; otherwise, false.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.SyncCapabilities.SupportsDimensions
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether Dimension data is available.
member.remarks: Feature layers and tables are always included in the generation and synchronization of the geodatabase. This property indicates whether Dimension data is also available from the service, for generation of an offline geodatabase and for resynchronization.
member.value: true if Dimension data is available; otherwise, false.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ArcGISFeature
member.summary: Represents an ArcGIS specific feature stored in an .

remarks.para: ArcGIS features are stored in ArcGIS specific data sources such as a or . Since is a subclass of , it contains a geometry that defines its location and shape, and a set of attributes that describe the real-world entity it represents.

In addition, objects can also contain:
Feature attachments such as images or documents, that can be edited, updated or deleted.
Feature subtypes that define different default values or domains for fields.
The ability to relate this feature to another .
Geometry that has m-values, which are typically used for linear referencing.
If you create a from the URL of a feature service, features in the table are initially in one of the following states:

list.item: A minimum set of attributes are available in the table, and m-values are omitted. This is an optimization for faster rendering.
All attributes defined by the service are available in the table, including m-values, which might be NaN.

remarks.para: The initial state of features is determined by the feature request mode of the table. For important details, see .

By default, a query on objects in a minimum state returns them in the same state. To access all attributes (or the m-values) of features returned by a query, you must load the features. You can do one of the following:

list.item: Call on each feature.
Call and pass a collection of features to be loaded.
Call with the constant , which returns all features in a fully loaded state.

remarks.para: For more information, see the class description for .

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ArcGISFeature.LoadAsync
member.summary: Initiates loading of feature.
member.remarks: An ArcGISFeature is loadable. Depending on a ServiceFeatureTable's feature request mode, queries on the table can return ArcGISFeature objects which have the minimum set of attributes required for rendering and labeling (and geometries do not include m-values). See , , and . To access all attributes in the returned ArcGISFeatures, call this method on each feature. For alternative ways to load all attributes in the returned features, see or .
member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous load operation.

member.summary: Retry loading feature.

member.remarks: Sets the load status to not loaded and calls load if the task is in the failed to load state. Does nothing if the task is in any other state.

member.summary: Sets up a relationship between this feature and the provided feature.
member.remarks: If this relationship is keyed on an Object ID or Global ID, and the origin feature in this call has been created locally, you must first call the ServiceGeodatabase.ApplyEditsAsync() method on the . Next, you must call to get the new Object ID and Global ID that are generated by the service. Otherwise, the destination feature will refer to an outdated ID rather than the one defined for the relationship.
member.param: The feature to which this feature should be related.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ArcGISFeatureTable
member.summary: A table of features that typically represent real-world objects created from an ArcGIS feature service or an ArcGIS geodatabase.

remarks.para: This is the base class for the ArcGIS specific feature tables and . You can create instances of these subclasses using their constructors.

If the is true, you can display the features in a map or scene. To do this create a from the feature table and add it to the map or scene's collection of operational layers (via GeoModel.OperationalLayers). The can also contain features that do not have a geometry ( is false).
provides methods for querying related features (for example, ), and methods to create new features based on a or a .
This table may represent a Subtype Group Table when is not empty. Use to build this collection for subtype-specific feature data access.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ArcGISFeatureTable.UnknownJson
member.summary: Gets unknown data from the source JSON.
member.remarks: Unknown JSON is a dictionary of values not defined in the ArcGIS specification used to create this object but found in the source JSON. If the object is written back to JSON, any unknown JSON data is not persisted. The ArcGIS specification may be for a web map, web scene, REST API, and so on.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ArcGISFeatureTable.UnsupportedJson
member.summary: Gets unsupported data from the source JSON.
member.remarks: Unsupported JSON is a dictionary of values defined in the ArcGIS specification used to create this object but not currently used in this API. If the object is written back to JSON, any unsupported JSON data is persisted. The ArcGIS specification may be from a web map, web scene, REST API, and so on.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ArcGISFeatureTable.SubtypeSubtables
member.summary: Gets the mutable collection of objects that provide access to features with the same subtype.

remarks.para: The collection is empty unless loaded from a web map or mobile map that contains a SubtypeGroupTable. Alternatively, if a is set, the collection will be populated when the table is loaded. Once loaded, you can use the collection to create and add tables to this collection. The must have a that is not already being used by another .
Add objects to for subtype-specific views and more fine-grained control over access and editing experience of features with the same subtype.
Use this property to refine or rearrange subtype-specific views.

member.value: The mutable collection of objects that provide access to features with the same subtype.

member.summary: Retrieves all the features that were added, updated or deleted since the last sync.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ArcGISFeatureTable.ValidateRelationshipConstraintsAsync(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ArcGISFeature)
member.summary: Asynchronously validates constraints for a given feature participating in a relationship.

remarks.para: The following are considered violations:
In a composite relationship, adding an orphan feature to the destination table without relating it to an origin feature.
Cardinality Violations:

list.item: In 1:1 relationships, if an origin feature is already related to a destination feature, relating another feature to either of them.
In 1:n relationships, relating a destination feature to more than one origin feature.

remarks.para: To recover from an orphaned destination feature violation, simply relate it to a valid origin feature. You can usually recover from a cardinality violation by unrelating the appropriate features. Also, see .

Edit operations do not error when there are constraint violations. This allows you to recover from violations in a back office operation after applying edits or syncing, if you choose to do so. See ArcGIS Desktop Validate Features process here.

Note: This method makes network calls to query for the related features if they are not present locally. If you have just related the feature you are validating, you need to first call to reflect the modified attribute value in the table. If this is not done, validation results may be incorrect.

member.param: The feature.
member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous validation of relationship constraints operation. The value of the task result contains a object.

For mobile geodatabases, use .

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ArcGISFeatureTable.GetContingentValues(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ArcGISFeature,System.String)
member.summary: Returns possible values for the specified field, in the context of defined contingencies.
member.remarks: This method iterates over all the field groups that the input field participates in. For each, it looks at the input feature's field values for the other fields participating in that field group. Contingencies that are satisfied by the other fields are identified, and their values for the input field are aggregated. These are returned in a , which contains a dictionary of field group names to a list of objects accessed via , specifying possible values for the input field that will satisfy a contingency in that field group. If the feature participates in multiple field groups and there are values that will satisfy all groups, they will be returned by .
member.param: The feature.
The name of the field for which to get possible values.
member.returns: A .

member.summary: Validates contingency constraints for the given feature.
member.param: The feature to validate.
member.returns: A read-only list of objects. An empty list indicates that all contingencies associated with the feature are valid.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.Attachment
member.summary: A file (such as an image or document) attached to a feature in a or a .

remarks.para: Attachments allow you to associate files containing additional information with specific, relevant features. For example, a building inspector could attach a PDF of a permit to a building feature. Attachments are not stored as attributes, rather the attachment data is linked with the feature inside its or a .

If a feature table supports attachments (), each feature in the table may have zero or more attachments. Get a feature's collection of attachments by calling . You can examine an attachment's name, type, and file size, and download the file using . Once downloaded, you can work with the data in your app (display it to the user, for example).

If a feature allows editing of its attachments ( value is true), you can add to the feature's collection of attachments using . The data must be in one of the supported attachment formats. You can also update or delete attachments from the , as required.

Your app's available memory and storage space may impact the size of attachments you can add to a feature, or retrieve from the feature's collection. Apps that need to work with large attachments should be tested using representative physical devices and sample attachments.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.CodedValue
member.summary: Represents a coded value found in a .

remarks.para: The coded value consists of a code and a name. For example, to represent road surfaces, a code of 1, 2, and 3 could have corresponding name values of gravel, asphalt, and concrete. The code is stored in the geodatabase and the corresponding user-friendly name is presented to the user for viewing or editing.
A consists of a collection of objects.

member.summary: An object that describes a to be created.

remarks.para: Each consists of a value and a name. For example, for roads data the values 1, 2, and 3 might correspond to three types of road surface such as gravel, asphalt, and concrete. Coded value domains are typically created and assigned to your datasets using ArcGIS Pro, ArcGIS Online, or ArcGIS Enterprise.
If your mobile geodatabase was created in ArcGIS Pro or using , you can create a . First, you must create a with a collection of , by using . Next, you create the by calling with the .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.CodedValueDomain
member.summary: Specifies an explicit set of valid values for a .

remarks.para: A coded value domain consists of a collection of coded values that are saved in the dataset. When a user is editing data, you can present the list of coded values for the fields (attributes) they edit, or validate their input values against the coded value domain before changes are saved. This ensures that the user maintains data integrity by only applying values that are in the .

You can create a coded value domain in a mobile geodatabase, if the mobile geodatabase was created in ArcGIS Pro or using . To create a , you can create a that has a collection of , by using . Next, you create the by calling with the .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.Feature
member.summary: A representation of a real-world geographic entity.

remarks.para: Features are composed of both a geometry (point, polyline, or polygon) and attributes. The geometry represents the location and shape of the real-world entity; the attributes (key-value pairs) represent the fields and values that describe the entity. Examples of features include roads, fire hydrants, and property boundaries. Applications can access features from a feature layer or a feature collection to visualize the feature's geographic and attribute information, execute spatial queries, perform analyses, or make edits to the feature's data directly. Feature attribute values can be changed, but attribute definitions cannot be added, deleted, or modified.

Features are typically persisted in a data source (such as a feature service, geodatabase, shapefile, GeoJSON file, or GeoPackage) and have a common attribute schema. Features can also be stored directly in a feature collection in a map or scene. A feature collection groups logically related tables of features that may have different schema, geometry types, and symbology. See for more information.
is the base class for . ArcGIS features are stored in ArcGIS specific data sources such as and .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.FeatureCollection
member.summary: A feature collection represents a lightweight collection of features that can be saved in a map or portal item.

remarks.para: A feature collection is typically used to hold features with mixed geometry or unstructured data. The features are stored in feature collection tables, where all the features that belong to a table share the same attribute schema (fields), geometry type, and spatial reference. The feature collection groups these different feature tables together so that you can display and manage them in a single layer.

There are a number of ways to create a feature collection:
You can build a feature collection in ArcGIS Online by creating a sketch layer and saving it as part of the map. The sketch layer may contain points, lines, polygons, and associated text to describe things in the map. Because they have different schema and geometry types, these features are stored in several distinct feature collection tables.
You can import features into ArcGIS Online from files, such as CSV or shapefiles, and save them in a separate (of type ).
You can create a programmatically by constructing an empty and adding features to the table.

If you need to share the feature collection between several maps or scenes, it is best to store it as a separate portal item. If you need to make frequent (near real-time) edits to features in a collection, consider storing these in a feature service instead, because the feature collection is not refreshed until the map or portal item is reloaded. If features are used by a single map or are not subject to frequent updates, it might be best to store them directly in the map. Edits made to features stored in a map will be saved when the map is saved. Edits made to features stored in a portal item (and loaded into a map, for example) must be explicitly saved to the original portal item.
To render the features in a map or scene, construct a using the and add the layer to the collection of operational layers.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.FeatureCollection.SaveAsAsync(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Portal.ArcGISPortal,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Portal.PortalFolder,System.String,System.String,System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{System.String},Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.RuntimeImage)
member.summary: Save the FeatureCollection to a new PortalItem.
member.remarks:
Asynchronous method to save a FeatureCollection as a new portal item. The FeatureCollection does not need to be associated with an existing PortalItem object. The FeatureCollection must be loaded. The result is a reference to the new PortalItem which the FeatureCollection is now associated with.
Note that if the FeatureCollection was already associated with a PortalItem object (either because the FeatureCollection was created using a PortalItem or was previously saved), that object will be replaced with a new PortalItem, effectively duplicating the FeatureCollection on the portal.

The thumbnail of the map
member.returns: The task object representing the asynchronous save as operation. The value of the task result is a object.
member.exception: Fails if the portal isn't loaded and associated with a user

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.FeatureCollectionTable
member.summary: A feature collection table represents an individual feature table in a .

remarks.para: A consists of a feature definition (table schema) and a set of features that match that feature definition. You can obtain a from an existing collection or you can create a new programmatically in your app.

To create a new , you can use a constructor that defines a set of fields, the geometry type, and spatial reference, and then populate it by adding new features. Alternatively, you can create a new from an , such as the returned from .

To render the features in a map or scene, add the to a collection, construct a from the , and add it to the map or scene's collection of operational layers. To specify the symbology, apply a renderer to the property. If required, you can override this symbology on a per-feature basis using the .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.FeatureRequestMode
member.summary: Describes the feature request mode for a . It determines (1) whether features are cached locally (for quicker access by map and scene layers) and (2) whether queries are performed on the local cache or on the server.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.FeatureRequestMode.Undefined
member.summary: Indicates the feature table has not been loaded and the mode has yet to be set.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.FeatureRequestMode.OnInteractionCache
member.summary: The features are requested from the server as they are needed, in response to user or developer interaction with the layer (pan, zoom). Features are cached in the local table for the duration of the session. Queries are executed on the local cache or (if requested features are not resident in the cache) on the server. This mode is the default.
member.remarks: In this mode, features have a minimum set of attributes, and feature geometries have no m-values even if m-values are defined by the service. This is an optimization for faster rendering. To access all attributes or access geometries that contain m-values, the features you get from a query must be loaded. You can load them yourself or request that the query method return them already loaded (see ). For more information, see the class description for .

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.FeatureRequestMode.OnInteractionNoCache
member.summary: The features are always requested from the server and are never cached. This mode ensures that you are working against the latest data, but it has a high network bandwidth since it goes to the server for all interactions (pans, zooms, selects, or queries).
member.remarks: In this mode, features have a minimum set of attributes, and feature geometries have no m-values even if m-values are defined by the service. This is an optimization for faster rendering. To access all attributes or access geometries that contain m-values, the features you get from a query must be loaded. You can load them yourself or request that the query method return them already loaded (see ). For more information, see the class description for .

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.FeatureRequestMode.ManualCache
member.summary: The features are requested from the server by an explicit call to , which populates the local cache. Queries performed on the table will be executed locally.
member.remarks: In this mode, the features have all fields defined by the you pass to .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.FeatureSubtype
member.summary: Describes a subset of features in an that share the same integer attribute value.

remarks.para: Feature subtypes categorize features in a dataset based on the value of an integer attribute. A subtype can define different default values or domains for fields. For example, city streets in a feature table could be categorized into three feature subtypes: local streets, collector streets, and arterial streets. Feature subtypes can also be used with utility networks to represent asset groups.

If contains one or more , you can use to create a . You can then obtain a sublayer for each of the feature sub types from . Each allows you to configure different layer properties, such as opacity, renderer, and scale, for each . This is particularly useful for data, such as utility networks, in which many different network elements are grouped into a single subtype feature layer.

If you build an editing application, you can use to create a new feature with the specified feature subtype. The will ensure that a default set of attribute values are applied to the and the are utilized.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.FeatureTemplate
member.summary: Defines the default attribute values for the fields of a newly created and suggests a type of drawing tool to sketch the feature's geometry.

remarks.para: The owner of the ArcGIS feature service can define one or more feature templates when they publish the service. These are held within the "Templates" tag of the ArcGIS feature service's REST Services Directory. Each template has a name, description, suggested drawing tool, and a list of attributes with their initial values (called prototype). You can get the feature service's collection of feature templates from the . Alternatively, if you are using a , you can get its templates from . Use to create a feature with the feature template.

If you are building an editing application, you can present users with a list of feature templates. Upon choosing a template, the app can create a feature with the attributes populated with their default values and present geometry sketching tools to match the .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.FeatureType
member.summary: Defines the domains and feature templates that are used to create a new feature.

remarks.para: A feature type is used to ensure that appropriate attribute values are captured when a new feature is created. A can specify the domains for the attribute values and can provide a template that populates the feature with initial values. For example, a roads layer may contain different types of roads, such as main roads, city streets, and unpaved tracks. The owner could set up a feature type named 'main road' to ensure that new features are only populated with attribute values appropriate for a 'main road', and so on.

The owner of the ArcGIS Feature Service defines the feature types when they publish the service. They are held within the "Types" tag of the ArcGIS feature service's REST Services Directory. Each type has an ID, a name, a set of domains and a set of templates. You can obtain the collection of feature types by using . Use to create a feature with the feature type.

If you are building an editing application, you can present users with a list of feature types. Upon choosing a feature type, the app can populate the feature's attribute values with the and ensure that any attributes in the user interface correspond to the .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.Geodatabase
member.summary: Represents a mobile geodatabase containing geographic data and non-spatial tables.

remarks.para: is used to open and access the contents of a mobile geodatabase (.geodatabase file) that can contain geographic features, non-spatial tabular data, and data such as attachments, utility networks, field domain definitions, contingent values, and relationships between layers/tables.

Geodatabases can contain one or more geodatabase feature tables. You can create a layer from tables, as follows:
A from any table returned by that contains point, polyline, or polygon features. Check that is true.
An from any table returned by .
A from any table returned by .

You can generate a mobile geodatabase file from an ArcGIS feature service and downloaded it to a device using offline workflows offered by the and the . Apps can then operate offline to view and edit the features in these geodatabases. If the geodatabases are enabled for synchronization with the original feature service, edits can be synchronized when connectivity is restored.

Mobile geodatabase files can also be created with ArcGIS Pro. These geodatabases can be used offline but they do not support synchronization with the original feature service. See Build offline applications for more information.

For editing workflows that use a mobile geodatabase, you can use geodatabase transactions to manage a set of edits (transaction). If you start a transaction with , you can control when edits are committed (saved) as a single unit using . If you decide to discard the edits you can use . If the app unexpectedly disconnects from the geodatabase, any active transaction will be rolled back. If the app crashes, or the user closes the app without saving edits, the transaction will be rolled back and the edits discarded.

member.param: The path to the local geodatabase.
member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous open operation. The value of the task result is a object.

member.param: The path at which the mobile geodatabase is created.
member.returns: A that returns a in a loaded state.

member.returns: A representing the asynchronous operation. If there is no error, the table was deleted successfully.

member.summary: Gets the geometry that was used to generate the sync-enabled geodatabase.

remarks.para: If the geodatabase was created using the , the geometry matches the . If the geodatabase was created using the , the geometry reflects the . If the geodatabase was downloaded as part of a , this property will match the geometry used by the web map author when the offline map area was created. See Build offline applications for more information on taking data offline.
Edits made to a sync-enabled must intersect this geometry. If an edit is made outside this extent, or area of interest, it will be rejected.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.Geodatabase.MinServerGeneration
member.summary: Gets the minimum server generation number for the geodatabase.

remarks.para: Note that this property is only required when using advanced workflows to manually apply pre-generated changes and not when performing a sync directly against the online service. Server generation numbers indicate the state of a geodatabase (or individual layers) with respect to changes which have been synced with the online service. The number increases as new changes from the online feature service are synced to the local geodatabase. If the is , the value will indicate the server generation number for the entire geodatabase. If the is , the value will be the lowest server generation number for all of the layers in the geodatabase. This property will be -1 if the geodatabase does not support sync ().
