member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.Geodatabase.SyncId
member.summary: Gets the sync ID of the geodatabase.
member.remarks: Sync ID is available if is true. The sync ID is the same as the replica ID described in the REST documentation. It is used by the geodatabase service to uniquely identify the geodatabase when carrying out sync operations. It is also used to unregister the geodatabase using the .
member.value: The sync ID of this geodatabase. A value of Guid.Empty will be returned if this is not a sync enabled geodatabase.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.Geodatabase.IsInTransaction
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether a transaction is active on the geodatabase.

remarks.para: A transaction manages a series of geodatabase edits as a single unit of work. You can start a transaction by calling . All edits made after this call are part of the transaction.

A geodatabase transaction stays active until you end it by calling or . saves all edits in the transaction to the geodatabase and ends the transaction. discards all edits in the transaction and ends the transaction.

If the app unexpectedly disconnects from the database, any active transaction will be rolled back. If the app crashes, or the user closes the app without saving edits, the transaction will be rolled back and the edits discarded.

member.value: true if a transaction is active on the geodatabase, false otherwise.

member.summary: Returns a value indicating whether database has local edits.

remarks.para: If the geodatabase was created by ArcGIS Pro, this method returns false because there is no concept of uploading or applying edits. If you want to determine which tables have local edits, examine .

member.returns: A value where true means the database has local edits, false means no edits.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.Geodatabase.IsSyncEnabled
member.summary: Returns a value indicating sync is enabled for the database.

remarks.para: A sync-enabled geodatabase can be requested from an ArcGIS feature service using the or the . It can also be downloaded as part of a .
If true, the geodatabase data can be synchronized with its original ArcGIS feature service.

member.returns: A value where true means the database has sync enabled, false means no sync.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.Geodatabase.Close
member.summary: Closes the connection to the geodatabase.
member.remarks: You should stop using the geodatabase before you close it. For example, you should terminate geodatabase synchronization, remove feature layers from maps, and release the feature tables. Any attempt to access the geodatabase after it is closed will fail, resulting in an error. After closing a geodatabase, you can delete the geodatabase file from the device.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.Geodatabase.BeginTransaction
member.summary: Starts a transaction on the geodatabase.

remarks.para: This method starts a new transaction on the geodatabase. The transaction fails to begin if another transaction is already active. A geodatabase cannot be synchronized while a transaction is active.

Transactions can be started and stopped on any thread. Any edits that take place after a call to but before a call to either or will be considered part of that transaction, no matter what thread made the edit.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.Geodatabase.CommitTransaction
member.summary: Commits the edits in the transaction to the geodatabase.

remarks.para: Commits the edits in the current transaction to the geodatabase. This will also end the transaction. An exception will be thrown if a transaction has not been started on the geodatabase.

Transactions can be started and stopped on any thread. Any edits that take place after a call to but before a call to either or will be considered part of that transaction, no matter what thread made the edit.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.Geodatabase.RollbackTransaction
member.summary: Rollback the edits in the transaction from the geodatabase.

remarks.para: Discards the edits in the current transaction from the geodatabase. This will also end the transaction. An exception will be thrown if a transaction has not been started on the geodatabase.

Transactions can be started and stopped on any thread. Any edits that take place after a call to but before a call to either or will be considered part of that transaction, no matter what thread made the edit.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.GeoElement
member.summary: An abstract representation of geographic entities in a map, scene, map view, or scene view.

remarks.para: Each geographic entity can possess geometry, to describe the location and shape of the entity, and a set of attributes to provide information about the real-world entity it represents. For example, a feature in a feature layer, a graphic in a graphics overlay, and a raster cell in a raster layer are represented by the , , and classes. Each class inherits from the geo element.

Operations that identify all of the layers in a map or scene, such as the geo view's IdentifyLayersAsync method, can return a collection of objects. You can obtain the various types of objects using .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.GeoPackage
member.summary: A GeoPackage file (.gpkg) that can contain multiple datasets of geographic features, non-spatial tabular data, and raster dataset that conform to the OGC GeoPackage format.
member.remarks: A GeoPackage is an open, standards-based, platform-independent, portable, self-describing, compact format for transferring geospatial information. It is a platform-independent SQLite database file that contains the GeoPackage data and metadata tables. See GeoPackage Encoding Standard for more information.

For these tables, the first geometry type found in the collection is rendered.
You can query the dataset using attribute or spatial criteria, and if permitted, also edit the data. If the dataset contains geographic features you can display them on a map using a .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.OgcFeatureCollectionTable
member.summary: Represents a feature collection in an "OGC API Features" service.

remarks.para: To display data from an OGC feature collection table in a use the constructor where the is the parameter that is passed into the constructor. Note: The should not be confused with which is used as an input to create a .

This API provides building blocks for manipulating features on the Web. For more information about the OGC API Features specification see the documents: OGC API - Features - Part 1 and OGC API - Features - Part 2.

The class includes two important pieces: the property and the methods. Only the mode is supported. This means that one of the methods must be called to populate, query, and return features from the service. For the that is used by the methods, you can put any CQL-TEXT or CQL-JSON string as defined in the document Common Query Language (CQL2). Use the method to explicitly specify the query language of the .

When populating or updating a local table from an OGC service, you can now request features in a specified (also referred to as SRS in ArcGIS or CRS in OGC). This leverages the power of the server to return features in the same spatial reference as your map rather than having to re-project them on the client.
This class supports loading and querying of features from a non-spatial OGC feature collection table.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.OgcFeatureCollectionTable.PopulateFromServiceAsync(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.QueryParameters,System.Boolean,System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{System.String},System.String,System.Threading.CancellationToken)
member.summary: Populate the OGC API - Features feature collection table with the results of a query.

remarks.para: Populates the with features from the service that match the query parameters. Use the default (empty) to get all features from the service. Specifying null or an empty enumerable for will result in the default set of outfields being used. Spatial queries (those that specify geometries) must use the Intersects spatial relationship.

Some OGC feature sources allow for query expressions to be written in different languages. The parameter allows you to explicitly specify the language of the for the OGC feature query. The value of this parameter may be 'CQL2-TEXT', 'CQL2-JSON', or any other language supported by the target server. For OGC feature services the default language is `CQL2-TEXT`. For more information, refer to the OGC specification OGC API - Features - Part3. The default value is an empty string, indicating that the uses the default language for the source.

member.param: Parameters that define how features are returned from the service.
If true, clears existing table data before populating it with features returned from the service.
An optional list of attribute fields to include in the result.
The query language that the is written in.
An optional cancellation token that can be used by other objects or threads to receive notice of cancellation.
member.returns: A that represents the asynchronous populate from service operation. The value of the task result is a object.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.QueryParameters
member.summary: Represents the parameters to perform a query on a dataset.

remarks.para: These parameters provide attribute, spatial, and temporal criteria that you can use to filter features. You can define attribute criteria using a standard SQL expression based on the available attribute fields, spatial criteria using a geometry and a spatial relationship (such as within, contains, intersect), or temporal filters using a single date/time, or a range. You can also control how the results are sorted and whether to return geometry.

In addition to querying features in a table, you can also use these parameters to count specific features, calculate the extent of features, select features in layers, and populate service-based tables. If the query method produces a set of features, they are returned in a .

Different query parameter objects are available to query data sources such as related features (), statistical information (), and portals (). Specialized query parameters also exist for working with utility networks and routing.

member.remarks: For consistent ordering of results when using pagination, provide a value for . Note that if you provide a maximum features value to support paging of results, you must also set a value. Otherwise, the result may contain local features in place of server features if they satisfy the query. To ensure strict paging of server features, specify both maximum features and .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.QueryParameters.WhereClause
member.summary: Gets or sets the attribute expression that defines features to be included in the query.

remarks.para: The where clause should follow standard SQL syntax similar to that discussed in the document SQL reference for query expressions used in ArcGIS.

If the where clause includes dates, they must be correctly formatted based on the geodatabase datasource used in the service. Refer to Dates and time for date formats expected by different data sources.

objects expect that any or parameters in the string that is used as the where clause are formatted as: '{XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX}'. All characters are uppercase, wrapped in curly braces and surrounded by single quotes.
If the where clause is used to populate a , you can use a CQL2-TEXT or CQL2-JSON string as defined in the document OGC API - Features - Part 3.

member.value: The where clause of the query.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.QueryParameters.TimeExtent
member.summary: Gets or sets the used to query features in a time-aware feature layer.

remarks.para: You can apply a temporal filter on any that supports time filtering (). Set the and to query features across a span of time. A single point in time can be queried by setting them to the same time value.
By default, this value is null and no time criteria is applied by the query.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RangeDomain`1
member.summary: Specifies a valid range of numeric or date values for a .
member.remarks: A range domain can specify the minimum and maximum value of a number or date for a field. Data entries outside the range will not be accepted in the field. When a user is editing data, you can present the and to guide the user, and validate their input against the range domain before changes are saved. This helps to maintain data integrity.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedFeatureQueryResult
member.summary: Contains the features that have been returned from a related tables query.
member.remarks: Instances of this class represents the result of related queries. A prerequisite for querying related features is that all the participating tables must be part of a map, as either feature layers or non-spatial tables. An array of instances are returned, one per related table containing features related to the queried feature. This class has methods that can be used to iterate over the collection of related features.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedFeatureQueryResult.IsTransferLimitExceeded
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the query resulted in a larger number of features than what the transfer limit supports.
member.remarks: Many ArcGIS services only return up to 1000 features even if the query matched more features. When that happens for the query that produces this result, this property is true.
member.value: true if transfer limit is exceeded; otherwise, false.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedFeatureQueryResult.Feature
member.summary: Gets the feature for which related features were queried.
member.value: a

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedFeatureQueryResult.RelatedTable
member.summary: Gets the related table for this query result.
member.remarks: There can be more than one instance of the same table or layer on the map with different scale level visibility, definition expression, and so on. All such instances share the same with the table the query operation is called on. In such cases, results from all such instances are returned, and the property is used to distinguish between them.
member.value: An or null.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedFeatureQueryResult.RelationshipInfo
member.summary: Gets the relationship info
member.remarks: If more than one related table were queried, multiple results are returned, each with its RelationshipInfo.
member.value: A or null.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedFeatureQueryResult.GetEnumerator
member.summary: Returns an enumerator that iterates through a collection of .
member.returns: A enumerator.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedQueryParameters
member.summary: Parameters to perform a related query on an .

remarks.para: When querying related features, all participating tables must be included in the same map or scene, as either feature layers or non-spatial tables. The query that uses these parameters will load these related tables.

Related query parameters lets you specify:
Details about the relationship to be queried.
A where clause used to filter features from the related table.
How to sort the results.
Whether to return geometry within the results.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedQueryParameters.#ctor(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.ArcGISServices.RelationshipInfo)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class with the specified relationship information.
member.param: The relationship information between related tables.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedQueryParameters.OrderByFields
member.summary: Gets the list of order by objects that indicates how to sort the results.
member.remarks: Results can be sorted by one or more fields. Each order by instance includes a field name and whether to sort by that field in ascending or descending order. If a service-based table is being queried it must support advanced queries.
member.value: A list containing types.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedQueryParameters.RelationshipInfo
member.summary: Gets or sets the relationship information used to define the tables to use for this query.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedQueryParameters.ReturnGeometry
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether geometries are returned in the related feature query result.

remarks.para: This is needed when you want to display the features on a map or a scene. When querying non-spatial data, results do not include geometry.

member.value: true if geometry values are returned in the results, otherwise false.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedQueryParameters.WhereClause
member.summary: Gets or sets where clause to apply to the destination table.

remarks.para: The where clause should follow standard SQL syntax similar to that discussed in the document SQL reference for query expressions used in ArcGIS.

If the where clause includes dates, they must be correctly formatted based on the geodatabase datasource used in the service. Refer to Dates and time for date formats expected by different data sources.

objects expect that any or parameters in the where clause are formatted as: '{XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX}'. All characters are uppercase, wrapped in curly braces and surrounded by single quotes.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedQueryParameters.MaxFeatures
member.summary: Gets or sets the maximum number of features the query should return.

remarks.para: For consistent ordering of results when using pagination, provide a value for . Note that if you provide a maximum features value to support paging of results, you must also set a value. Otherwise, the result may contain local features in place of server features if they satisfy the query. To ensure strict paging of server features, specify both maximum features and .

member.value: The maximum number of features the query should return.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelatedQueryParameters.ResultOffset
member.summary: Gets or sets the starting offset of results to fetch. This is useful for paging through results.
member.remarks: For consistent ordering of results when using pagination, you should also provide a value for the . Note that if you provide a result offset to support paging of results, you must also set a value. Otherwise, the result offset is ignored.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelationshipConstraintViolationType
member.summary: The various constraint violations that may occur when relating two features.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelationshipConstraintViolationType.None
member.summary: There are no constraint violations.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelationshipConstraintViolationType.Cardinality
member.summary: Cardinality of the given feature is violated.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.RelationshipConstraintViolationType.Orphaned
member.summary: The given feature is an orphaned destination feature in a composite relationship. Note: Orphaned destination feature in a simple relationship is not considered a violation.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceFeatureTable
member.summary: A table of features that typically represent real-world objects created from an ArcGIS feature service.

remarks.para: A service feature table has a , which controls (1) whether features are cached locally (for quicker access by map and scene layers) and (2) whether queries are performed on the local cache or on the server. You can retrieve or modify a table's feature request mode using .

If the table's mode is , features are requested from the server in response to user or developer interaction (pan or zoom) and are cached locally. Queries are executed on the cache or (if requested features are not resident in the cache) on the server. This mode is the default.
If the table's mode is , features are always requested from the server. Features are not cached, and all queries are executed on the server.

If the table's mode is , you must call , which populates the local table with the features specified by and containing the attributes specified by . Features are cached for the duration of the session and queries are executed against the cache.

If the table's feature request mode is or , ArcGIS features in the table initially contain a minimum set of attributes and geometry that omits any m-values. This is an optimization for faster rendering. To access all attributes (and geometry with m-values) in features you get from a query, do one of the following:

list.item: Call on each returned feature individually.
Call the query method and pass the constant , which performs the query and returns features fully loaded (with all attributes and any m-values defined by the service). See and .
Call and pass a mutable array of features to be loaded.

remarks.para: When used to create a in a MapView, features are requested from the services in the correct spatial reference to match the spatial reference of the map. Use to change how curve geometries are returned from the service.
You can use a service feature table to access non-spatial tables from feature services where is false. The feature request mode of the table must be , and you must call .
The parent class definition allows a service feature table to have editable attribute fields, feature templates, feature types, and editing capabilities defined in . See and .
A service feature table might require authentication to access the service.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceFeatureTable.BufferFactor
member.summary: Gets or sets the factor used to calculate a buffered extent around the current visible area when requesting new features from the service.
member.value: A double representing the buffer factor.
member.remarks: Any interaction with the MapView which results in a Viewpoint change, such as a pan or zoom action, will request features only if the new visible area exceeds the buffered extent. The BufferFactor is applied only when the is in either OnInteractionCache or OnInteractionNoCache. By default it is 2.0 (twice the current extent).

member.summary: Gets or sets the mode defining when features are requested from the service.
member.value: The mode defining when features are requested from the service.
member.exception: FeatureRequestMode cannot be set to Undefined by user code.

member.summary: Gets or sets the geodatabase version.
member.value: The geodatabase version.

member.summary: Gets the ArcGIS Item
member.remarks: This is only available if the table is associated with an ArcGIS Item.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceFeatureTable.DefinitionExpression
member.summary: Gets or sets the expression which is a SQL statement WHERE clause to filter out the features to be queried.
member.remarks: The definition expression string uses the SQL-92 WHERE clause syntax (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SQL-92). Be sure to escape special characters in the expression string as required for your platform. The DATE keyword expects the date format yyyy-mm-dd and the TIMESTAMP keyword expects the time stamp format yyyy-mm-dd hh:mm:ss. For more information, see the ArcGIS Blog article Querying Feature Services Date-Time Queries.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceFeatureTable.ServiceGeodatabase
member.summary: Gets the this service feature table is part of.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceFeatureTable.ApplyEditsAsync
member.summary: Asynchronously uploads any changes to the local table to the feature service.
member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous apply edits operation. The value of the task result contains a read-only collection of objects.

remarks.para: ServiceFeatureTable.ApplyEditsAsync() is meant to be used for single table workflows or tables without geodatabase behavior (see below). If an edit on this table can cause an edit to another table due to geodatabase behavior, it is highly recommended to use ServiceGeodatabase.ApplyEditsAsync() instead to prevent data inconsistency, so that dependent edits are all applied or none are (if an error occurs).

For example, when tables have a composite relationship, applying the delete of a destination feature in a composite relationship deletion will only delete the destination feature on the server, therefore causing inconsistency in the data.
Areas which have geodatabase behavior:

list.item: Composite relationships
Annotation feature layers
Utility network association deletion semantics
Attribute rules

member.summary: Asynchronously uploads any changes to the local table to the feature service.
member.param: A cancellation token that can be used by other objects or threads to receive notice of cancellation.
member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous apply edits operation. The value of the task result contains a read-only collection of objects.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceFeatureTable.PopulateFromServiceAsync(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.QueryParameters,System.Boolean,System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{System.String})
member.summary: Queries the feature service and places the resulting features in the local table, which is cached for the duration of the session. The must have its set to .
member.param: The used to filter feature results.
If true, then will be called before populating the local table. If false, the resulting features will be appended to the local table.

An containing . Each string is the name of a field to be used when populating the cache. If the array contains the single element "*", then all fields will be used. If the ObjectID field string name is not provided as part of the , no features will be returned for this method.
member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous populate from service operation. The value of the task result is a object.

remarks.para: This method is useful for non-geographic data. It's also helpful when you want to avoid accessing the service for a feature whose geometry is in the current extent of the map or scene.
Specifying null or an empty for the outfields results in the minimum set of fields being used when populating the local table. This is the same set of attributes described for or .

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceFeatureTable.PopulateFromServiceAsync(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.QueryParameters,System.Boolean,System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{System.String},System.Threading.CancellationToken)

member.param: The used to filter feature results.

The attribute fields to include in the result.

member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous populate from service operation. The value of the task result is a object.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceFeatureTable.QueryFeaturesAsync(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.QueryParameters,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.QueryFeatureFields)
member.summary: Queries features from this table's cache and/or from the feature service used to create this table.
member.param: Options for controlling the operation.
Options for controlling what fields are in the features of the query result.
member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous query features operation. The value of the task result is a object.

remarks.para: If the request mode of the table is , then the query is always performed on the local table.

If the mode is , then the query is performed on the local cache, provided that the geometries of the features are within an extent that has been cached; otherwise, the query is performed on the server.
If the mode is , the query is always performed on the server.
The constants control which fields will be included with the returned features.

list.item: A table whose feature request mode is manualCache contains all fields that you specified when calling .

A table whose feature request mode is onInteractionCache or onInteractionNoCache initially contains features with a minimum set of attribute required for rendering. You must load a feature to access all its attributes. The convenience constant automatically loads the returned features into the local table, which makes all attributes available.

remarks.para: Note that you can query on any attribute defined by the feature service's table. If the attribute is not in the minimum set of attributes in your local table, the query will be performed on the server.

member.param: Options for controlling the operation.
Options for controlling what data is in the query result.
A cancellation token that can be used by other objects or threads to receive notice of cancellation.
member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous query features operation. The value of the task result is a object.

member.summary: Asynchronously queries for related features in this service feature table using the provided parameters and query feature fields. See for more details.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceFeatureTable.UndoLocalEditsAsync
member.summary: Undoes all of the local edits since the last server acknowledgment.

remarks.para: All features that were added, updated, or deleted after the last successfully completed ServiceFeatureTable.ApplyEditsAsync() will revert back to their original state; thus, any in-memory feature(s) must be discarded in favor of a new query result.

ServiceFeatureTable.UndoLocalEditsAsync() is meant to be used for single table workflows or tables without geodatabase behavior (see below). If the service which the table belongs to has geodatabase behavior, it is highly recommended to use ServiceGeodatabase.UndoLocalEditsAsync(). Undoing edits in one table can cause data inconsistencies between the local cache and the service if ServiceFeatureTable.UndoLocalEditsAsync() is used.

For example, when tables have a composite relationship, undoing only the origin feature would violate the composite relationship rule that an origin and destination feature must be either deleted together or not at all.
Areas which have geodatabase behavior:

list.item: Composite relationships
Annotation feature layers
Utility network association deletion semantics
Attribute rules

member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous undo local edits operation.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceFeatureTable.LoadOrRefreshFeaturesAsync(System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.Feature})
member.summary: Loads all attributes and geometries for not loaded features, and re-fetches attributes and geometries for previously loaded features. All features loaded or refreshed by this method will have m-values if defined by the service. Note that an m-value might be NaN.
member.param: set of s to load or refresh from the table.

remarks.para: Features you get from or might be in the minimally loaded state (minimum attributes required for rendering and no m-values). To load all attributes, pass a mutable array of the features to this method.
Any feature requested, but not returned, by the server will have its object id cleared (set to an invalid negative value) to indicate it is no longer associated with the service feature table.
After calling this method, any non-applied edits on the table will be lost.
This method will not refresh feature attachments. Use to retrieve attachments.

member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous load or refresh of features operation.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceGeodatabase.SwitchVersionAsync(System.String,System.Threading.CancellationToken)
member.summary: Asynchronously switches all connected feature tables to the new version.

remarks.para: An error is thrown if:

list.item: The service isn't branch versioned.
No version exists with the supplied name.
Any of the have unapplied edits.

remarks.para: Check the result of before attempting to switch versions, to make sure all changes are saved to the service or discarded from the local cache. Use the ServiceGeodatabase.ApplyEditsAsync(), ServiceGeodatabase.ApplyEditsAsync(CancellationToken) or ServiceGeodatabase.UndoLocalEditsAsync() methods as appropriate to save or discard changes before switching versions.

member.param: The name of the version to connect to.
A with which to cancel the operation if required.
member.returns: A task that represents the switching to the specified version.
member.exception: No version exists with the supplied name.
Any of the have unapplied edits.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceGeodatabase.ApplyEditsAsync(System.Threading.CancellationToken)
member.summary: Applies all local edits in all tables to the service.

remarks.para: This method applies all feature table edits to the feature service as a single transaction. If one of the edits fails, all edits are rolled-back, and geodatabase integrity is preserved. This is the recommended approach to applying edits and is typically more efficient than calling ServiceFeatureTable.ApplyEditsAsync() on each table.
However, this method can only succeed if all tables either support or do not support global IDs. A mix of global ID support is not permitted and will result in .
Use to determine whether you can successfully apply your edits through this method.

member.param: A with which to cancel the operation if required.
member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous apply edits operation. The value of the task result contains a list of loaded objects.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceGeodatabase.ApplyEditsAsync
member.summary: Applies all local edits in all tables to the service.

remarks.para: This method applies all feature table edits to the feature service as a single transaction. If one of the edits fails, all edits are rolled-back, and geodatabase integrity is preserved. This is the recommended approach to applying edits and is typically more efficient than calling ServiceFeatureTable.ApplyEditsAsync() on each table.
However, this method can only succeed if all tables either support or do not support global IDs. A mix of global ID support is not permitted and will result in .
Use to determine whether you can successfully apply your edits through this method.

member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous apply edits operation. The value of the task result contains a list of loaded objects.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ServiceGeodatabase.CloseAsync
member.summary: Closes this service geodatabase.

remarks.para: A request to stop any active reading or editing sessions with the feature service is sent and once this asynchronous process is complete, this service geodatabase will be closed.

Before calling this method, there should be no references to all related data. For example, terminate fetch versions, create version, switch, apply or undo edits, remove feature layers, and release tables from map.
After calling this method, accessing this service geodatabase or any of its connected tables will fail with .
Closing this service geodatabase is not necessary if it has not been loaded.

member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous close operation.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ShapefileFeatureTable
member.summary: A table of features that typically represents real-world objects created from a local shapefile data source.

remarks.para: A shapefile is a vector data storage format that stores the location, shape, and attributes of geographic features with the same geometry type (such as point, line, and polygon) and the same spatial reference. A shapefile dataset consists of at least three files (.shp, .shx, .dbf), and may include many others. Each of the component files of a shapefile must be smaller than 2 GB. If the minimum required files are not present (or are larger than 2 GB), the table will fail to load. If the file permissions are read-only, features cannot be edited.
See Shapefiles in ArcGIS Pro for more information about shapefile data types and limitations.
On-the-fly projection of a shapefile is supported if an associated .prj file is present. Otherwise, the features are assumed to have the same spatial reference as the map.
A default renderer is used to render a shapefile feature table in a feature layer. In most cases, the default should be replaced with a custom renderer.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ShapefileFeatureTable.#ctor
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class.

member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class.
member.param: Path to the *.shp shape file.
member.remarks: When creating a shapefile feature table, the shapefilePath must point to the *.shp file and the associated *.shx and *.dbf files must be present at the same location.

member.summary: Creates and initializes a from a file path.
member.param: Path to the *.shp shape file.

member.returns: A task that represents the asynchronous open operation. The value of the task result is a object.

member.summary: Gets the shapefile info.

member.summary: Gets or sets the path to the shapefile
member.remarks: The path must point to the *.shp file in a folder accessible by the application and the associated *.shx and *.dbf files must be present at the same location. The path can only be assigned when the is not loaded or has previously failed to load.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ShapefileFeatureTable.SpatialIndexEnabled
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the shapefile uses spatial indexes.
member.remarks: ArcGIS for Desktop can be used to create spatial indexes for shapefiles (stored in *.sbn and *.sbx files). Having a current spatial index ensures that a high level of performance is maintained when drawing and working with the shapefile's features and that the shapefile's extent is accurate.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.ShapefileFeatureTable.Close
member.summary: Closes a shapefile feature table.

remarks.para: Before you close a shapefile feature table you should remove all references to the data it contains, such as removing the feature layer from a map and releasing the shapefile feature table. Attempts to access a shapefile feature table after it is closed will fail, resulting in an error.
After closing a shapefile feature table, the underlying files it references can be deleted or moved.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.StatisticsQueryParameters.WhereClause
member.summary: Gets or sets the attribute expression that defines features to be included in the statistics query.

remarks.para: The where clause should follow standard SQL syntax similar to that discussed in the document SQL reference for query expressions used in ArcGIS.

If the where clause includes dates, they must be correctly formatted based on the geodatabase datasource used in the service. Refer to Dates and time for date formats expected by different data sources.

objects expect that any or parameters in the where clause are formatted as: '{XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXXXXXXXXXX}'. All characters are uppercase, wrapped in curly braces and surrounded by single quotes.

A combination of attribute, spatial, and temporal criteria can be used to filter the features evaluated in a statistical query. If no filters are defined, all features in the table are included in the query.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.WfsFeatureTable
member.summary: A table of features that typically represents real-world objects from an OGC Web Feature Service (WFS) dataset.

remarks.para: You can create WFS feature table using the URL of an OGC Web Feature Service and a specified layer name. Alternatively, you can connect to a , obtain its metadata from , and discover its available layers using . You can then create a from an individual layer using .
When you have created a WFS Feature table, features are not automatically requested. Instead, you must set the WFS Feature table request mode to and then populate the table using , for example.
You can visualize features from a WFS feature table using a but the table does not support editing. See the OGC Web Feature Service standard for more information.

member.param: The URL of a WFS service.
The name of the feature table object (feature type) from the WFS service.

member.param: object that contains the metadata of the layer.

member.summary: Gets the service .
member.value: The URL of the Web Feature Service.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.WfsFeatureTable.AxisOrder
member.summary: Gets or sets the axis order, which defines how coordinates are interpreted (X,Y or Y,X).
member.remarks: Some WFS services return coordinates in (x,y) order, while others use (y,x). Setting this property to will result in all coordinates in geometries being swapped from what was sent by the server. The axis order can be changed on a loaded table, but that change will only affect future calls to . Features that are already in the table will not be modified. The default value is . In this mode, the results are optimized using heuristics from popular WFS Servers.
member.value: Whether coordinates are interpreted as-is or swapped.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.WfsFeatureTable.FilterAxisOrder
member.summary: Gets or sets the filter axis order, which determines how coordinates are ordered when sent to the server as part of spatial queries.
member.remarks: Spatial queries are queries with set. Some WFS services expect coordinates to be sent in (X,Y) order, while others use (Y,X). Use to send coordinates in (Y,X) order or to use (X,Y) order. Defaults to .
member.value: Whether coordinates are swapped before being sent to the server.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.WfsFeatureTable.FeatureRequestMode
member.summary: Gets or sets the mode defining when features are requested from the service.
member.remarks: You must set the feature request mode to before attempting to populate the table using .
member.value: The mode defining when features are requested from the service.
member.exception: FeatureRequestMode cannot be set to Undefined by user code.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.WfsFeatureTable.LayerInfo
member.summary: Gets the WFS layer metadata, which includes name and description.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.WfsFeatureTable.PreferredSpatialReference
member.summary: Gets or sets the preferred spatial reference for the table.

remarks.para: If the service does not provide a preferred spatial reference, or you have not set it explicitly, then the table will fail to load. The preferred spatial reference cannot be changed once the table is loaded.
Set the preferred spatial reference to match the map or scene's spatial reference to avoid the automatic reprojection of features.

member.value: Spatial Reference

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.AreaUnit
member.summary: Class used to represent units of measurement for an area.

remarks.para: A common scenario for using the AreaUnit class is to display various for polygon geometry. For example, a user would want to know the area of a polygon in Acres, Hectares, SquareMiles, etc. Typically, the static method is used on an input and the value returned is in square meters. The method could then be used to obtain one or more of those AreaUnits values (i.e. Acres, Hectares, SquareMiles, etc).

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.CoordinateFormatter
member.summary: Converts between s and formatted coordinate notation strings. Supported formats include: decimal degrees (DD); degrees, minutes, and seconds (DMS); U.S. National Grid (USNG); Military Grid Reference System (MGRS); and others.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.CoordinateFormatter.FromGars(System.String,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GarsConversionMode)
member.summary: Parses a coordinate string in the Global Area Reference System (GARS) notation, and returns a representing that location.

remarks.para: The provided parameter must have an ellipsoid and datum matching those used by the source of the GARS string. If no is provided, it is assumed that the GARS string is referenced to . The GARS string must not contain any whitespace.

member.param: The GARS notation string for the coordinate.
An optional that defines the datum and ellipsoid referenced by the GARS coordinate. May be null, in which case is used.
Select whether the returned point's location represents the south-west corner of the GARS cell the coordinate lies within, or its center.
member.returns: A with the location from the GARS string in the spatial reference provided.
member.exception: Given coordinates could not be parsed or converted.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.CoordinateFormatter.FromGeoRef(System.String,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference)
member.summary: Parses a coordinate string in World Geographic Reference System (GEOREF) notation, and returns a representing that location.

remarks.para: The provided parameter must have an ellipsoid and datum matching those used by the source of the GEOREF string. If no is provided, it is assumed that the GEOREF string is referenced to . The GEOREF string may contain leading and trailing whitespace.

member.param: The GEOREF notation string for the coordinate.
A that defines the datum and ellipsoid referenced by the GEOREF coordinate.
member.returns: A with the location from the GEOREF string in the spatial reference provided.
member.exception: Given coordinates could not be parsed or converted.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.CoordinateFormatter.FromLatitudeLongitude(System.String,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference)
member.summary: Parses a coordinate string in latitude-longitude notation, and returns a representing that location. The coordinate may use decimal degrees (DD); degrees and decimal minutes (DDM); or degrees, minutes, and seconds (DMS) format.

remarks.para: The provided parameter must have an ellipsoid and datum matching those used by the source of the latitude-longitude string. If no is provided, it is assumed that the latitude-longitude coordinates are referenced to .

The latitude-longitude string may contain leading and trailing whitespace, and may also contain a space, comma, or vertical bar symbol to separate the latitude from the longitude. It may also contain the characters 'N', 'S', 'E' and 'W', or use a minus (-) symbol to indicate the hemisphere of each value.

member.param: The latitude-longitude notation string for the coordinate.
An optional that defines the datum and ellipsoid referenced by the latitude-longitude coordinate. May be null, in which case is used.
member.returns: A with the location from the coordinate string in the spatial reference provided.
member.exception: Given coordinates could not be parsed or converted.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.CoordinateFormatter.FromMgrs(System.String,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MgrsConversionMode)
member.summary: Parses a coordinate string in the Military Grid Reference System (MGRS) notation, and returns a representing that location.

remarks.para: The provided parameter must have an ellipsoid and datum matching those used by the source of the MGRS string. If no is provided, it is assumed that the MGRS string is referenced to .

For an explanation of the different modes for interpreting an MGRS notation string, please see . Note that the choice between zone 01 and 60 has no impact when reading from an MGRS notation string. The MGRS string can contain leading and trailing whitespace and can have whitespace between the grid zone designator, the 100km square identifier, and the numerical eastings and northings.

member.param: The MGRS notation string for the coordinate.
An optional that defines the datum and ellipsoid referenced by the MGRS string. May be null, in which case is used.
The used by the given MGRS coordinates.
member.returns: A with the location from the MGRS string in the spatial reference provided.
member.exception: Given coordinates could not be parsed or converted.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.CoordinateFormatter.FromUsng(System.String,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference)
member.summary: Parses a coordinate in United States National Grid (USNG) notation and returns a representing that location.

remarks.para: The provided parameter must have an ellipsoid and datum matching those used by the source of the USNG string. If no is provided and the USNG string is suffixed with "(NAD 27)", it is assumed the USNG string is referenced to NAD 27. If no spatial reference is provided and no such suffix exists, it is assumed the USNG string is referenced to . The USNG string can contain leading and trailing whitespace and can have whitespace between the grid zone designator, the 100km square identifier, and the numerical eastings and northings.

member.param: The USNG notation string for the coordinate.
An optional that defines the datum and ellipsoid referenced by the USNG coordinate.
member.returns: A with the location from the USNG string in the spatial reference provided.
member.exception: Given coordinates could not be parsed or converted.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.CoordinateFormatter.FromUtm(System.String,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.UtmConversionMode)
member.summary: Parses a coordinate in the Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM) notation and returns a representing that location.

remarks.para: The provided parameter must have an ellipsoid and datum matching those used by the source of the UTM string. If no is provided, it is assumed that the UTM string is referenced to . The UTM string can contain leading and trailing whitespace; whitespace between the zone and latitude designator; and between the numerical eastings and northings.

member.param: The UTM notation string for the coordinate.
An optional that defines the datum and ellipsoid referenced by the UTM coordinate. May be null, in which case is used.
The latitude notation scheme used by the given UTM coordinate: either a latitudinal band or a hemisphere designator.
member.returns: A with the location from the UTM string in the spatial reference provided.
member.exception: Given coordinates could not be parsed or converted.

remarks.para:
Example output for a point in the southern hemisphere:
parameter value
parameter valueExample output
false30U4898846199881
true30U 489884 6199881

true30N 489884 6199881

member.param: The coordinate to be represented in UTM notation. It must have a set.
The to use in the returned UTM notation string: either a latitudinal band or a hemisphere designator.
If false, the generated string contains no spaces. If true, a space separates the UTM zone and latitude designator, and each numerical easting/northing value.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.DatumTransformation
member.summary: Represents a function to convert between two coordinate systems.

remarks.para: This is the base class for classes used to transform coordinates between spatial references that have different datums. The inverse of the datum transformation, used to transform in the opposite direction, may be accessed using a member function.
A datum transformation has a property intended to be suitable for display, such as when displaying a list of available transformations to an end user.

You can get a list of suitable transformations for a given input and output spatial reference using methods of the class. Some transformations require that certain Projection Engine data files be present on the local file system. The property indicates whether any of the files are missing. The complete list of necessary files is available using the property.
In order for any Projection Engine files to be found, the data location must be set first using the property.
A datum transformation object is immutable.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.DatumTransformation.InputSpatialReference
member.summary: Gets the input SpatialReference.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.DatumTransformation.OutputSpatialReference
member.summary: Gets the output SpatialReference.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.DatumTransformation.IsMissingProjectionEngineFiles
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the supporting file for the transformation can be located in the location set in the property.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.DatumTransformation.Name
member.summary: Gets the name of the datum transformation.
member.remarks: For multi-step transformations, the name contains the concatenated names of each step's transformation, separated by a plus sign '+'. If the transformation is inverted, the name starts with a tilde (~).

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.DatumTransformation.GetInverse
member.summary: Returns the inverse of this datum transformation or null if the transformation is not invertible.
member.returns: An inversed DatumTransformation

member.returns: true if the two transformations are the equal.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment
member.summary: An elliptic arc segment for use in a multipart geometry.
member.remarks: An elliptic arc is the portion of the boundary of a 2D ellipse that connects two points.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment.#ctor(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint,System.Double,System.Double,System.Double,System.Double,System.Double,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class based on parameters that define an ellipse and the portion of that ellipse that defines the arc.

remarks.para: The spatial reference parameter is used if the center point parameter has a null spatial reference. If both spatial references are supplied, they must be equal.
The z-value and m-value of the center point (if present) are ignored. Use to create an with end points with a z-value and/or m-value.

member.param: The center point of the embedded ellipse.

The angle in radians by which the major axis of the embedded ellipse is rotated from the x-axis. A positive value corresponds to a counterclockwise rotation from the x-axis. The value is taken in the form of the modulo of 2 * PI (360 degrees). For example, an input rotation angle of 7.5 radians (430 degrees) would return a segment with a rotation angle of 1.22 radians (70 degrees).
The length of the semi-major axis of the embedded ellipse in the units of the spatial reference.
The ratio of the length of the semi-minor axis to the length of the semi-major axis of the embedded ellipse.

The parametric angle in radians of the start of the arc relative to the major axis of the embedded ellipse. A positive value corresponds to a counterclockwise rotation from the major axis. The value is taken in the form of the modulo of 2 * PI (360 degrees). For example, an input rotation angle of 7.5 radians (430 degrees) would return a segment with a rotation angle of 1.22 radians (70 degrees).

The parametric angle in radians measuring the span of the arc from to . A positive value corresponds to a counterclockwise arc sweep. Values larger than 2 * PI (360 degrees) return a segment with a central angle of exactly 2 * PI (360 degrees), resulting in a full ellipse where the start and end points are identical.
A spatial reference to use for the segment if the center point parameter does not have a spatial reference set.
member.exception: Indicates that the spatial reference objects supplied (as a parameter or as a property of the center point parameter, that are not null), are not equal.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment.#ctor(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint,System.Double,System.Boolean,System.Boolean,System.Double,System.Double,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class from the given start and end points, and other parameters that define an ellipse.

remarks.para: The z-value and m-value of the start and end points (if present) are used in the .

To maintain the given startPoint and endPoint in the new segment, the other parameters may be adjusted if required. Such adjustments are made according to the Scalable Vector Graphics 1.1 Specification, Appendix F.6.5. If these cannot be adjusted sufficiently, an arc represented with a straight line is created.

member.param: The start point of the segment.
The end point of the segment.
The angle in radians by which the major axis of the embedded ellipse is rotated from the x-axis. A positive value corresponds to a counterclockwise rotation from the x-axis.
true if of the segment is less than PI.
true if the direction of the segment, from start point to end point, proceeds in a counterclockwise direction, otherwise false.
The length of the semi-major axis of the embedded ellipse in the units of the spatial reference.
The ratio of the length of the semi-minor axis to the length of the semi-major axis of the embedded ellipse.
A spatial reference to use for the segment if the points do not have spatial references set.
member.exception: Indicates that the spatial reference objects supplied (as a parameter or as a property of the center point parameter, that are not null), are not equal.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment.CenterPoint
member.summary: Gets the center point of the ellipse that this segment is defined upon.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment.CentralAngle
member.summary: Gets the parametric angle in radians measuring the span of the arc from to .

remarks.para: A positive value corresponds to a counterclockwise arc sweep.
This value is always between -2*PI and 2*PI, these limits indicating this arc forms a complete ellipse in either clockwise or counterclockwise direction.

member.value: Central angle in radians.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment.EndAngle
member.summary: Gets the parametric angle in radians of the end of the arc relative to the major axis of the embedded ellipse.
member.remarks: A positive value corresponds to a counterclockwise rotation from the major axis.
member.value: End angle in radians.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment.IsCircular
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether this arc is a portion of the boundary of a 2D circle.

remarks.para: An elliptic arc is circular if the ellipse upon which it is based is a circle, meaning the lengths of its major and minor axes are equal.
Use and to create circular arcs.

member.value: true if the arc is circular and the major and minor axes are equal, otherwise false.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment.IsCounterClockwise
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the direction of the segment, from start point to end point, proceeds in a counterclockwise direction.
member.value: true if the arc direction is counterterclockwise, otherwise false.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment.MinorMajorRatio
member.summary: Gets the ratio of the length of the semi-minor axis to the semi-major axis.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment.RotationAngle
member.summary: Gets the angle in radians by which the major axis of the ellipse this segment is based upon is rotated from the x-axis.
member.value: Rotation angle in radians.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment.SemiMajorAxis
member.summary: Gets the length of the longer of the two axes of the ellipse upon which this arc is based.
member.remarks: The semi-major axis always lies on the line between 0 and PI radians. The length is in the units of the spatial reference.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment.SemiMinorAxis
member.summary: Gets the length of the shorter of the two axes of the ellipse upon which this arc is based.
member.remarks: The semi-minor axis always lies on the line between PI/2 and 3*PI/2 radians. The length is in the units of the spatial reference.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.EllipticArcSegment.StartAngle
member.summary: Gets the parametric angle in radians of the start of the arc relative to the major axis of the embedded ellipse.
member.remarks: A positive value corresponds to a counterclockwise rotation from the major axis.
member.value: Starting angle in radians.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Envelope
member.summary: Represents an axis-aligned envelope.

remarks.para: An envelope is defined by a pair of x,y coordinates representing the lower-left and upper-right vertices of a rectangular bounding-box. The vertices may optionally contain z coordinates and/or m (measure) values. An envelope is associated with a which specifies the coordinate system.

An envelope is typically used to represent the minimum bounding rectangle for a or to define a simple area of interest such as a 's extent. Ordinarily, an envelope is a rectangle whose sides are oriented parallel to the x and y axes. These characteristics allow for simpler construction and manipulation of this object than is possible with a polygon.
An envelope may optionally have a , in which case its geometry is that of a rectangular cuboid.

Envelopes are based upon the parent Class. The Geometry Class is immutable which means that you can not change its shape once it is created. If you need to modify an Envelope once it has been created, use the Class instead. The Method will provide you with the Envelope object.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GeographicTransformation
member.summary: Used to transform coordinates of geometries between spatial references that have two different geographic coordinate systems.

remarks.para: Each geographic transformation has an input and an output . The transformation operates on the horizontal (geographic) datums in each spatial reference.
The inverse of the geographic transformation, if any, used to transform in the opposite direction, may be accessed using a member function.

A geographic transformation can be constructed from a single geographic transformation step object, or from a list of geographic transformation steps objects that are chained together. Most transformations between spatial references that do not share the WGS 1984 datum use WGS 1984 as an intermediate datum. Thus, it is common to create a geographic transformation object with two geographic transformation steps: first to transform from the datum in the input spatial reference to WGS 1984, and then from WGS 1984 to the output spatial reference's datum. There are a limited number of transformations directly between two non-WGS84 datums, such as WKID 4461, which is NAD_1983_HARN_To_NAD_1983_NSRS2007_1. These do not need WGS 1984 as an intermediate datum.

In most cases, you do not need to construct your own GeographicTransformation. You can get a list of suitable transformations for a given input and output spatial reference using one of the functions on the transformation catalog class.
A geographic transformation object is immutable.

member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class.
member.param: The IEnumerable of objects used to create a .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GeographicTransformation.Steps
member.summary: Gets a list of geographic transformations that define this composite geographic transformation.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GeographicTransformationStep
member.summary: Represents a step in the process of transforming between datums.

remarks.para: Each geographic transformation step can be constructed from a well-known ID (WKID) that represents a geographic transformation. Because the Projection Engine supports thousands of transformations, WKIDs are not presented in the SDK as enumerations. Instead, they are documented in the guide and online.

The list of supported WKIDs includes a transformation from every supported datum to WGS 1984. Additionally, there is more limited list of transformations directly between two non-WGS84 datums, such as 4461, which is NAD_1983_HARN_To_NAD_1983_NSRS2007_1.
Transformations with more than one step typically go via WGS84, with one forward and one inverse geographic transformation chained together to get the required geographic coordinates.
A geographic transformation step object is immutable.

member.remarks: Occasionally, WKIDs may change, and older codes may be deprecated in favor of a new code. Both old (deprecated) and new (latest) WKIDs continue to work for instantiation, as long as they are supported by the Projection Engine. The property returns the new (latest) WKID code.
member.param: The well-known ID of the geographic transformation step to create.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GeographicTransformationStep.IsInverse
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether this geographic transformation step is an inverted transformation.

remarks.para: Transformations have a specific direction that is indicated by the value. An inverted transformation is used to transform geometries in the opposite direction to that indicated by the well-known text. has and properties that respect the inverse value of the contained transformations.

This API supports a large number of transformation WKIDs, including transformations from every supported datum to the World Geodetic System 1984 (WGS84) datum. To transform coordinates between two non-WGS84 datums, typically one forward and one inverse are added to a , to get the required inputs and outputs.

member.value: true if this geographic transformation step is an inverted transformation; otherwise, false.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Geometry
member.summary: Base class for all classes that represent geometric shapes.

remarks.para: is the base class for two-dimensional (x,y) and three-dimensional (x,y,z) geometries, such as , , , , and . It represents real-world objects by defining a shape at a specific geographic location, and is used throughout the API to represent the shapes of features and graphics, layer extents, viewpoints, and GPS locations. It is also used to define the inputs and outputs for spatial analysis and geoprocessing operations, and to measure distances and areas.
All types of geometry:

list.item: Have a indicating the coordinate system used by its coordinates.
Can be empty, indicating that they have no specific location or shape.
May have z-values and/or m-values to define elevation and measures respectively.
Can be converted to and from JSON to be persisted or to be exchanged directly with REST services.

remarks.para: Most geometries are created and not changed for their lifetime. Examples include features created to be stored in a geodatabase or read from a non-editable layer, and features returned from tasks such as a spatial query, geocode operation, network trace, or geoprocessing task. Immutable geometries (geometries that cannot be changed) offer some important benefits to your app. They are inherently thread-safe, help prevent inadvertent changes, and allow for certain performance optimizations.

On the surface, immutability may appear to present a problem when attempting to edit existing geometries. Creating and updating, however, is handled by the various types of , which are designed to represent the state of a geometry under construction while allowing modifications, thus enabling editing workflows. You can also use the to create new geometries, and change existing geometries, by interacting directly with a MapView.

If you want to modify the shape of a there are two options available:
. Use a geometry builder if you want to incrementally reshape a geometry. If you want to reshape a , for example, then pass the polygon to a . The polygon builder copies the polygon and provides methods to add, update, and delete the polygon parts and segment vertices. The geometry builder represents the state of a geometry under modification, and you can obtain it at any time using .
. Use a geometry editor if you want to allow the user to interactively modify an existing geometry. Start the by passing the geometry to . The start method signals to the geometry editor to start capturing user interaction with the map through mouse or touch gestures.

Note that the offers a range of topological and spatial transformations that can create a new geometry from an existing geometry. The allows you to perform actions on an existing geometry, such as a buffer, cut, clip, densify, or project, to produce a new output geometry. See to explore various supported geometric operations.
Coordinate units

The coordinates that define a geometry are only meaningful in the context of the geometry's . The vertices and spatial reference together allow your app to translate a real-world object from its location on the Earth to its location on your map or scene.

In some cases, a geometry's spatial reference may not be set. A that does not have a spatial reference is drawn using the same spatial reference as the geo view to which it was added. When using to create a or from a collection of , you don't need to set the spatial reference of every point before you add it to the builder, as it is assigned the spatial reference of the builder itself. In most other cases, such as when using a geometry in geometry operations or when editing a feature table, must be set.
Spatial reference and projection

Changing the coordinates of a geometry to have the same shape and location represented using a different is known as "projection" or sometimes as "reprojection". Because geometries are immutable, they do not have any member methods that project, transform, or otherwise modify their content.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Geometry.HasCurves
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the geometry has any curves.

remarks.para: ArcGIS software supports polygon and polyline geometries that contain curve segments (where is true, sometimes known as true curves or nonlinear segments). Curves may be present in certain types of data, such as Mobile Map Packages (MMPK) or geometry JSON. When connecting to ArcGIS feature services that support curves (see ), this API retrieves densified versions of curve feature geometries by default.

If a polygon or polyline geometry contains curve segments, this property returns true. You can use curve segments when using a to create or edit polygon and polyline geometries, and also get curve segments when iterating through the segments of existing geometries when this property returns true. You can also choose to return true curves from feature services by using .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Geometry.Dimension
member.summary: Gets a number that describes the dimensionality of a geometry.
member.remarks: The dimension indicates the number of spatial dimensions in which the geometry may have a size. Geometry dimensions can be used to determine which type of symbol can be applied to a specific type of geometry. For example, and are both zero-dimensional point geometries, and both can be displayed using a .

remarks.para: Various implementations of method (i.e. , , , , , , ) provide the strictest comparison of two geometries to ensure that they are identical. For a slightly more relaxed comparison (one that does not take coordinate order into account), use . The following table provides a comparison of these options:

member.param: A geometry to test for equality.
member.returns: true if this geometry is exactly equal to ; otherwise false.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Geometry.Equals(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Geometry,System.Double)
member.summary: Checks if two geometries are approximately same, within some tolerance.

remarks.para: This function performs a lightweight comparison of two geometries that might be useful when writing test code. It uses the tolerance to compare each of x, y, and any other values the geometries possess (such as z or m) independently in the manner: abs(value1 - value2) <= tolerance. The single tolerance value is used even if the x, y, z or m units differ. This function does not respect modular arithmetic of spatial references which wrap around, so longitudes of -180 and +180 degrees are considered to differ by 360 degrees.
Returns true if the difference of each is within the tolerance and all other properties of the geometries are exactly equal (such as spatial reference and vertex count).
For topological equality, use a relational operator such as .

member.param: The second geometry.
The tolerance.
member.returns: true if given geometries are equal within the tolerance; otherwise false.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GeometryBuilder`1
member.summary: Builder for creating and modifying geometries incrementally.

remarks.para: This is the base class for a range of geometry builders, such as a , and . Each has a corresponding type of builder. You can create and modify polygons with , envelopes with , and so on. Use a geometry builder in editing workflows where you need to build up or edit geometry one vertex at a time, for example, when you need to add or edit a vertex from a custom streaming GIS data source. You can either create an empty geometry builder and build up the shape of a , or you can create a geometry builder with an existing and modify it.

When you construct the builder, you can explicitly set its or you can construct the builder with a geometry and the builder will adopt the . Once set, the cannot be changed. The of any geometry or coordinates added to the builder must be compatible with the of the builder, as they will not be reprojected. The of a geometry added to the builder can be null, in which case the object is assumed to have the same as the builder it is added to.

There are other ways to create and edit geometries. If you know all the geometry coordinates up front, then you can use geometry constructors, such as , to create the geometry. If you are going to create a new geometry as a result of a topological operation, such as the buffer operation, then explore the . If you want your app users to interactively create or edit geometries in the user interface then use the .

member.remarks: Once set, the of the geometry builder cannot be changed. Ensure that all objects added to the builder have a compatible .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GeometryBuilder`1.HasM
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the geometry builder supports geometries with m values.
member.remarks: M values are often referred to as measures, and are used in linear referencing workflows on linear datasets. NaN is a valid m value. If true, m values are stored for each vertex of the constructed geometry. Geometries with m values are created by using setters or constructors that take an m value as a parameter.
member.value: true if the geometry builder supports geometries with m values, false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GeometryBuilder`1.HasZ
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the geometry builder supports geometries with z values.
member.remarks: Z values are generally used as a z coordinate, indicating height or elevation. NaN is a valid z value. If true, z values are stored for each vertex of the constructed geometry. Geometries with z values are created by using setters or constructors that take a z value as a parameter.
member.value: true if the geometry builder supports geometries with z values, false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GeometryBuilder`1.IsEmpty
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the geometry builder is empty or not.
member.remarks: An empty geometry builder may have a valid , even without coordinates.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GeometryBuilder`1.IsSketchValid
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether this builder contains sufficient points to show a valid graphical sketch.

remarks.para: This can be used as an initial lightweight check to see if the current state of a builder produces a non-empty geometry. For example, it may be used to enable or disable functionality in an editing user interface. The exact requirements vary depending on the type of geometry produced by the builder:

list.item: A must contain non-NaN x,y coordinates.
A must contain at least one valid .
A must contain at least one . Each it contains must have: at least two valid points, or at least one where is true.
A must contain at least one . Each it contains must have: at least three valid points, or at least one where is true.
must contain non-NaN minimum and maximum x and y values.

remarks.para: Note that this is not equivalent to topological simplicity, which is enforced by and checked using . Geometries must be topologically simple to be successfully saved in a geodatabase or used in some service operations.
Does not check the .

member.value: true if the geometry builder contains sufficient points to show a valid graphical sketch, false otherwise.

You can use curves in a . New segment types, such as and , represent types of curve that can be added to polygon and polyline geometries.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GeometryEngine
member.summary: Performs geometric operations such as spatial relationship tests, reprojections, shape manipulations, topological query and analysis operations on objects.

remarks.para: Capabilities include:

list.item: Create new geometries from others with , and .
Test spatial relationships between geometries such as and .
Find the or between geometries.
Reproject a geometry to another using .
Calculate area and length using and , or the geodetic equivalents ( and ) that account for the curvature of the earth.

remarks.para: generally operates in two dimensions; operations do not account for z-values unless documented as such for a specific method (for example transforms z-values in some cases).

Geodetic methods are better suited to data that have a geographic spatial reference (see , especially for large-area, small-scale use, while planar methods are suitable to data that have a projected coordinate system, especially for local, large-scale areas. Geodetic methods indicate this in the name, for example .

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GeometryEngine.NormalizeCentralMeridian(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Geometry)
member.summary: Folds the geometry into a range of 360 degrees. This may be necessary when wrap around is enabled on the map. If Geometry is an Envelope then a Polygon will be returned unless the Envelope is empty then and Empty Envelope will be returned.
