member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference.HasVertical
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether spatial reference has a vertical coordinate system set.

remarks.para: A spatial reference can optionally include a definition for a vertical coordinate system (VCS), which can be used to interpret the z-values of a geometry. A VCS defines linear units of measure, the origin of z-values, and whether z-values are "positive up" (representing heights above a surface) or "positive down" (indicating that values are depths below a surface).
A may have a VCS set, for example by calling the method. , , and provide more information about the specific VCS set on this instance.
Vertical coordinate systems (VCS) are used when projecting geometries using a .

member.value: true if spatial reference has a vertical coordinate system set; false otherwise.

member.remarks: Some SpatialReferences can have older referring to the same underlying definition. An example is the Web mercator old id of 102100 and 3857 are both considered to be equal.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference.GetConvergenceAngle(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint)
member.summary: Calculates the grid convergence for a spatial reference at a given point.
member.remarks: The grid convergence is the angle between True North and Grid North at a point on a map. The grid convergence can be used to convert a horizontal direction expressed as an azimuth in a geographic coordinate system (relative to True North) to a direction expressed as a bearing in a projected coordinate system (relative to Grid North), and vice versa. Sign convention The grid convergence returned by this method is positive when Grid North lies east of True North. The following formula demonstrates how to obtain a bearing ( b ) from an azimuth ( a ) using the grid convergence ( c ) returned by this method: b = a - c This sign convention is sometimes named the Gauss-Bomford convention. Other notes Returns 0 if the spatial reference is a geographic coordinate system. Returns NAN if the point is outside the projection's horizon. If the point has no spatial reference, it is assumed to be in the given spatial reference. If the point's spatial reference differs from the spatial reference given, its location is transformed automatically to the given spatial reference.
member.param: The point
member.returns: The grid convergence in degrees.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.TransformationCatalog
member.summary: Allows discovery and management of the transformations used to convert coordinates between different datums.

remarks.para: The transformation catalog class provides a mechanism for discovering available objects. You can retrieve the default transformation used internally to convert coordinates, and find other available transformations. An area of interest may be taken into account.

This class also allows you to set the location where Projection Engine data files needed for some transformations are found on local storage. The property must be set at process startup. Projection Engine data files are available in the downloads area of developers.arcgis.com, under the relevant SDK, in the `Projection Engine Data` section. The default transformation used when reprojecting geometries is the most suitable available transformation across the area covered by the input and output spatial references. You can sometimes get a more suitable transformation for work within a smaller area by specifying the extent of your specific work area, using .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.TransformationCatalog.ProjectionEngineDirectory
member.summary: Gets or sets the directory of the Projection Engine files on the local file system.

remarks.para: This property should only be set upon process startup - a suitable point could be after license or license keys, but before any use of maps, maps views, scene views, or any other functionality that may involve geometry or projection functionality.
Projection Engine data files are available in the downloads area of developers.arcgis.com (requires login).

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.TransformationCatalog.GetTransformation(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference)
member.summary: Gets the best usable (default) transformation to transform between the input and output spatial references, without taking into account an area of interest.
member.param: The to use as the input.
The to use as the output.

remarks.para: The default transformation is the one that is used when calling without specifying a transformation.

returns.para: A instance that represents the best choice given the parameters. Always returns a usable transformation where is false. Returns null if no usable transformation is available for the given input parameters, or if no transformation is required.
If both inputSpatialReference and outputSpatialReference have a vertical coordinate system, this method returns a instance. If either does not have a vertical coordinate system, a is returned.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.TransformationCatalog.GetTransformation(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Envelope)
member.summary: Gets the best usable transformation to transform between the input and output spatial references and takes into account the area of interest, if specified.
member.param: The to use as the input.
The to use as the output.
The bounding box of coordinates to be transformed, or null to consider the entire world extent.

remarks.para: If is null or , the returned transformation does not take into account an area of interest, and the best choice for the entire world extent is assumed.
Otherwise, if does not intersect the area of interest use , this method returns null.
If the of differs from , then is reprojected to .

returns.para: A instance that represents the best choice given the parameters. Always returns a usable transformation where is false. Returns null if no usable transformation is available for the given input parameters, or if no transformation is required.
Prior to version 100.9, this method returned a .

From version 100.9 onwards, if both and have a vertical coordinate system, this method returns a instance. If either does not have a vertical coordinate system, a is returned. To replicate the former behavior, use the method instead with 'ignoreVertical = true'.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.TransformationCatalog.GetTransformation(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Envelope,System.Boolean)
member.summary: Returns the best usable transformation used to transform between the input and output spatial references, taking into account the area of interest, if specified. Optionally disregards any vertical transformations.

remarks.para: Use this method to determine whether or not any vertical coordinate systems set on the spatial reference parameters should be accounted for in the returned transformation. If you set to false, this is equivalent to calling .
If is null or , the returned transformation does not take into account an area of interest, and the best choice for the entire world extent is assumed.
Otherwise, if does not intersect the area of use of , this method returns null.
If the of differs from , then is reprojected to .

member.param: The spatial reference to use as the input.
The spatial reference to use as the output.
The bounding box of coordinates to be transformed, or null to consider the entire world extent.
true if should ignore any vertical coordinate system set on the or , and only consider horizontal (geographic) transformations; false otherwise.
member.returns: A instance that represents the best choice given the parameters. Always returns a usable transformation where is false. member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.TransformationCatalog.GetTransformationsBySuitability(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.SpatialReference)
member.summary: Gets a list representing the datum transformations suitable for projecting between spatial references without taking into account the area of interest.

remarks.para: The list is ordered in descending order by suitability, with the most suitable being first in the list.

Some transformations require a supporting Projection Engine (PE) dataset to function correctly, If this API cannot locate these datasets, the transformation is not currently usable, and is true. The list may include transformations like this. To use such transformations, ensure is set correctly, and that the required dataset is available within that location. Use and to determine the dataset required for a specific transformation instance.

member.param: The to use as the input.
The to use as the output.

returns.para: A read-only list containing elements, each suitable for transforming between the given input and output spatial references.

If both and have a vertical coordinate system, this method returns a list of instances. If either spatial reference does not have a vertical coordinate system, a is returned. A geographic transformation is not needed when input and output spatial references have the same underlying geographic (horizontal) coordinate system. A vertical transformation is not needed if both datums (for ellipsoidal heights) or vertical datums (for gravity-related heights) are the same. If neither type of transformation is needed, an empty read-only list is returned.
To replicate the former behavior, use the method instead with ignoreVertical = true.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Unit
member.summary: Defines a base class for units of measurement.

remarks.para: The unit abstract class is a base class for measurement classes such as , , and . Many function parameters only accept specific types of measurement to reduce the chance of accidental use of inappropriate values, but some generic functions accept all unit types, such as for translation from ID to text description.
- Projected coordinate systems define coordinates using linear measurements, for example using meters or miles. They, are also used to return distance measurements, for example by some members of .
- Geographic coordinate systems define coordinates using angular measurements, for example using degrees or radians.
- Projected coordinate systems define area units for two dimensional measurements such as the area enclosed by a ring, for example in acres or square kilometers.

Linear, angular, and area units can be defined by using enumerations of the most common units of measurement. They can also be defined by well-known ID (WKID) or well-known text (WKText). Create the unit instances using the Unit base class or the subtype, passing in the enumeration for a specific unit of measurement.

Construction of related units are also supported so that if, for instance, the linear unit (e.g. METERS) is known, then the corresponding area unit (e.g. SQUARE_METERS) can be created based on the linear unit.
Custom implementations are not supported.

Each instance of the various units types has properties for the unit name (singular, plural, and abbreviated) and provides methods for unit conversion between different units of measurement in the same category of measurement.
All unit names and abbreviations are returned in the English language. Instances of unit are immutable.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.UtmConversionMode
member.summary: Determines how latitude is designated in UTM notation.
member.remarks: Within a single longitudinal zone within the UTM system, two points share the same grid position: one in the northern hemisphere and one in the south. Two schemes are used to resolve this ambiguity. In the first, the point is designated a latitude band, identified with letters C through X (omitting I and O). In the second, in place of the latitude band, a hemisphere indicator (N or S) is used.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.UtmConversionMode.LatitudeBandIndicators
member.summary: The letter after the UTM zone number represents a latitudinal band (C through X, omitting I and O).

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.UtmConversionMode.NorthSouthIndicators
member.summary: The letter after the UTM zone number represents a hemisphere (N or S).

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geotriggers.FeatureFenceParameters
member.summary: Geotrigger fence data created from features.

remarks.para: The features that create a provide a set of geometries (and attributes) that define targets for the . For example, if the condition is "notify me when my position comes within 50 meters of one of my target areas", the fence data are "my target areas" and each is one target area.
Create a using a . The features can have point, line, or polygon geometry. For points and lines, you must also supply a . Each feature in the table represents a single fence.
You can filter features using attributes or geometry by supplying a where clause. For example, you could further refine "my target areas" to be "my target areas that are open and are in California".
The number of features fetched by querying an online table is limited by the layer's . Service feature tables that support pagination () are not subject to the maximum feature count limit.

A can also take into account floors within buildings. If the feature table used to create the is referenced by a that is , and the used to create the includes information, then fences will only be entered when they are intersected by a on the correct floor. If the fence or location data does not include valid floor information, the floor is ignored and the fence is entered when it is intersected by the location.
A floor aware feature layer or table must be part of a that includes a valid . All layers and tables that are used by the floor definition are loaded when the is started.
A can contain floor property as part of . This is populated automatically by , or can be supplied as part of your own implementation.

A accounts for changes to the while the geotrigger is active. Added, removed, or updated fence features trigger a notification by the geotrigger monitor when the updated feature meets or no longer meets the geotrigger condition.

Updates to the occur in the following situations:
An edit (add, update, or delete) is submitted to the local feature table.
User interactions, like performing a pan or zoom in a map or scene, request updates from an online feature table () for the new visible extent.
If is set on a layer associated with the , feature fences are updated at the specified interval. For a , fence features requested from the server include features within the buffered visible extent as well as features near the current device location. This allows the geotrigger monitor to check fences both inside and outside of the current visible area.
Online feature fences are explicitly requested using . Use this method to control how and when feature fence updates are requested from a server. Setting the clearCache parameter of this method to true clears fence features from the local feature table before repopulating with updated features from the server. This ensures that deleted features are recognized and that the geotrigger monitor sends exit notifications.

Dynamic feature fence capabilities are enabled for , , and table types. They are disabled for all other table types and in the following circumstances:
has a set to .
is part of a that sets to . For feature fences from a , is recommended for highest accuracy.

When saving new geotriggers into a web map, ensure all feature layers are assigned a unique . This ensures the saved is associated with the corresponding layer. When the saved and are loaded, the layer and geotrigger will use the same .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geotriggers.FenceEnterExitSpatialRelationship
member.summary: The spatial relationship that determines when a fence has been entered or exited.

remarks.para: When determining whether a feed entity has entered or exited a fence polygon, there are two spatial operations that can be used:

list.item: Intersects, where the geometry of the feed must intersect the fence polygon.
Contains, where the geometry of the feed must be contained by the fence polygon.

remarks.para: These operations can be used to determine whether a fence polygon has been entered, and also when it has been exited. Containment is a stricter version of intersection (e.g. when a fence polygon contains a feed entity they also intersect) so will tend to result in fewer notifications when the two geometries are nearby. By using different combinations of these rules for enter and exit you can control the likelihood that notifications will be sent.

Note that when the feed geometry is a there is no difference between intersection and containment so these options will not affect behavior. When using a , set to in order to create a polygon geometry that accounts for the area of uncertainty around a .

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geotriggers.FenceEnterExitSpatialRelationship.EnterIntersectsAndExitDoesNotIntersect
member.summary: The fence polygon will be entered when it intersects the entity from a and exited when it no longer intersects.

remarks.para: This option will tend to increase the number of notifications sent as you approach a fence polygon. Use this option when you wish to receive all possible notifications - for example when you want to be warned that you are approaching a hazard.
This option will have no effect unless the feed entity geometry is a . Set to in order to create a polygon geometry that accounts for the area of uncertainty around a .
In the diagram below, the feed enters the fence on the left (shown in yellow) and exits on the right (shown in purple).

img.alt: The feed enters the fence on the left (shown in yellow) and exits on the right (shown in purple).

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geotriggers.FenceEnterExitSpatialRelationship.EnterContainsAndExitDoesNotContain
member.summary: The fence polygon will be entered when it contains the entity from a and exited when it no longer contains.

remarks.para: This option will limit notifications to situations where the entity is fully within the fence polygon. Use this option to be certain you are within a given fence - for example when carrying out work within a specific parcel.
This option will have no effect unless the feed entity geometry is a . Set to in order to create a polygon geometry that accounts for the area of uncertainty around a .
In the diagram below, the feed enters the fence on the left (shown in yellow) and exits on the right (shown in purple).

img.alt: The feed enters the fence on the left (shown in yellow) and exits on the right (shown in purple).

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geotriggers.FenceEnterExitSpatialRelationship.EnterContainsAndExitDoesNotIntersect
member.summary: The fence polygon will be entered when it contains the entity from a and exited when it no longer intersects.

remarks.para: This option will tend to minimize the number of notifications sent. The entity must be fully within the fence polygon before entering and must also be fully outside before exiting. This combination acts to reduce the number of spurious notifications that occur as you move around the border of a fence. Use this option when you wish to limit notifications - for example when driving past properties on your way to a delivery address.
This option will have no effect unless the feed entity geometry is a . Set to in order to create a polygon geometry that accounts for the area of uncertainty around a .
In the diagram below, the feed enters the fence on the left (shown in yellow) and exits on the right (shown in purple).

img.alt: The feed enters the fence on the left (shown in yellow) and exits on the right (shown in purple).

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geotriggers.FenceGeotrigger
member.summary: A that defines regions or fences to be monitored using a .

remarks.para: You can monitor areas of interest with a using spatial rules like enter or exit. This type of operation is also known as "Geofencing".
Three main components comprise a :

list.item: The that is monitored for changes - usually the device location read from a .
The that describes the check that is performed with the feed and fence data - for example, "enters within 50 meters of".
The that the feed data are evaluated against. For example, a set of polygon features representing areas of interest. The can cover many features or graphics that are checked.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geotriggers.FenceGeotrigger.FeedAccuracyMode
member.summary: Gets or sets the value that describes how a will use accuracy information from a .

remarks.para: This property controls how the accuracy information from a is used to provide the for checking a condition. In particular, you can choose how to use the property to reflect the uncertainty around a fixed . Due to factors including signal strength, GPS positions can be inaccurate, with a larger representing a larger area where the device may actually be located. This can lead to spurious notifications when the is inaccurate and is incorrectly reported as inside or outside of a fence. By using the you can choose to account for that wander in various ways.
This property is used in conjunction with to affect when notifications are sent.
The default value is .
Note that you can also use an with the property to reject objects with a poor accuracy.

member.value: A value that describes how a will use accuracy information from a .

For example, with a , using the following expression:
returns the "You have entered Area A", where "area_name" is an attribute field on the .

Optionally, the expression can return extra properties in addition to the message. For example, it could produce another string, suitable for logging to a database. To supply additional properties, write an arcade expression that evaluates to a dictionary of key / value pairs:

list.item: A value with the key "message" will be used to populate the property.
A value with the key "actions" will populate the actions property.
Any additional key / value pairs are returned in the dictionary.

remarks.para: For example, this expression results in a of: "Hello world" and a actions list of either "startTracking" or "stopTracking".
remarks.code: var action = IIf($fencenotificationtype == 'enter', 'stopTracking', 'startTracking') return { 'message': 'Hello world', 'actions': [ action ] }
remarks.para: The must evaluate to either a string or a dictionary; see . Note that this property is an object rather than a string.
This property may be null; if so, notifications sent by the geotrigger will pass a with evaluated to an empty string and an empty dictionary.
For more information on the available profile variables and functions for creating the , see the "Geotrigger Notification" profile at Profiles.

member.value: The for the .

list.item: Display the expected behavior of a to the user, prior to monitoring.
Check that your app supports the expected actions for the .

remarks.para: This list of strings shows the full set of recommended actions for any notifications from this . Individual events may include a subset of these actions. The specific actions for an individual notification event (for example, when a given fence is entered) are shown in . To populate the list of actions for a notification, the must return a dictionary with the key "actions", and a value showing the list of action strings.
The list of actions are simple strings, so you can use them to control any custom action you wish your app to perform.

When a is defined with a of type , you must supply a to provide your device location. The general workflow is as follows:
Get the from the property.
Load the to populate the list.
For each in the list, check whether the is of type .
Set the to your - for example, using the .
Create a for each you want to use.
Connect to notifications sent by the .
Call to begin monitoring.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geotriggers.GeotriggerMonitor
member.summary: A monitors a condition and, when it is met, results in a .

remarks.para: A defines a query that is evaluated against GIS data as it changes in real time. For example, "Notify me when my device location enters one of my target areas" or "Notify me when my device location is within 50 m of my destination".
A is created with a that it continually checks to determine whether the condition has been met.

Once a is created, you begin checking the condition by calling . Once you are finished, call . When a evaluates to true, a is sent. You can subscribe to the notification info to receive details of where the was activated and the condition that was met.

A can continue monitoring its condition when a mobile application is in the background. To enable this in an iOS or Android application, the following is recommended:
Use a with a based on the GPS of the device.
Add manifest entries and request user permissions to allow background location updates.

The uses processing time provided by device location updates to monitor the active geotrigger. For backgrounded applications, the timing of location updates (and therefore the timing of geotrigger notifications) is determined by the operating system. If a uses that reference a , and the table is associated with a that has a non-zero , the geotrigger monitor requests fence updates from the server on that interval even when the application is in the background.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geotriggers.GeotriggerMonitor.Warning
member.summary: Gets an error that describes a problem encountered while starting or monitoring the .

remarks.para: A warning can occur at various stages of monitoring a :

list.item: when starting the
when accessing the components of the such as the
when performing on-going monitoring

remarks.para: If the problem is resolved, this property will be cleared.

Problems that occur when starting the result in a of . These issues must be fixed and start called again before you receive any notifications. For example, if the is an invalid , monitoring cannot be carried out.

Problems that occur when accessing the components of the will not prevent the from starting. However, until the problem is resolved, you will not receive any notifications. For example, if a uses a that is not started, you will not receive any notifications. Once the is successfully started, monitoring will resume. If a has no valid data for a fence you will not receive any notifications.

Problems that occur during ongoing monitoring relate to issues with individual objects such as the graphics that make up the fences for a . These warnings do not prevent notifications from being sent for other valid fences. For example, a fence is considered invalid if it has:

list.item: an empty
a missing
a point or polyline and a buffer distance of 0

member.value: The current monitoring-related warning.

remarks.para: This property is consistent for all notifications for a given feed entity. For example, use this ID to count the number of sites a team member visits. If a feed is a , all location updates for that feed share the same ID.

With a , you can combine this property with the to create an ID that groups all notifications for a given feed entity and a specific fence. For example, use a concatenation of the two properties to display a list of areas of interest that are currently occupied and the details of who has entered them.
To group events by the geotrigger, use the property.

member.summary: Gets a set of strings describing recommended actions based on this notification.

remarks.para: Optionally, use these strings to determine whether your app will perform custom behavior, such as showing messages or starting/stopping tracking. Note that there is no requirement to take any action when a notification is delivered, but these provide a way to understand the intention of the author of the . This property can be empty.

These actions are the result of evaluating the for the specific objects that triggered this notification. If the expression returns a dictionary containing a list of strings with the key "actions", they are shown in this property.
For example, if the evaluates to a dictionary of containing the following key value pairs:

actions with a list of action strings startTracking,showMessage

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geotriggers.LocationGeotriggerFeed.Filter
member.summary: Gets or sets an that controls whether an update from the is sent to a .

remarks.para: The expression uses the Arcade language to determine whether feed entity updates are suitable for monitoring. The must evaluate to a boolean where:

list.item: true indicates that the update will be used.
false indicates that the update will not be used.

remarks.para: Filtering out undesirable updates reduces inaccurate notifications from your . For example, you could filter out GPS updates where the horizontal accuracy is poor, to avoid false enter and exit notifications.

With a , the expression inspects a new object and returns true if it is suitable and false if not. Typically, your expression excludes objects where the does not meet your criteria - but it could also consider other properties such as and .

Arcade gives you access to the properties of the feed entity with the $locationupdate profile variable. This represents a new received from your device GPS. For example, an with the following string rejects updates with a horizontal accuracy greater than 10 meters: return $locationupdate.horizontalaccuracy <= 10;

member.value: An that controls whether an update from the is sent to a ; or null if no filter is defined.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Graphic
member.summary: A type of that has a shape (geometry), symbol, and attributes and can be displayed in a map view or scene view.

remarks.para: Graphics are used to represent temporary data that exists for the lifetime of the application. For example, you can:

list.item: Show updated locations for objects in the map view, such as moving vehicles.
Display results from an analysis, geocoding, or routing operation.
Allow the user to draw temporary sketches on top of the map.
Store user input, such as a set of route stops to visit.
Show ad hoc text labels to describe things on the map.

remarks.para: To display a graphic, add it to a collection and add the graphics overlay to your map view or scene view.

Each graphic has a geometry describing its location and shape. To maximize performance, the geometry should have the same as the map view or scene view to which it is added. If the geometry has a different , it will be reprojected on-the-fly. This can be computationally expensive when displaying a large number of graphics.

A graphic can have an associated that defines the graphic's appearance. Alternatively, the can be assigned a that determines the appearance of all graphics in the overlay. If a graphic has an associated symbol, the symbol will override the overlay's renderer for the graphic's display. The symbol used depends on the geometry type associated with the graphic. A is used for and geometries, a for geometries, and a for or geometries.
You can select or deselect graphics (), change the visibility of a graphic (), and manage the order in which they are displayed ().
Each graphic can have a collection of attributes (as key-value pairs) to provide more information.

member.remarks: For graphics to appear in a map view or scene view, they must have a or be added to a graphics overlay that has a renderer assigned. A defines the properties used to display the graphic, such as color, outline, size, and style. There are many different types of symbols, such as , , and . The symbol type must match the of the graphic. For example, a is used with graphics that are based on polygon geometry. The rendering of a graphic's symbol takes precedence over the that may be set on the .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Graphic.ZIndex
member.summary: Gets or sets the draw order for graphics. Graphics with higher Z-index values are drawn on top of lower Z-index graphics.

remarks.para: A graphic's Z-index is mostly relevant for display in a two dimensional map. For dynamic 3D rendering (in a scene), graphic display order is determined by the distance to the camera rather than by Z-index. Z-index is considered, however, when using static rendering in a scene (draping graphics on the surface, in other words).

If Z-index is not set, graphics will usually render in the order in which they were added to the graphics overlay, the first added is rendered first and subsequent ones on top. In rare cases, the rendering algorithm may change the display order to provide more efficient rendering. If ordering is important, set the Z-index explicitly on graphics. You can also place graphics of the same geometry type in their own graphics overlay and manage the order of the overlays in the MapView or .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GraphicCollection
member.summary: An observable collection of .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GraphicsOverlay
member.summary: A collection of vector graphics for display in a map or scene view.

remarks.para: Graphics overlays displays a collection of graphics on top of all the layers (including reference layers) in a map view or scene view. Every map view or scene view can contain a collection of graphics overlays, and each graphics overlay can contain a collection of graphics (). The graphics in the first are drawn first, with subsequent on top. The defines the symbology of the collection.

A graphic's value defines its draw order within the graphics overlay that contains it. The value applies in a map view and a scene view if the scene view drapes the graphics on the surface (). In other scene view rendering, the graphic's display order is determined by the distance to the camera rather than by Z-index.

If the value is not set, graphics will render in the order in which they were added to the graphics overlay. The first added is rendered first and subsequent ones on top. In rare cases, the rendering algorithm may change the display order to provide more efficient rendering. If ordering is important, set the explicitly on graphics.
To improve view responsiveness, you can set the to either of these options:

list.item: - graphics are updated immediately as the is panned or zoomed (default).
- graphics are updated once the pan or zoom operation on the is finished. This is best for rendering large numbers of graphics.

If the overlay is not in a view, returns null.
If the overlay is in a view but that view does not yet have a spatial reference, returns null.
If the overlay is in a view with a spatial reference, but has no graphics, then an empty envelope will be returned.

remarks.para: For more efficient rendering of large numbers of graphics, set the rendering mode to . In order to ensure map responsiveness, static rendering does not refresh the display while pan and zoom operations are in progress. The amount of graphics an overlay can handle in this mode is mainly limited by the system memory.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GraphicsOverlay.ScaleSymbols
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether the overlay's symbols and labels honor the map reference scale when displayed in a map view.

remarks.para: If the map has a positive reference scale, and the overlay honors it, then symbols and labels will be drawn at their specified size when the viewing scale is the same as the reference scale, and will grow or shrink as the view zooms in or out, to keep the symbol a fixed size on the map. If the has no reference scale, the reference scale is zero or the graphic overlay's ScaleSymbols property is false, then the symbols and labels will be drawn at their fixed screen size. The default value is false.
Scale symbols only affects display in a MapView. The symbols and labels will be displayed at fixed screen size in a .

member.value: true if the graphic overlay's symbols and labels honor the map reference scale when displayed in a map view, false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GraphicsOverlay.MinScale
member.summary: Gets or sets the minimum scale to render this layer at. A large number allows the map to display the layer when zooming further out.
member.value: The minimum scale at which to display the graphics overlay. A large number allows the map to display the layer when zooming further out.

remarks.para: This controls the minimum scale at which the graphics overlay is displayed. If the current map scale is larger than the minimum scale, then this graphics overlay will not be displayed.

A scale is usually referred to as 1:X, where X is the scale specified here. This value is the relative scale to the real world, where one inch on the screen is X inches in the real world. Note that this is only an approximation and is dependent on the map's projection that can add some distortion, as well as the system's reported DPI setting which doesn't necessarily match the actual DPI of the screen.
The default value of this property is which makes the layer unbounded by any scale.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GraphicsOverlay.MaxScale
member.summary: Gets or sets the maximum scale to display this layer at. A small number allows the map to display the layer when zooming further in.
member.value: The maximum scale at which to display the graphics overlay. A small number allows the map to display the layer when zooming further in.

remarks.para: This controls the maximum scale at which the graphics overlay is displayed. If the current map scale is smaller than the maximum scale, then this graphics overlay will not be displayed.

A scale is usually referred to as 1:X, where X is the scale specified here. This value is the relative scale to the real world, where one inch on the screen is X inches in the real world. Note that this is only an approximation and is dependent on the map's projection that can add some distortion, as well as the system's reported DPI setting which doesn't necessarily match the actual DPI of the screen.
The default value of this property is which makes the layer unbounded by any scale.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GraphicsOverlay.Graphics
member.summary: Gets the graphics collection.

remarks.para: If Z-index for graphics is not set, they will usually render in the order in which they were added to the graphics overlay, the first added is rendered first and subsequent ones on top. In rare cases, the rendering algorithm may change the display order to provide more efficient rendering. If ordering is important, set the explicitly on graphics. You can also place graphics of the same geometry type in their own graphics overlay and manage the order of the overlays in the view.
The graphics property is initially set (not null).

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay
member.summary: Defines how location is displayed on a MapView.

remarks.para: Location display can retrieve the device's current location from a and display it on top of a . As the device moves, the map view can respond to new locations by updating the position of the location symbol on the display. It can also set the viewpoint when an updated location is received, or automatically pan and rotate the map when the current location, heading, or course changes.

Each MapView has its own instance of so you don't have to explicitly instantiate the location display. The location information is provided by a . The current position of the device is provided by default from the operating system. To start receiving location updates, and display the current location, you must start the location display's data source.

Apart from the device operating system's built-in location, you can also display locations from other sources, including external (high accuracy) GPS, an indoor positioning system (IPS), or simulated locations (for mocking location updates) using the following location data sources:

By default, the location display uses a blue, round symbol to show the current location. Depending on signal strength, satellite positions, and other factors, the accuracy of the reported location can vary. An additional blue circle () around the location symbol indicates the estimated range of accuracy for the current location. As locations are read from the configured , this outer circle may contract and expand as accuracy increases or decreases. You can display further information about the location using the following symbols:

You can change the default symbology of these symbols to make them more visible against the underlying map cartography, increase their visibility for visually impaired users, or match an app's visual appearance.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.Location
member.summary: Gets the most recent location reported by the location data source in the projection of the current map view.
member.value: The most recent location reported by the location data source in the projection of the current map view.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.MapLocation

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.IsEnabled
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether the location display and its datasource are enabled.

remarks.para: If the location display fails to start the , this property will automatically turn off again. You can also use the property to check for an active location display, which will not become true until the has successfully started to run.
If you need more detail why a location datasource failed to start, use the method instead which will return an error, if it could not be started.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.Heading
member.summary: Gets the heading angle of the current location in degrees relative to north.
member.remarks: This is only applicable when the auto-pan mode is . The value 0 means the device is pointed toward magnetic north, 90 means it is pointed due east, 180 means it is pointed due south, and so on. A negative value indicates that the heading could not be determined.
member.value: The heading angle of the current location in degrees relative to north.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.NavigationPointHeightFactor
member.summary: Gets or sets the navigation point height factor.

remarks.para: The factor will decide the location symbol position vertically from the bottom of the map when the auto-pan mode is . When navigating in a map, the current location is typically vertically positioned near the bottom of the map, and horizontally centered in the map, so that the map displays as much of the route ahead as possible.

A value of 0 implies the location symbol should be positioned at the bottom edge of the mapview, and 1 implies the top edge. A value between 0 and 1 positions the symbol somewhere along the center line from the bottom edge to the top edge. Default is 0.125 (i.e 1/8).

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.InitialZoomScale
member.summary: Gets or sets the scale that the map should automatically be zoomed to upon receiving the first location update.
member.remarks: The initial map scale to which the will zoom when the first location update is received after the auto-pan mode is changed from to any other value. The default value is 10,000.
member.value: The scale that the map should automatically be zoomed to upon receiving the first location update.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.WanderExtentFactor
member.summary: Gets or sets the wander extent factor controls the re-centering behavior of the MapView when the location changes.
member.remarks: The proportion of the current extent within which the location can be without automatically panning the map. Permitted values are in the range between 0 (continuous panning) and 1 (only pan when the location reaches the edge of the current extent). This value only applies when the auto-pan mode is . Lower values within this range will cause the map to re-center more often, leading to higher CPU and battery consumption.
member.value: The wander extent factor controls the re-centering behavior of the MapView when the location changes.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.DefaultSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used for location when not moving.
member.remarks: Changing the location symbols is typically done if the existing symbols do not work well with the underlying map cartography, to increase visibility for visually impaired users, or to match an apps visual appearance.
member.value: The default symbol.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.AccuracySymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the fill symbol indicating how accurate the current location is.

remarks.para: The accuracy of the location can vary depending on signal strength, satellite positions, and other factors. This circle may contract and expand as the accuracy of the locations, read from the , increases or decreases.
The default symbol is a blue outline with a semi-transparent white fill.

member.value: A symbol used to represent location accuracy.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.ShowAccuracy
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether to display the accuracy of the current location.
member.value: true is the accuracy circle should be displayed, false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.CourseSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to display the current location and the course (direction of travel) when moving.
member.remarks: This symbol rotates with the heading of the movement. If course information is not available (for example if the device is not moving), the is used.
member.value: The symbol used to display the current location and the course (direction of travel) when moving.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.UseCourseSymbolOnMovement
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether the is used to display current location, when the location updates indicate the device is moving.
member.remarks: If no course information is available, the default symbol will be used instead. The is rotated in order to indicate the device's direction of travel. Generally, course information is only available if the device is actively moving (the location has a positive speed).
member.value: true if the course symbol should be used while moving; false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.HeadingSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used for the location when the auto-pan mode is .
member.remarks: The symbol is rotated to match with the heading of the compass. If there is no heading information (for example, if the device does not have a compass sensor), the is used.
member.value: The symbol used for the location when the auto-pan mode is .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.AcquiringSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to display the last known location while the current location is being acquired.
member.remarks: The symbol will be shown until a new location fix is found. This symbol may be used immediately after starting the . Once an up-to-date location is acquired, the , , or is used, depending on the current settings of the . The default symbol is a grey circle.
member.value: The symbol used to display the last known location while the current location is being acquired.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.AutoPanMode
member.summary: Gets or sets the auto pan mode which determines how location changes affect the current viewpoint.
member.value: The auto pan mode.

remarks.para: The different auto-pan modes are suited for different types of app, including modes designed for navigation on foot and in a vehicle. They affect the symbols used to display device location, the initial zooming behavior, and the panning behavior of the MapView.

The auto-pan modes include:
Recenter - the map view attempts to keep the location symbol on-screen by re-centering the location symbol when the symbol moves outside an extent defined by the .
Navigation - the location symbol is pinned to a point on the screen and always points toward the top edge of the device. The map pans and rotates based on location updates and the direction of travel.
Compass navigation - the location symbol is pinned to a point on the screen and always points toward the top edge of the device. The map rotates based on the user's bearing relative to magnetic north.
Off - the map only updates the position of the location symbol on the map.
To start the in a specific mode, call this method before you start the .
Any default zooming behavior (besides scrolling) or any operation that modifies the will automatically set the auto-pan mode to .
The default auto-pan mode is .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.Opacity
member.summary: Gets or sets the opacity value of all symbols displayed by the . The value should be between 0 and 1.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.DataSource
member.summary: Gets or sets the location data source used for generating location updates.

remarks.para: uses a location data source to provide the current position of the device. To start receiving location updates, and display the current location, you must start the location data source.
By default the location data source obtains locations from the device's operating system.
Alternative location data sources include:

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.ShowLocation
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether the location is shown on the MapView this instance is associated with.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.ShowPingAnimationSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether the ping animation is shown.
member.value: true if the ping animation is shown; otherwise, false.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplay.PingAnimationSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol that will be animated around the location symbol, to indicate when location updates are received.
member.remarks: Location update animations are only shown when is true.
member.value: The symbol that will be animated around the location symbol, to indicate when location updates are received.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplayAutoPanMode
member.summary: An enumeration of the various modes that define how the GeoView viewpoint reacts to location changes.
member.remarks: If the visible map extent is changed by the user navigating the map interactively, or by a programmatic navigation method, this will cause to be reset to . However, if the user double-taps or pinches to zoom the map only, the remains as while the map is zoomed in or out, allowing users to easily zoom the map while in this mode.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplayAutoPanMode.Off
member.summary: Displays the location symbol at the current location without adjusting the map view's extent, so no auto-panning is performed. The location symbol may move off screen.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplayAutoPanMode.Recenter
member.summary: Centers the map view at the current location, and shows the location symbol. When the current location changes, the map view is automatically panned to re-center at the new location. The behavior is affected by the wander extent factor.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplayAutoPanMode.Navigation
member.summary: Pans the map view so that the current location symbol is shown near the bottom of the map view, and rotates the map view to align it with the direction of travel. When the current location or direction of travel changes the map view is automatically panned and rotated to maintain this position. To use this mode effectively, the device location must be moving at a speed greater than 0 meters per second. The behavior is affected by the navigation point height factor.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LocationDisplayAutoPanMode.CompassNavigation
member.summary: Centers the map view at the current location, rotates the map view to align with the direction in which the device is currently, and shows the location symbol. When the current location or position of the device changes, the map view is automatically panned and rotated to maintain this position, thus if the device is spun in a circle, the map view stays aligned with the real world.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.AnimationCurve
member.summary: Control how the view is moved between positions.
member.remarks: Controls how the map view is animated. To play with the easing functions, see the document located at: easing.

member.summary: Move linear.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.AnimationCurve.EaseInQuad
member.summary: Move in quadratically.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.AnimationCurve.EaseOutQuad
member.summary: Move out quadratically.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.AnimationCurve.EaseInOutQuad
member.summary: Move in and out quadratically.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.AnimationCurve.EaseInCubic
member.summary: Move in cubically.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.CameraController
member.summary: A camera controller manages the position, orientation, and movement of a in a .

remarks.para:
This is a base class for a number of camera controllers. Each type of camera controller is designed to provide a specific user experience for interacting with the scene display. The camera controller and its properties can be changed at run time, so you can provide the scene interaction experience best suited for the current context. The following are examples of camera controllers:
(default) — Provides the default scene view camera behavior. Allows the user to freely move and focus the camera anywhere in the scene.
— Locks the scene view's camera to maintain focus on a (possibly moving) . The user can navigate around and zoom in/out on the specific object.
— Locks the scene view's camera to orbit a fixed point location. The camera can only move relative to this point location.
When a camera controller other than is active, the scene view's viewpoint cannot be assigned. Attempts to do so are ignored.

member.name: E:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.CameraController.PropertyChanged
member.summary: Occurs when a property value changes.

member.summary: The device is in landscape mode, with the device held sideways and the home button on the left side.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.DeviceOrientation.LandscapeRight
member.summary: The device is in landscape mode, with the device held sideways and the home button on the right side.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor
member.summary: Allows you to create new geometries, and change existing geometries, by interacting with a MapView.

remarks.para:
To use a :
Set the property.
Call one of the start methods.
To interactively create new geometries from scratch, call passing in a to determine the type of geometry created.
For geometry editing workflows, pass an existing into .
You can then edit the geometry directly on the map view using mouse or touch gestures.
The determines how you draw on the map to define the new geometry, for example by adding individual vertices or with a freehand gesture. The default tool is .
If you passed in an initial geometry, it is displayed in the view, and you can interact with the geometry to make changes.
Call to return the geometry and prevent the geometry editor from processing any more interactions.
Geometries are immutable, therefore a new object reflecting the updates is returned from and each time.
When started ( is true), you can interact with the different objects that compose the representation of the in the map view. Depending on what is selected, different types of are returned from .
The type of the current determines how you create and edit geometries, with the current or allowing you to enable or disable different interactions and capabilities of the tool.

list.item: The default allows you to create and edit geometries by interacting with individual vertices. You can tap to select the different elements and drag to move them, and also tap multiple times on an element to select the different types of representing vertices, parts, or the entire geometry. You can rotate and scale the selected element (if applicable to the selected element type), and can also create new parts in a multipart geometry (if enabled in the ).
The is ideal for creating new polygon or polyline geometries using a single freehand drag gesture. It also allows multiple taps to cycle the selected element type.
The is ideal for creating new polygon or polyline geometries with a geometric shape by using a single drag interaction. It also allows multiple taps to cycle the selected element type.
The is recommended for precise, vertex-based, geometry editing on touch devices, especially when using snapping (). It does not support multiple taps to cycle the selected element.

remarks.para: Programmatic changes to the or (for example calling or ) not affected by the settings in or .
Change the appearance of the geometry by using the of the current - for example to match the symbology used by the graphic or feature being edited.

Enabling snapping can help ensure that interactive edits are more accurate, with fewer errors, by allowing you to create geometries that connect to each other and are coincident. Use to configure snapping behavior. When editing the geometry of features that participate in a utility network, configure rule-based snapping to help ensure snapping behavior respects the geometric connectivity of the utility network for the specific feature type being edited. See for more information.

Programmatic editing methods are also provided so you can augment your interactive geometry editing to support specific user requirements. For example:
Use to add a subsequent vertex based on a GPS location from .
Use to delete the current .
Use to move the currently selected to a specific , or to nudge it in a specific direction.
Use to take the current , apply an operation such as a union or intersect by using , and then replace the current geometry with the result so you can continue to edit as required.
Use and to undo and redo individual interactive or programmatic edits.
Use event to be notified when a user interaction or API call has changed the or . Changing the geometry state can also change , which also triggers .

The value of changes, raising the event, when:

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.Geometry
member.summary: Gets the current geometry, updated as you interact with the view.

remarks.para:
The geometry returned depends on the state of the :
Returns null while is false.
Returns an empty geometry ( is true) immediately after the was started using .
Returns the input geometry immediately after the was started using .
Returns the current geometry while the is in use ( is true).

If the was started by calling , then the geometry is returned in the same as the input geometry. If the input geometry spatial reference is null, or the editor was started using , then the geometry is returned in the spatial reference of the associated MapView. If no spatial reference is set on either input geometry or via an associated map view, then the spatial reference of the geometry will be null.

Editing feature data that is reprojected on the fly can introduce spatial errors into the data. When accuracy is important, best practice is to use a spatial reference for the map view which matches the feature layer geometry you are editing. The ArcGIS Pro editing help topic Introduction to projection on the fly contains further advice.

The value of changes, raising the event, when:
starting or stopping the geometry editor - for example using or
performing programmatic edits - for example using or
performing interactive edits - for example adding or moving a vertex
undoing or redoing edits - using or

member.value: The current geometry, updated as you interact with the view.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.SelectedElement
member.summary: Gets the element that is currently selected in the , or null if nothing is selected.

remarks.para: Clicking or tapping on the different visible elements of a in a view allows you to select and unselect them. This property returns a different type of depending on what is selected - a vertex (), a mid-vertex (), a part of a polygon or polyline (), or the entire geometry (). You can change which selection interactions are allowed by using the of the .
You can also programmatically select specific vertices (), mid-vertices (), parts of polygons or polylines (), or the entire geometry (). You can clear the selection using .

The value of changes, raising the event, when:
performing programmatic selection - for example using or
performing interactive selection - for example selecting or unselecting a
the location of the selected element changes - for example moving the selected element programmatically or interactively
performing edits - for example inserting a vertex interactively or deleting the selected element programmatically

It may also change when:
undoing or redoing edits - using or
stopping the geometry editor using

member.value: The element that is currently selected in the , or null if nothing is selected.

member.remarks: Snapping allows you to create geometries that connect to each other and are coincident, so that interactive edits are more accurate, with fewer errors. Snapping is disabled by default - see for information about setting up snapping workflows.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.InsertVertex(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint)
member.summary: Creates a new vertex at the specified location and inserts it after the currently selected element, or appends the point if there is nothing selected.

remarks.para: If is empty ( is true), the given point is added as the initial vertex, and then selected. If nothing is selected ( is null), the point is appended to the end of the , and then selected. If is the first vertex in a part of a , the given point is inserted before the currently selected vertex, and then selected.
If is z-aware ( = true), this method will change the to be z-aware, if it is not already.
If is a non-empty , this method replaces the existing point with the given point and selects it.

member.param: The location of the new vertex.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.MoveSelectedElement(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint)
member.summary: Moves the selected element to the specified location.

remarks.para: If is a , then the location of the vertex is moved to the given point. If the is any other type of , then the center of the element is moved to the given point. If is z-aware ( = true) and is not currently z-aware, this will change the to be z-aware, if it is not already.
If is z-aware and is not z-aware, then the current z-value(s) of will remain unchanged.

member.param: The new location for the selected element.

member.summary: Moves the selected element by the specified deltas.

The amount to move the selected element along the y-axis, in the units of the associated MapView spatial reference.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.ReplaceGeometry(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Geometry)
member.summary: Replaces the current with the specified geometry.

remarks.para: Use this method to make changes to the that are not directly supported using other methods. For example, to start a new part in a or geometry, get the current , use a to add the required part, and then pass the updated to this method.
The is replaced by the given geometry, and therefore the z-awareness of will be the same as the given geometry.

member.param: The new geometry to replace the current geometry. The of these geometries must match.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.SelectGeometry
member.summary: Selects the entire .

remarks.para: When the entire geometry is selected it can be edited interactively and programmatically. For example you can move the entire geometry interactively by dragging, or call or to move it programmatically.

When the entire geometry is selected, the returned from has a geometry equal to . If the is a , then a is returned from instead, which is equivalent to the entire geometry and provides immediate access to the x,y coordinates of the selected point geometry.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.SelectMidVertex(System.Int64,System.Int64)
member.summary: Selects the mid-vertex with the specified indices.

remarks.para: Mid-vertices do not exist in the . They exist only in the display to serve as a visual cue that new vertices can be inserted between existing vertices. Since they do not exist in the geometry, mid-vertices cannot be programmatically moved or deleted. They can be used to insert a new vertex interactively.
When a mid-vertex is selected, a is returned from .

member.param: The index of the part in the geometry editor's geometry that contains the mid-vertex to be selected. Use a value of 0 for non-multipart geometry types - and .
The index of segment that the mid-vertex to be selected lies on, within the given part of the geometry editor's geometry, or within the geometry. Use a value of 0 for geometries.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.SelectPart(System.Int64)
member.summary: Selects the part with the specified index.

remarks.para: When a part of a polygon or polyline geometry is selected, a is returned from . If the polygon or polyline has only one , then a with a shape equivalent to the entire geometry is returned from instead, which provides immediate access to the extent () and can be directly used in other geometry operations. If the current is a or , the entire geometry is selected.

When a part of a or () geometry is selected it can be edited separately from the other parts of the geometry. For example, you can move only the selected part interactively by dragging, or call or to move only the selected part programmatically.

member.param: The index of the part in the geometry editor's geometry to be selected. Use a value of 0 for geometries.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.Start(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Geometry)
member.summary: Starts a geometry editing session based on the specified geometry.

remarks.para: Use this method for data editing workflows, where you have an existing to be updated. After calling this method, the geometry is drawn in the map view, and interactions with the map view will select and edit the geometry, depending on the current and its .

When the of the initial geometry differs from that of the map view, the display will project the geometry to the of the map view, using the default , for display and user interactions. Editing feature data that is reprojected on the fly can introduce spatial errors into data. When accuracy is important, best practice is to use a spatial reference for the map view which matches the feature layer geometry you are editing. The ArcGIS Pro editing help topic Introduction to projection on the fly contains further advice.

member.param: The initial to start the editing session with.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.Start(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.GeometryType)
member.summary: Starts a geometry editing session with an empty geometry of the specified .

remarks.para: Use this method for data creation workflows, where you know the required but do not have an existing geometry to edit. After calling this method, nothing is drawn on the map view initially - user interactions on the map view will define and edit the geometry, depending on the current .
can be used to create , , , and geometries - however , and are suitable for creating , and geometries only.

The new geometry will have the of the map view. You can use or to project the final result to a different spatial reference if required, for example when editing feature geometry. When accuracy is important, best practice is to use a spatial reference for the map view which matches the feature layer geometry you are creating. The ArcGIS Pro editing help topic Introduction to projection on the fly contains further advice.

member.param: The of geometry to edit.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.Stop
member.summary: Stops the editing session by making the no longer respond to user interaction and clears the .
member.returns: The final before it is cleared. null if the geometry editor is not started.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.Undo
member.summary: Undoes the last action on the .

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.RotateSelectedElement(System.Double,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint)
member.summary: Rotates the by the specified angle.

remarks.para: The angle of rotation is used in the form of the modulo of 360 degrees; for example rotating by 540 degrees is equivalent to rotating the by 180 degrees. A positive value corresponds to a counterclockwise rotation.
If the origin has a different than the associated MapView, the point will be reprojected before the geometry is rotated, using the default transformation.
Any z-value of the is ignored. only supports rotations in the xy-plane.
If is a , or a of type , and the origin parameter is null (or is at the same location as the selected element), then rotating the selected element has no effect.

member.param: The angle by which to rotate the selected element, counterclockwise, in degrees.
The center of rotation. If null, the center of the extent of the is used.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditor.ScaleSelectedElement(System.Double,System.Double,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint)
member.summary: Scales the selected element by the specified factors.

remarks.para: If the origin has a different than the associated MapView, the point will be reprojected before the geometry is rotated, using the default transformation.
Any z-value of the is ignored. only supports scaling the xy-dimensions.

Positive scale factors greater than 1 increase the size of the , and positive factors between 0 and 1 reduce the size of the geometry. Negative scale factors produce a geometry reflected across the axes of the origin point. Negative factors less than -1 both reflect and increase the size of the geometry, and negative factors between -1 and 0 both reflect and reduce the size of the geometry.

member.param: The scale factor along the x-axis. It can be positive or negative. It cannot be 0, or a non-numeric value.
The scale factor along the y-axis. It can be positive or negative. It cannot be 0, or a non-numeric value.
The point relative to which the geometry will be scaled. If null, or is true, the center of the extent of the is used.

member.summary: The element in a representing the entire .

remarks.para: A represents the entire geometry in a - it displays the existing location and shape of the geometry, and allows you to select and move the entire geometry in interactive workflows. For more information about selecting and working with selections, see .
When an entire geometry is selected (displayed with a selection halo), a is returned from , except if the is a , in which case a is returned instead.
Select the entire geometry programmatically using . To prevent whole geometries from being selected interactively while still allowing programmatic selection, set to false.
Use and to change the appearance of polygons and polylines displayed by a . You may also wish to control the appearance of a geometry during interactive edits by using and .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorMidVertex
member.summary: A mid-vertex element in a .

remarks.para: Mid-vertices do not exist in the , but instead exist only in the display. They are displayed between vertices in polygon and polyline geometries and are used in interactive workflows as a visual cue to indicate new vertices can be inserted between existing vertices. They may be most helpful for users with less editing experience. Tap on a mid-vertex to select it, and drag to change the mid-vertex into a vertex () in the and move it to the required location.
When a mid-vertex is selected (displayed with a selection halo), a is returned from . Select a mid-vertex programmatically using .

Use and to change the appearance of the mid-vertex when unselected and selected, or set to null to prevent mid-vertices from being displayed entirely. If mid-vertices are not displayed, you can still insert vertices between existing vertices by selecting an existing and tapping on the map at the location to insert the new vertex.

To prevent mid-vertices from being selected interactively while still allowing programmatic selection, set to false. If is false then new vertices cannot be inserted into the geometry, including by the use of mid-vertices.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorStyle
member.summary: Defines the visual appearance of a geometry displayed by the .

remarks.para: Each has a which controls the appearance of the different elements of the . You can change the symbology used for vertices, selections, lines and fills. For example, in a or editing workflow, you can set the to match the symbol of a polyline geometry in order to provide visual consistency.
When a is created, a consistent set of default symbology appropriate for that tool type is applied to its property.

You can change the of the overall style, or alternatively set different opacity (alpha) values on the individual symbols of the style, so that the underlying map components are visible through the geometry being edited.
You can set each symbol to null - consider carefully how this can affect your workflows, as you will not be able to see, select, or otherwise interact with those types.
When snapping is enabled (), the visual cues that indicate to a user when snapping has occurred can be customized using , , and .

member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class with a consistent set of red symbols, and displays white numbers on vertices.
member.remarks: Using this constructor results in the same default symbology as the .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorStyle.FeedbackLineSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to draw the edges currently being changed by an interactive edit, when editing polyline or polygon geometries.

remarks.para: During an interactive edit, this symbol displays the current location of edges (polylines, or boundaries of polygons) that are changed by the interaction in progress. Unchanged lines remain displayed with .
The default value is a dotted red .

member.value: The symbol used to draw the edges currently being changed by an interactive edit, when editing polyline or polygon geometries.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorStyle.FeedbackVertexSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to draw vertices currently being changed by an interactive edit, when editing any geometry type.

remarks.para: During an interactive edit, this symbol displays the current location of vertices that are changed by the interaction in progress. Unchanged vertices remain displayed with .

Vertex numbers are not displayed for the changed vertices. For most tools, the default value is the same as the default . For a , the default value is similar to the default but a larger size, helping you distinguish when you have started a move.

member.value: The symbol used to draw vertices currently being changed by an interactive edit, when editing any geometry type.

member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to fill the current geometry when editing polygon geometries.
member.remarks: The default value is a red semi-transparent with a red outline.
member.value: The symbol used to fill the current geometry when editing polygon geometries.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorStyle.LineSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to draw the current geometry when editing polyline geometries, and the outline of the geometry when editing polygon geometries.
member.remarks: The default value is a solid red .
member.value: The symbol used to draw the current geometry when editing polyline geometries, and the outline of the geometry when editing polygon geometries.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorStyle.MidVertexSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to draw the mid-vertices of the current geometry when editing polygon or polyline geometries.

remarks.para: Mid-vertices do not exist in the , but instead exist only in the display, located between vertices in polygon and polyline geometries as a visual cue for interactive editing. When moved interactively, a new vertex is inserted at that .
If null, no mid-vertex is displayed. In this case, you can still insert vertices between existing vertices by selecting an existing and tapping on the map.

member.value: The symbol used to draw the mid-vertices of the current geometry when editing polygon or polyline geometries.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorStyle.MultipleSnapCandidatesSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to indicate that multiple candidates for snapping exist at the interactive edit position, when the has snapped the interaction position to the best candidate.

remarks.para: This symbol may indicate, for example, an intersection of any two different geometries, two very close geometries, the presence of multiple vertices, or a self-intersection of a geometry. The and/or will also be displayed when this symbols is displayed.
The default value is a marker symbol showing an orange ring.

member.value: The symbol used to indicate that multiple candidates for snapping exist at the interactive edit position, when the has snapped the interaction position to the best candidate.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorStyle.Opacity
member.summary: Gets or sets the opacity of the display in the view, a value between 0 (fully transparent) and 1 (fully opaque). Default is 1.

remarks.para: It can be useful to change opacity of the entire display to allow features, graphics, or imagery in the underlying map view to show through the geometry editor display, which can help allow precise placement of new vertices relative to those map components.
Alternatively, you can set opacity separately on each of the symbols used by this style, allowing you to make specific aspects of the geometry editor semi-transparent.

member.value: The opacity of the display in the view, a value between 0 (fully transparent) and 1 (fully opaque). Default is 1.
