member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to draw the currently selected mid-vertex of the current geometry when editing polygon or polyline geometries.

remarks.para: When a mid-vertex is selected, it is displayed with this symbol instead of .
As soon as a mid-vertex is moved interactively, it is replaced by a vertex that is part of the , and is displayed with the .

member.value: The symbol used to draw the currently selected mid-vertex of the current geometry when editing polygon or polyline geometries.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorStyle.SelectedVertexSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to draw the currently selected vertex of the current geometry when editing any geometry type.
member.remarks: This symbol can be used to emphasize the currently selected vertex, which can be useful if you require extra emphasis in addition to the selection halo, or wish to make the selected vertex a larger target for user interactions.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorStyle.SnappedToLineSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to indicate an edge (segment) of a geometry during an interactive edit, when the has snapped the interaction position to that edge.

remarks.para: This symbol is shown when an edge (segment) is the best candidate to snap the edit position to. It may be shown in addition to , because the end of a segment effectively shares the same position as the vertices that define the start and end of that segment.
The default value is a solid orange line.

member.value: The symbol used to indicate an edge (segment) of a geometry during an interactive edit, when the has snapped the interaction position to that edge.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorStyle.SnappedToVertexSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to indicate a vertex of a geometry during an interactive edit, when the has snapped the interaction position to that vertex.

remarks.para: This symbol is shown when a vertex is the best candidate to snap the edit position to. When the vertex being edited has been snapped to a vertex, the is also adjusted to this position, which may differ slightly from the (unsnapped) pointer position. This symbol is drawn on top of the .
The default value is an orange circle marker symbol.

member.value: The symbol used to indicate a vertex of a geometry during an interactive edit, when the has snapped the interaction position to that vertex.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorStyle.VertexSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets the symbol used to draw the vertices of the current geometry when editing any geometry type.

remarks.para: Setting a larger symbol can help users on touch devices select and work with vertices, especially for field apps where users may wear thick gloves that work with touch screens.

If null, the vertices of the geometry are not explicitly displayed. By default when using a , this is null, as you work with geometry parts instead of individual vertices. For a or , consider your workflow carefully before setting this property to null, as you must be able to see vertices in order to select, move, or otherwise interact with them.

member.value: The symbol used to draw the vertices of the current geometry when editing any geometry type.

Control handles for interactively scaling the element are drawn upon the bounding box (see ).
The default value is a dashed black . Use either a , a , or an appropriate advanced symbol if setting this property.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.GeometryEditorTool
member.summary: A base class for tools that determine how you interact with a view when editing geometries with a .

remarks.para: Different types of provide different ways of interacting with the view to create and edit geometries with a . They control how an interaction (for example a tap or a drag gesture on the map view) uses the interaction position to update the , or to navigate the map view. and are both types of tools that allow you to customize how the reacts to each user interaction, allowing you to tailor the exact behavior appropriately for your users and workflow. also determines the appearance of the by specifying the used to draw the geometry in the view.

You may wish to create multiple different tool objects in order to hold different settings, and set them into as appropriate for your workflow. For example, you could create multiple objects with values corresponding the different symbols used by different feature layers.

The different tools do not correspond to different geometry types - for example the can be used to edit , , and geometries. The type of geometry being edited using the tool is defined by the start method parameter (see and .
When a is created, a consistent set of default symbology appropriate for that tool type is applied to the property.

member.remarks: When a is created, a consistent set of default symbology appropriate for that tool type is applied to its property. For example, the defines all vertex symbols as null by default because this tool uses drag gestures to define entire parts of polyline and polygon geometries, whereas the defines non-null vertex symbols because it is important to be able to see and interact with individual vertices when editing geometries with this tool.
member.value: Defines the visual appearance of geometries when this tool is in use.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.InteractionConfiguration
member.summary: Defines the set of interactive operations you can enable or disable for a or .

remarks.para:
An allows you to customize the interactive geometry editing experience to suit your specific users. For example:
Disable vertex, mid-vertex, and part selection (, , ) to define a tool to only move the complete geometry.
Disable moving the selected element () but allow all selections, and provide a tool for a user to enter new coordinates instead of moving interactively.

You can tap multiple times on any to select the different types of representing vertices, parts, and the entire geometry. For example if all elements are allowed to be selected:
tap twice on a vertex of a to select first the vertex and then the entire multipoint geometry
tap three times on a vertex of a or to select first the vertex, then the part, and then the entire geometry
tap twice on an edge of a or to select first the part and then the entire geometry
tap twice on a fill of a to select first the part and then the entire geometry

You can change the interaction properties of a tool at any point, including when a geometry editor is started ( = true) and the tool is in use by the editor. If you change a property that disallows an in-progress interaction, then the interaction is cancelled and does not affect the state of the .

Convenience methods are available to quickly set all properties relative to a category of interaction - for example use to change all selection interactions, and to change all properties that control geometry transformation.

Users less experienced with GIS data may not understand how visually separate parts of polygons and polylines form a single geometry. You may wish to prevent such users from inadvertently creating geometries with multiple parts in order to keep their workflows straightforward and understandable. remarks.para: This property applies when the is:

list.item: a when is a , a , or a
a

remarks.para: For example, this does not apply if the selected element is a because a zero-dimensional geometry cannot be rotated.

When this property is true, and the is rotatable, then a control handle is displayed (see ) that allows the selected element to be interactively rotated. The bounding box of the selected element is also displayed ().

Interactive rotation changes the position of the selected element according to the movement of the rotation handle using the center of the extent of the selected element as the center of rotation (origin). Use to rotate the selected element around a given origin.

When this property is true, and the is scalable, then control handles are displayed (see ) that allow the selected element to be interactively scaled. The bounding box of the selected element is also displayed ().

Interactive scaling changes the extent of the selected element according to the movement of the scale handle being moved - the opposite handle is used as the origin of the scale transformation. Use to scale the selected element relative to a given origin.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.ISnapSource
member.summary: Indicates a source of geometries that a could snap interactive edits to.

remarks.para: is implemented by objects that can provide a source of geometries to snap interactive edits to when using the . For example, a feature layer or graphics overlay can provide a set of geometries that you may want your users to snap to.

Following a call to synchronize snap sources ( or ), the collection is populated with objects that are valid for snapping. Each represents a that is in the currently connected map, is suitable for snapping, and is loaded. For each you want to allow snapping to, set the corresponding to true. If rule-based snapping is configured, then snapping behavior may be limited or prevented by .

Snapping is used to maintain data integrity between different sources of data when editing, so it is important that each provides full resolution geometries to be valid for snapping. This means that some of the default optimizations used to improve the efficiency of data transfer and display of polygon and polyline layers based on feature services are not appropriate for use with snapping. To snap to polygon and polyline layers based on a , the recommended approach is to ensure the following properties are configured before the layer is loaded:

list.item: Set the feature tiling mode to , and
Use the default feature request mode

remarks.para: You can configure these properties on a per-layer basis using and , or for a whole using and .
Layers and graphics overlays providing a valid source of geometries for snapping are represented by a corresponding object in the collection after snap sources are synchronized.
Alternative approaches to create a valid snappable layer for polygon or polyline hosted feature layers are to:

list.item: Disable feature tiling using , and use .
Use with any feature tiling mode.

remarks.para: If your app sets to to fetch true curves, you should consider using one of these alternatives. Curve geometries are not currently supported in the PBF format used by feature tile requests, and densified curves are returned from the service if feature tiling is used. Using these alternatives will ensure that true curves are preserved in a based on a feature service.
Local data sources, such as geodatabases, always provide full resolution geometries. Point and multipoint feature layers are also always full resolution.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.ShapeTool
member.summary: Allows you to create polygon or polyline geometries with a geometric shape by using a single drag interaction.

remarks.para: Create a new shape tool using and specify the type of shape to be created (the cannot be changed after the tool is instantiated). Call , passing in either or , then drag on the MapView to define the location and size of the shape. For example, drag diagonally to define the corners of a bounding box within which a or shape fits, or to define the center point and a point on the circumference of an or point of a .
You can select different objects by tapping, move selected elements, and use transformation control handles to rotate and scale the selected elements, subject to the current .
You can also use this tool to add new geometric shaped parts to existing polygon or polyline geometries by setting to true in the and calling . You cannot use to create geometry types other than or .

By default, this tool does not display vertices or mid-vertices because you do not interact with individual vertices using this tool. The appearance can be customized by changing . By default, this tool enables creation, selection, transformation, and deletion. This behavior can be customized with .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapRules
member.summary: A table of snapping rules to apply to one or more objects

remarks.para: When using the to edit the geometry of features that participate in a utility network, rule-based snapping can be configured on . Utility networks rely on geometric coincident-based connectivity to provide pathways for resources, and snapping can be a useful tool to help maintain those pathways. A utility network contains rules for connectivity, and using rule-based snapping respects the rules for the specific feature type being edited, based on its asset type and asset group, helping maintain network connectivity. To use rule-based snapping to edit a specific :

list.item:
Connect a to the map view, and ensure the is set and contains the pertinent utility network(s).
See for more information about general set up and configuration.
The collection should include, at a minimum, the layer you wish to edit, and the layers that have connectivity with the edit layer that will be used for snapping. The utility network data model makes use of sublayers, and adding the layers using offers the ability to change visibility and enable or disable snapping for specific subtypes. If you are programmatically adding layers to the map, ensure that you use a when adding multiple layers from the same feature service.

Get the that you will be working with.
If you are opening a web map or , the collection may already contain the relevant utility network. Alternatively you may get the utility network from the same that backs your operational layers, or call to create the network from a feature service URI and associate it with your .

Create a object appropriate for use with the utility network and asset type you will edit:
If you know the , you can pass this to . For example, if you have identified an existing feature to be edited, you can call , then get .
If you have a set of , you can pass these to . For example, you may be creating a new feature from a or by using a .
Get the , and ensure and are true.

Call , passing in the snap rules you created previously, and .
If your workflow has previously synchronized snap sources, consider if is more appropriate instead.

Call passing in the of the feature you wish to edit, or if you have no initial geometry.
The user can interactively edit the feature geometry.
When edits are complete, set the feature geometry with the result of .

remarks.para: After calling , each in is configured with the appropriate based on the connectivity rules specified in the utility network. For details, see .
Ensure that your and the layers in the map that you will snap to use the same feature tables. For more information, see .

Utility network features are z-aware — each feature geometry includes z-values ( is true). Two points, or vertices, are geometrically coincident when the x, y, and z-values of the points or vertices are equal. When you configure rule-based snapping, by calling , any edits made using snapping will use the z-value of the feature geometry from the , if that snap source geometry is z-aware. If multiple snap candidates exist at the same location, then the default z-value is used instead.
See also: Network Rules ArcGIS Pro help topic

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapSettings
member.summary: Controls interactive snapping functionality for the .

remarks.para: Snapping allows you to create geometries that connect to each other and are coincident, so that interactive edits are more accurate, with fewer errors.

If is true and a user edits a geometry interactively, then the position of the interactive edit is adjusted to coincide with ("snap to") other geometry edges (segments) or vertices. When the edit position has been adjusted, visual cues show the user the adjusted edit position, and which aspect of the referenced geometry was used as the best candidate for that adjustment. You can enable different types of snapping:

list.item: allows snapping to other features or graphics.
allows snapping to the current .

remarks.para: To use snapping in the :

list.item: Connect a to a map view using .
Get the and set to true.

To snap to other features or graphics in the map view:
Ensure the and any layers in it are loaded. Only layers that provide full resolution geometries can be used for snapping.
Call , and then set to true for each that you want users to be able to snap to. You may wish to enable all the , use only select sources, or delegate the choice to your users.
Or call , to configure rule-based snapping behavior to limit snaps based on connectivity rules stored in a utility network.
Call one of the start methods on to allow the end user to create or edit a new geometry using the current .
You can also call or to refresh the collection of snap sources at any time.

remarks.para: You can snap to objects that are contained in the MapView associated with the , and are visible. If you configured rule-based snapping, then the features that you can snap to will additionally be limited by the you set.
As snapping to objects is done to maintain data integrity between different sources of data, only objects that provide full resolution geometries can be used for snapping. See for more information.

The visual cues that indicate to a user when snapping has occurred can be customized using and . If there are multiple potential candidates that could be snapped to for a given pointer position, then is used to indicate this.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapSettings.#ctor
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapSettings.IsEnabled
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether interactive geometry edits made using the should happen using the current .

remarks.para: If is true and a user edits a geometry interactively, then when the pointer is close to an existing snappable graphic or feature, the position of the interactive edit is adjusted to coincide with ("snap to") that graphic or feature. The content of determines which sources of geometries can be used to snap to.

Setting this value temporarily to false may be useful if you wish to temporarily disable all snapping without affecting how snapping is currently configured. For example, changing this property will not affect the value of , or if rule-based snapping has been configured by using .

member.value: true if interactive geometry edits made using the should happen using the current , false otherwise. Default is false.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapSettings.IsFeatureSnappingEnabled
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether to allow interactive snapping to other features and graphics, based on the configured , false otherwise. Default is true.

remarks.para: Ignored if is false.
Feature snapping can be combined with geometry guides by ensuring is true.

member.value: true to allow interactive snapping to other features and graphics, based on the configured , false otherwise. Default is true.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapSettings.IsGeometryGuidesEnabled
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether to allow interactive snapping relative to the current , false otherwise. Default is true.

remarks.para: When enabled, additional visual cues appear as temporary guides when interactively editing polygon or polyline geometries, allowing edits to be snapped to:

list.item: coincide with an existing edge (segment) or vertex of the current
create a new segment perpendicular to the previous segment of the current
create a new segment as an extension of the previous segment of the current
create a new segment parallel to an existing segment of the current

remarks.para: The guides shown indicate the type of snap occurring.
Ignored if is false.
Geometry guides can be combined with snapping to other features or graphics by ensuring is true and configuring appropriately.

member.value: true to allow interactive snapping relative to the current , false otherwise. Default is true.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapSettings.IsHapticFeedbackEnabled
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether to enable haptic feedback whenever a snap occurs (if supported by the device), false otherwise.

remarks.para: Enables haptic feedback whenever a snap occurs to a feature or graphic from a , or geometry guide.
Note that the specifics of the haptic will vary on different platforms and devices, and this boolean will have no effect if the end user's device does not support haptics.

Haptic feedback should be expected whenever the event fires. If you want to implement your own haptics experience, set this boolean to false and trigger your own custom haptics by listening to the event.
The default value is true.

member.value: true to enable haptic feedback whenever a snap occurs (if supported by the device), false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapSettings.SourceSettings
member.summary: Gets the collection of snapping sources and whether or not each is enabled for snapping.

remarks.para: Call to populate this collection with a for each valid present in the connected MapView. To use rule-based snapping, call instead.

is connected to a MapView via , and . If there is no connected MapView, or the map view and the associated map contains no valid objects, then the collection will be empty after the synchronization call returns.

The order of objects will match the order that the associated objects appear in the and GraphicsOverlays collections, with objects from appearing first, followed by GraphicsOverlays objects, in order.
For objects that correspond to a , returns a collection of separate objects representing each .
After synchronization, the collection will not contain a for any loadable which is not yet .

As snapping is used to maintain data integrity between different sources of data, only objects that provide full resolution geometries as considered valid for creating a object - see for more information.

member.value: Defines the collection of snapping sources and whether or not each is enabled for snapping.

member.value: A radius in device-independent pixels (DIP) that specifies how close the interactive edit position must be to the snapping source geometry in order to trigger the snap.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapSettings.SyncSourceSettings
member.summary: Synchronizes the collection based on objects in the currently connected .

remarks.para: Call this method to populate with a for each valid present in the MapView currently connected to this via , and . If there is no connected MapView, or the map view and the associated map contains no objects valid for snapping, then the collection will be empty after this method returns.

The order of objects will match the order that the associated objects appear in the and GraphicsOverlays collections, with objects from appearing first, followed by GraphicsOverlays objects, in order. The collection will not contain a for any loadable which is not yet when is called.

Subsequent calls will update the existing collection, accounting for objects that have been added, removed, or moved since the last call to the same connected map. Any changes made to existing objects, for example setting , will be preserved.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapSourceSettings
member.summary: Configures a valid source of geometries that the can snap interactive edits to.

remarks.para: Following a call to or , the collection is populated with objects that are valid for snapping. Each represents a that is in the currently connected map and provides full resolution geometries suitable for snapping.
, , , and objects are types of snap source. For snap sources, the collection is populated with snap sources.

Set to true for each item that you want allow your users to snap to. You may wish to delegate the choice of snapping sources to users by binding the collection of snapping sources to controls which allow them to change for each snapping source individually. When rule-based snapping is configured, interactive snapping behavior is affected by the used in . See and for more information.

Snapping works for visible objects within a visible - for example if a feature layer or graphics overlay is not visible due to the current scale range, definition expression, or other reason, it will not participate in snapping at that time. Visibility does not affect whether a object is present in the collection or not.
See for more information about which data sources are valid for snapping.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapSourceSettings.IsEnabled
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether to enable snapping to the geometries in the , false otherwise. Default is false.

remarks.para: Snapping sources can be enabled or disabled individually. If or are false, this value is ignored and interactive snapping is not enabled to this .

If rule-based snapping is configured, this value is affected by the value of passed to . In this case, also affects if interactive snapping occurs for this snap source, and which parts of the snap source can be snapped to. See for more information about configuring rule-based snapping.

member.value: true to enable snapping to the geometries in the , false otherwise. Default is false.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapSourceSettings.RuleBehavior
member.summary: Gets a value that describes how affect the interactive snapping behavior of this .

remarks.para: This value indicates how interactive snapping behavior is affected by , which may allow, limit, or prevent snapping to this . Rule-based snapping is configured by calling - see for more information about configuring a with rule-based snapping.
If rule-based snapping is not configured (if snap sources were synchronized by calling ), then this value will be for all in the collection.

This information can be used to provide UI feedback to end users about which can be snapped to. For example if your app presents a list of snap sources to the end user to enable or disable, you may wish to exclude snap sources because they cannot actually be snapped to, regardless of the value of .

member.value: Describes how affect the interactive snapping behavior of this .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapState
member.summary: The resulting snap state when snapping to or from a snap candidate.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapState.Unsnapped
member.summary: The previous snap candidate is no longer suitable and the current edit position is not snapped.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapState.SnappedToFeature
member.summary: A snap has occurred to a .

remarks.para:
This value will be returned in the following cases:
When moving from an unsnapped position to a position that's snapped to an existing snappable feature or graphic.
When there is a change in snap candidate. For example, moving from a snap point on a polyline segment to its end vertex. This includes moving to a multiple candidate snap where one of the candidates was part of the previous snap.
Note that if the snap contains multiple candidates, where some are from features/graphics and some are from geometry guides, then will take priority over .

This value will not be returned in these cases:
When there is a change in snap point but not snap candidate. For example, moving from a snap point on a polyline segment to another snap point on the same polyline segment.
When moving around a feature or graphic such that the snap point and snap candidate do not change.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.SnapState.SnappedToGeometryGuide
member.summary: A snap has occured to a geometry guide.

remarks.para:
This value will be returned in the following cases:
When moving from an unsnapped position to a position that's snapped to an existing geometry guide.
When there is a change in snap candidate. For example, moving from a snap point on a geometry guide to a different geometry guide, or to a multiple candidate snap that involves the same guide.

This value will not be returned in these cases:
When there is a change in snap point but not snap candidate. For example, moving from a snap point on a geometry guide to another snap point on the same geometry guide.
When moving around a geometry guide such that the snap point and snap candidate do not change.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.VertexTool
member.summary: Allows you to edit geometries in a by interacting with individual vertices.

remarks.para: The default is a , and is suitable for creating and editing the widest range of geometry types - , , , and . You create and edit these geometries by creating, selecting, and transforming individual vertices, and can also select and transform parts of (polygon and polyline) geometries. Use the properties to customize the possible interactions.
If your app targets touch screen devices, you may want to consider using the instead.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.VertexTool.#ctor
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class.
member.remarks: By default, this tool enables the creation, selection, transformation, and deletion interactions appropriate for creating and editing geometries by interacting with individual vertices. This behavior can be customized with .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.Editing.VertexTool.Configuration
member.summary: Gets or sets the defines the set of interactive operations enabled when using this tool.

remarks.para: By default, the allows you to create and select vertices by tapping on the map, and also allows you to move vertices by dragging the selected vertex.

By default, it does not allow the creation of new parts, but does allow the selection, transformation, and deletion of any existing parts if they already exist in the . Set to true to allow you to tap on the view when there is no current selection ( is null) to start new parts in polyline and polygon geometries.

member.value: Defines the set of interactive operations enabled when using this tool.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.SketchEditConfiguration
member.summary: Defines which edit capabilities of the will be enabled or disabled.

remarks.para: If sketch is started without edit configuration parameter, default configuration is determined based on specified geometry parameter and/or creation mode; unless, optional drawAndEdit parameter is false, at which case, all edit capabilities are disabled.

Each time one of the StartAsync methods is called on the , it creates a default with parameters that make sense for that particular shape (e.g. circles are scaled in an aspect-ratio-preserving way, but ellipses are not). The intended use of the property is to override these defaults after each StartAsync call, to modify the defaults based on context. You can use any one of the StartAsync overloaded methods which accepts a parameter, or modify the after calling StartAsync.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.GeoElementLineOfSight
member.summary: An analysis object that evaluates visibility along a line defined by observer and target objects.
member.remarks: GeoElementLineOfSight renders a line between an observer and a target (each using geometry). Visible and obstructed portions of the line are displayed with unique colors (green and red by default). The observer and/or target GeoElements may be moving, in which case visibility will be dynamically updated. Supported types are , , and .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.LineOfSight
member.summary: A base class for visibility analyses such as and .

remarks.para: The LineOfSight analysis result is a line rendered between the observer and target with distinct colors representing visible and obstructed segments.

When at least one of the two ends (the observer and the target) of the line is placed outside the view, the colors of the visible part of the line may not accurately represent the hidden part. In such cases, the line is rendered gray. To display the color, move both the observer and the target into the view.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.LineOfSight.TargetVisibility
member.summary: Gets the target visibility from the LineOfSight object.

member.summary: Gets or sets the color used to render segments that are visible along LineOfSight analysis results.
member.remarks: Sections of the line between the observer and the target that are visible are drawn with the specified color. This property is applied to all LineOfSight analyses in the view.
member.value: The color for visible segments.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.LineOfSight.ObstructedColor
member.summary: Gets or sets the color used to render segments that are not visible along LineOfSight analysis results.
member.remarks: Sections of the line between the observer and the target that are obstructed (not visible) are drawn with the specified color. This property is applied to all LineOfSight analyses in the view.
member.value: The color used to display obstructed segments.

member.remarks: This property is applied to all LineOfSight analyses in the view.
member.value: The LineOfSight line width.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.LocationDistanceMeasurement
member.summary: An analysis object that evaluates direct, vertical, and horizontal distances between two points defined by start and end point locations.
member.remarks:
LocationDistanceMeasurement measures the distance between a start and an end location (each using geometry). Three distance components are calculated:
Direct distance - the direct distance between the start and end location
Horizontal distance - the horizontal component of the direct distance
Vertical distance - the vertical component of the direct distance

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.LocationDistanceMeasurement.#ctor
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class that defines a start and an end location using Point objects.
member.remarks: The LocationDistanceMeasurement analysis result is a distance measurement between the start and end locations with direct, horizontal, and vertical components.

member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class that defines a start and an end location using coordinates.
member.remarks: The LocationDistanceMeasurement analysis result is a distance measurement between the start and end locations with direct, horizontal, and vertical components.
member.param: A point that defines the end location of the measurement.
A point that defines the start location of the measurement.

member.summary: Gets the direct distance component of the LocationDistanceMeasurement.
member.remarks: The direct distance length measurement component. Will return null if valid start and end locations have not been set, or if an error occurs.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.LocationViewshed
member.summary: LocationViewshed is an analysis object that renders a viewshed for a specified point location.
member.remarks: LocationViewshed determines visible and non-visible areas in a GeoView for an observer defined with a map location.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.LocationViewshed.#ctor(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.Camera,System.Double,System.Double)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class.
member.param: A Camera to define the frustum of the viewshed. The camera location is that of the observer.
The maximum distance from the observer at which visibility will be evaluated.
The minimum distance from the observer at which visibility will be evaluated.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.LocationViewshed.#ctor(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint,System.Double,System.Double,System.Double,System.Double,System.Double,System.Double)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class whose observer is defined using a point location.
member.remarks: Visible and non-visible areas are determined for an area defined by the observer location, view angles, and the min/max distance range. The parameters define the frustum over which visibility is calculated from the observer location. The horizontal and vertical angles must be greater than zero to define a valid frustum and are constrained to a maximum of 360 degrees. Terrain and other obstacles between the observer and the minimum distance are excluded from the analysis, as are areas beyond the maximum distance.
member.param: The heading value (azimuth) that defines the observer's view direction.
The horizontal angle of the observer's view. The value must be greater than 0 and no greater than 360.
A geographic point location for the observer.
The maximum distance from the observer at which visibility will be evaluated.
The minimum distance from the observer at which visibility will be evaluated.
The pitch value (angle) of the observer's view relative to the surface. Valid values are from 0 (looking straight down) to 180 (straight up).
The vertical angle of the observer's view. The value must be greater than 0 and no greater than 360.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.LocationViewshed.Pitch
member.summary: Gets or sets the pitch of the observer's field of vision.
member.remarks: Pitch is the angle of the observer's field of vision relative to the surface.
member.value: A double that represents the pitch of the observer's field of vision.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.LocationViewshed.Heading
member.summary: Gets or sets the observer's heading.
member.remarks: The heading represents the azimuth of the observer's direction of view.
member.value: A double with the heading of the observer.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.LocationViewshed.Location
member.summary: Gets or sets the observer location of the viewshed.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.LocationViewshed.UpdateFromCamera(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.Camera)
member.summary: Updates the frustum of the viewshed using the properties of the supplied Camera.
member.remarks: The Camera is used to update the position, pitch, and heading of the viewshed frustum. This is convenient for quickly aligning the viewshed to the current active view.
member.param: A Camera from which the viewshed will be updated.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.Viewshed
member.summary: Viewshed is a base class for the Analysis classes that determine visible and non-visible areas in a GeoView, and .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.Viewshed.HorizontalAngle
member.summary: Gets or sets the horizontal angle of the observer's field of vision.
member.remarks: Visible and non-visible areas are determined for an area defined by an observer location, horizontal and vertical view angles, and a minimum/maximum distance range. These properties define a frustum (3D solid) inside of which visibility is calculated. The horizontal angle must be greater than zero to define a valid frustum and is constrained to a maximum of 360 degrees.
member.value: The horizontal angle of the viewshed. The value must be greater than 0 and no greater than 360.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.Viewshed.VerticalAngle
member.summary: Gets or sets the vertical angle of the observer's field of vision.
member.remarks: Visible and non-visible areas are determined for an area defined by an observer location, horizontal and vertical view angles, and a minimum/maximum distance range. These properties define a frustum (3D solid) inside of which visibility is calculated. The vertical angle must be greater than zero to define a valid frustum and is constrained to a maximum of 360 degrees.
member.value: The vertical angle of the observer's field of vision. The value must be greater than 0 and no greater than 360.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.Viewshed.MinDistance
member.summary: Gets or sets the minimum distance from the observer at which visibility will be evaluated.
member.remarks: The minimum distance allows you to exclude obstacles close to the observer (the corner of a building, for example). Since terrain and other obstacles between the observer and the specified minimum distance are completely excluded from the analysis, this value should be relatively small.
member.value: The minimum distance from the observer at which visibility analysis begins.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.Viewshed.MaxDistance
member.summary: Gets or sets the maximum distance from the observer at which visibility will be evaluated.
member.remarks: Visibility is not evaluated beyond the maximum distance specified. This can be used to model factors that restrict the visible distance.
member.value: The maximum distance for visibility analysis.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.Viewshed.IsFrustumOutlineVisible
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether or not the Viewshed frustum is visible.
member.remarks: The frustum visibility can be controlled for individual Viewsheds.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.Viewshed.VisibleColor
member.summary: Gets or sets the color with which visible areas of all viewsheds will be rendered.
member.remarks: Areas of the GeoView that are visible from the observer location and within the frustum defined for the viewshed analysis will be rendered using the specified color.
member.value: The color used to render visible areas in the viewshed.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.Viewshed.ObstructedColor
member.summary: Gets or sets the color with which non-visible areas of all viewsheds will be rendered.
member.remarks: Areas of the GeoView that are not visible from the observer location and within the frustum defined for the viewshed analysis will be rendered using the specified color.
member.value: The color used to render non-visible areas of a viewshed.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.GeoAnalysis.Viewshed.FrustumOutlineColor
member.summary: Gets or sets the color used to render the frustum outline.
member.remarks: The frustum (3D solid) within which visibility is evaluated can be symbolized with a specified outline color.
member.value: The frustum outline color.

member.summary: Grid is a base class for MGRSGrid, USNGGrid, UTMGrid and LatitudeLongitudeGrid that represents the display of a coordinate system grid on the map view.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LatitudeLongitudeGrid
member.summary: A grid consisting of east-west lines of latitude and north-south lines of longitude.

remarks.para: This class represents graticules — lines showing parallels of latitude and meridians of longitude for the earth.
The position of the graticules are based on the WGS84 ellipsoid, irrespective of the spatial reference used by the map or scene view.
To display graticules, create a LatitudeLongitudeGrid and set it as the grid property within the MapView or SceneView. LatitudeLongitudeGrid extends Grid, adding the label unit property.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LatitudeLongitudeGrid.#ctor
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class.

remarks.para: Constructs a grid consisting of east-west lines of latitude and north-south lines of longitude (also known as graticules). The grid is supported in spatial references which are continuously pannable or some polar spatial references. You can check the property is true on your spatial reference. Examples of continuously pannable spatial references include WGS84 (WKID 4326) and Web Mercator Auxiliary Sphere (WKID 102113, 102100, or 3857).
The following polar spatial references are also supported:

list.item: North pole Azimuthal Equidistant (WKID 102016)
North pole gnomic (WKID 102034)
North pole Lambert equal area (WKID 102017)
North pole orthographic (WKID 102035)
North pole stereographic (WKID 102018)
NSIDE EASE North (WKID 3408)
NSIDC Sea Ice polar stereographic north (WKID 3411)
WGS 1984 NSIDC Sea Ice Polar Stereographic North (WKID 3413)
UPS North (WKID 32661)
South pole Azimuthal Equidistant (WKID 102019)
South pole gnomic (WKID 102036)
South pole Lambert equal area (WKID 102020)
South pole orthographic (WKID 102037)
South pole stereographic (WKID 102021)
NSIDE EASE South (WKID 3409)
NSIDC Sea Ice polar stereographic South (WKID 3412)
UPS South (WKID 32761)
WGS1984 Antarctic polar stereographic (WKID 3031)

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LatitudeLongitudeGrid.LabelFormat
member.summary: Gets or sets the format used for labeling the grid.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LatitudeLongitudeGridLabelFormat
member.summary: The unit to use when labeling a .

member.summary: Label the grid lines (graticules) in decimal degrees.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.LatitudeLongitudeGridLabelFormat.DegreesMinutesSeconds
member.summary: Label the grid lines (graticules) in degrees, minutes and seconds.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.MgrsGrid
member.summary: A class that represents the display of the Military Grid Reference System (MGRS) on the map or scene view.

remarks.para: The military grid reference system is the geocoordinate standard used by various militaries for locating points on the earth. The MGRS grid is derived from the Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM) grid system and the universal polar stereographic (UPS) grid system, but uses a different labeling convention. MGRS is used for the entire earth.
To display the MGRS grid, create a MGRSGrid and set it as the grid property within the MapView or SceneView.
MGRSGrid inherits from Grid and adds a property for the label unit to use.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.ImageFrame
member.summary: A frame that, when added to an , renders an image on top of a map or scene.
member.remarks: An ImageFrame can contain images that do not have georeferencing information. In such cases, location data can be provided to geolocate the image.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.ImageFrame.#ctor(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.RuntimeImage,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Envelope)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class that contains the image for display at the specified geographic extent.

remarks.para: If a spatial reference is not defined for the extent, it is assumed to be the same as the map or scene. If the spatial reference of the extent is different from that of the map or scene, then the image would fail to render.
A can be created from PNG and JPEG images. In general, prefer using constructors accepting a Uri, as these constructors support more image formats and use memory more efficiently.

member.param: An image to display in the frame.
The geographic extent at which the image is displayed.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.ImageFrame.#ctor(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.RuntimeImage,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Polygon)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class using the image and a four-point polygon representing the corners of an image.

remarks.para: If a spatial reference is not defined for the polygon, it is assumed to be the same as the map or scene. If the spatial reference of the polygon is different from that of the map or scene, then the image would fail to render. If the provided polygon does not contain exactly four points, the image frame will fail to load.
A can be created from PNG and JPEG images. In general, prefer using constructors accepting a Uri, as these constructors support more image formats and use memory more efficiently.

member.param: An image to display in the frame.
A polygon consisting of four points that represent each corner of the image.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.ImageFrame.#ctor(System.Uri)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class containing a georeferenced image defined by the provided URI.
member.remarks: The URI can be a local file path or an HTTP path to an image. Supported image formats are TIFF, GeoTIFF, ICO, BMP, GIF, JPEG, and PNG. Animated GIF is not supported.
member.param: A URI that identifies a local or online image source.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.ImageFrame.#ctor(System.Uri,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Envelope)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class containing the image identified by the URI for display at the specified geographic extent.
member.remarks: If a spatial reference is not defined for the extent, it is assumed to be the same as the map or scene. If the spatial reference of the extent is different from that of the scene or map, then the image would fail to render. The URI can be a local file path or an HTTP path to an image. Supported image formats are TIFF, GeoTIFF, ICO, BMP, GIF, JPEG, and PNG. Animated GIF is not supported.
member.param: A URI that identifies a local or online image source.
The geographic extent at which the image is displayed.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.ImageFrame.#ctor(System.Uri,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.Polygon)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class using the URI of an image and a four-point polygon representing the corners of an image.
member.remarks: If a spatial reference is not defined for the polygon, it is assumed to be the same as the map or scene. If the spatial reference of the extent is different from that of the map or scene, then the image would fail to render. If the provided polygon doesn't contain exactly four points, the image frame will fail to load. The URI can be a local file path or an HTTP path to an image. Supported image formats are TIFF, GeoTIFF, ICO, BMP, GIF, JPEG, and PNG. Animated GIF is not supported.
member.param: A URI that identifies a local or online image source.
A polygon consisting of four points that represent each corner of the image.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.ImageOverlay
member.summary: An overlay that contains an with an image to display in the .

remarks.para: The overlay can contain exactly one image at any given time. Multiple overlays, however, can be added and rendered at the same time on the . An can be used to quickly render frequently changing images. For example, rendering realtime sensor data, such as weather, where each static image represents a single frame from the radar. In this workflow, images are constantly updated via a new each time new data is available. This provides a fast-refreshing workflow where the underlying images are frequently updated as new data comes in.
Since does not support the rich processing and rendering capabilities of a , you should use and for workflows that require static image rendering, analysis, and persistence.
Image overlays are rendered above all other layers in the map or scene, draped on the surface, and below any graphics overlays in the map or scene view.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController
member.summary: A camera controller that supports orbital camera navigation around a stationary or moving .
member.remarks: This camera controller is used to associate camera navigation and movement with objects, such as , , and . When this controller is set on the scene view, the interaction mode allows the user to rotate around and zoom in and out from the . If the object is moving, the camera moves in synchronization with it and the camera position, heading, pitch and roll automatically adjusts. If the geometry is a polyline or a polygon, the camera will focus on its center point. Once you have applied this camera controller to a , you cannot change the nor the viewpoint of the scene view.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.#ctor(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Data.GeoElement,System.Double)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class using a distance from a specified .
member.remarks: This camera controller is used to associate camera navigation and movement with objects, such as , , and . When the controller is set on the scene view, using , the interaction mode allows the user to rotate around and zoom in and out from the object. For a moving object, the camera will move in synchronization with it and the camera position, heading, pitch and roll will automatically adjust. If the geometry is a polyline or a polygon, the camera will focus on its center point.
member.param: The that will be the focus of the camera in the scene view.
The distance in meters that the camera will be positioned from the .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.CameraDistance
member.summary: Gets or sets the distance from the to the camera in meters.
member.remarks: Camera position is derived from this distance plus camera offsets. Must be given an initial value in the constructor.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.CameraHeadingOffset
member.summary: Gets or sets the heading of the camera relative to the in degrees.
member.remarks: The clockwise angle in the symbol's horizontal plane starting directly behind the symbol. Any angle in degrees is valid. The value will be truncated if set below the or above the . Defaults to 0 degrees.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.CameraPitchOffset
member.summary: Gets or sets the pitch of the camera relative to the in degrees.
member.remarks: The anti-clockwise angle from the positive Z axis of the symbol to the symbol's horizontal plane. Any angle in degrees is valid. The value will be truncated if set below the or above the . Defaults to 45 degrees.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.MinCameraDistance
member.summary: Gets or sets the minimum distance from the camera to the , in meters.
member.remarks: Must be greater than 0. Animations and interactions are bounded by this limit. The default value is 0.
member.value: The minimum distance from the camera to the target, in meters.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.MaxCameraDistance
member.summary: Gets or sets the maximum distance from the camera to the , in meters.
member.remarks: There is no limit on maximum distance. Animations and interactions are bounded by this limit.
member.value: The maximum distance from the camera to the target, in meters.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.MinCameraHeadingOffset
member.summary: Gets or sets the minimum limit on , in degrees.
member.remarks: Any angle, positive or negative, will be wrapped to a clockwise angle between 0 and 360. The value must be less than . Animations and interactions are bounded by this limit. The default value is -180.
member.value: The minimum camera heading offset relative to the target's , in degrees.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.MaxCameraHeadingOffset
member.summary: Gets or sets the limit on relative to the , in degrees.
member.remarks: Any angle, positive or negative, will be wrapped to a clockwise angle between 0 and 360. The value must be greater than . Animations and interactions are bounded by this limit. The default value is 180.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.MinCameraPitchOffset
member.summary: Gets or sets the minimum limit on , in degrees.
member.remarks: The angle must be between -90 and . Animations and interactions are bounded by this limit. The default value is -90.
member.value: The minimum camera pitch offset relative to the target's , in degrees.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.MaxCameraPitchOffset
member.summary: Gets or sets the maximum limit on , in degrees.
member.remarks: The angle must be between and 90. Animations and interactions are bounded by this limit. The default value is 90.
member.value: The maximum camera pitch offset relative to the target's , in degrees.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.IsAutoHeadingEnabled
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether the property is automatically synchronized with the symbol.

remarks.para: If true, the camera will maintain its heading offset when the target's heading changes. Otherwise, the camera position is not affected by the target's heading. The default value is true.
If you have applied a renderer to the target , you can use the renderer's to control its heading.

member.value: true if the camera heading offset is updated with the target's heading, false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.IsAutoPitchEnabled
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether the property is automatically synchronized with the symbol.

remarks.para: If true, the camera will maintain its pitch offset when the target's pitch changes. Otherwise, the camera position is not affected by the target's pitch. The default value is true.
If you have applied a renderer to the target , you can use the renderer's to control its pitch.

member.value: true if the camera pitch offset is updated with the target's pitch, false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.IsAutoRollEnabled
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether the camera roll is automatically synchronized with the roll of the symbol.

remarks.para: If true, the camera will maintain its roll offset when the target's roll changes. Otherwise, the camera position is not affected by the target's roll. The default value is true.
If you have applied a renderer to the target , you can use the renderer's to control its roll.

member.value: true if the camera roll offset is updated with the target's roll, false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.IsCameraDistanceInteractive

member.remarks: If true, the user can interactively change the distance between the camera and the target object. The default value is true.
member.value: true if a zoom interaction can affect the camera distance, false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.IsCameraHeadingOffsetInteractive
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether a horizontal pan interaction can affect the camera heading offset.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitGeoElementCameraController.IsCameraPitchOffsetInteractive
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether a vertical pan interaction can affect the camera pitch offset.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitLocationCameraController
member.summary: Camera controller that supports orbital camera navigation around a stationary point in a .
member.remarks: An orbit camera controller locks navigation around a target point. The camera can orbit around the target point but always focuses directly on the point. To activate the controller, set the controller on the property. In this mode, the user can rotate around and zoom in and out from the target point. Once you have applied this camera controller to a , you cannot change the nor the viewpoint of the scene view.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitLocationCameraController.#ctor(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint,System.Double)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class from the given point.
member.remarks: The orbit camera controller locks navigation around a target point. When this camera controller is applied, using , navigation interaction always focuses on the target point object. In this mode, the user can rotate around and zoom in and out from the point. Once specified, the target point cannot be changed.
member.param: The for the camera to focus on.
Distance from the point at which this camera will be placed.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.UI.OrbitLocationCameraController.#ctor(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class from the given target point and camera point.
member.remarks: The orbit camera controller calculates the distance of the camera from the target point and locks navigation around that target point. When this camera controller is applied, using , navigation interaction always focuses on the target point object. In this mode, the user can rotate around and zoom in and out from the point. Once specified, the target point cannot be changed.
member.param: A point which will be the focus of this camera.
A point which is where this camera is located.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Http.ArcGISHttpClientHandler.DefaultReferer
member.summary: Gets or sets the default referer used by default for all requests going through .

remarks.para: A referer is text in the Http header that is passed between web requests of the client application and web server.

In many scenarios, the referer is used to provide the web server how the incoming web request was initiated. For example a user clicked a particular hyperlink on a web page and the referer gets populated with that hyperlink. The referer is often the URL of the previous web page from which a link was followed. Some companies use referer information to find out user internet browsing patterns for their internet site. Setting referer information in the Http header of a web request is many times required by secured web sites so they can ensure that only approved users are using their resources.

IMPORTANT
Setting a string value for the DefaultReferer Property to the correct referer is necessary in order to gain access to ArcGIS Server web services that require long term tokens for authentication. ArcGIS Online and ArcGIS Portal require long term token for web services that are secured. Depending on the development platform, specifying the correct string for the AuthenticationManager.TokenGeneration Property can be imperative to accessing secured services by the AuthenticationManager.

member.value: The default referer.

If possible, the collection will contain a single entry identifying the application package ID and version. You can remove, replace or add additional information to this if necessary.

member.name: E:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Http.ArcGISHttpClientHandler.HttpRequestBegin
member.summary: Sets a global HTTP request listener invoked every time a request is made by the .

remarks.para: You can use this to intercept any outgoing requests and for instance inject extra headers, append data to the query string etc, based on the request URL.
This listener is called on each and every request made by the ArcGIS Runtime, and therefore must be both thread-safe and highly performant.
Requests that implement provide the additional option of configuring or replacing the that services that request.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Http.HttpResponseEndEventArgs
member.summary: Provides data for the event.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Http.HttpResponseEndEventArgs.GetDeferral
member.summary: Returns a deferral which can be completed when the event is ready to continue.

remarks.para: Sometimes, you may need to use async code when working with the event. Normally, you might be able to use an async void event handler without issue.

Consider a scenario where you want to change the response received based on the contents of the current response. In order to do this, you need to asynchronously read the contents of the response before you can inspect it. In this scenario, an async event handler needs a way to delay the HTTP handler until it has completed processing the response.

The approach that WinRT has taken for this scenario is through "deferral" objects. An asynchronous event handler acquires a deferral before the first await and notifies that deferral when the handler is complete.

member.returns: A deferral object that implements .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Http.IHttpMessageInterceptor
member.summary: Defines an interface for intercepting and processing HTTP requests and responses made by the Maps SDK.

remarks.para: The allows you to intercept, monitor, and modify HTTP requests and responses. You can use it for various purposes, such as logging, tracing, modifying headers, or mocking responses.

Examples of usage:
Logging:You can create an implementation that logs the request and response details, such as the request URI, HTTP method, response status code, and elapsed time. This can be useful for monitoring and debugging.
Mocking:You can create an implementation that mocks the HTTP responses for automated testing scenarios. This allows you to simulate different response scenarios without actually making real network calls.
Modifying Headers:You can create an implementation that modifies the request or response headers. For example, you can add custom headers, modify the user agent, or adjust the cache control settings.
To use an , create a class that implements this interface and register it using the extension method on the instance.
remarks.example: Inserting a header on requests to a specific domain: public class MyHttpListener : IHttpMessageInterceptor { public Task<HttpResponseMessage> SendAsync(HttpMessageInvoker invoker, HttpRequestMessage message, CancellationToken cancellationToken) { if(message.RequestUri?.Host == "mydomain.com") { message.Headers.Add("X-CustomHeader", "myHeaderValue"); } return invoker.SendAsync(message, cancellationToken); } }

member.summary: Sends an HTTP request and returns the response.
member.param: The default invoker responsible for sending and processing the request.

Cancellation token
member.returns: HTTP Response message

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Http.HttpConfigurationExtensions.UseDefaultReferer(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Http.IArcGISHttpConfiguration,System.Uri)
member.summary: Sets the default referer used by default for all requests going through .
member.param: HTTP Configuration
The default referer.
member.returns: HTTP Configuration

remarks.para: A referer is text in the Http header that is passed between web requests of the client application and web server.

In many scenarios, the referer is used to provide the web server how the incoming web request was initiated. For example a user clicked a particular hyperlink on a web page and the referer gets populated with that hyperlink. The referer is often the URL of the previous web page from which a link was followed. Some companies use referer information to find out user internet browsing patterns for their internet site. Setting referer information in the Http header of a web request is many times required by secured web sites so they can ensure that only approved users are using their resources.

IMPORTANT
Setting a string value for the DefaultReferer Property to the correct referer is necessary in order to gain access to ArcGIS Server web services that require long term tokens for authentication. ArcGIS Online and ArcGIS Portal require long term token for web services that are secured. Depending on the development platform, specifying the correct string for the AuthenticationManager.TokenGeneration Property can be imperative to accessing secured services by the AuthenticationManager.

remarks.para: Allows you to append a set of custom values to the user agent string, useful for tracking the application usage from a server endpoint. This method can be called multiple times to append more values.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Http.HttpConfigurationExtensions.UseHttpMessageHandler``1(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Http.IArcGISHttpConfiguration)
member.summary: Sets a delegate that will be used to create new instances of , that will be used by ArcGIS Maps SDK for .NET to perform network requests.
member.remarks:
By default, ArcGIS Maps SDK for .NET selects an implementation that works best for the current platform. If there is a specific need to use a different handler, the following types may be used:
HttpClientHandler
SocketsHttpHandler (all .NET 6 platforms)
NSUrlSessionHandler (iOS and macOS only)
AndroidMessageHandler (Android only)
WebRequestHandler (.NET Framework only)
DelegatingHandler (InnerHandler needs to be initialized from the default constructor)
member.param: HTTP Configuration
member.returns: HTTP Configuration

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Http.HttpConfigurationExtensions.UseRemoteServerCertificateValidationCallback(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Http.IArcGISHttpConfiguration,System.Net.Security.RemoteCertificateValidationCallback)
member.summary: Sets the delegate that validates the server certificate during SSL/TLS handshake.

remarks.para: This callback can examine all SSL connections made by the Maps SDK and by to service HTTPS requests. When is null, server certificates are validated by the operating system using default policies and known certificate authorities. A custom validator can be used to override the default behavior and accept or reject server certificates based on custom criteria, such as:

list.item: Accept server credentials that would otherwise be rejected by the system. For example, your app needs to make an HTTPS connection to a development server or through a proxy with a self-signed certificate. Refer to the Ignore specific validation errors section in the .NET Security Best Practices guide for a sample implementation.

Reject credentials that would otherwise be accepted by the system. For example, your app needs to "pin" a set of specific keys or certificates under your control, rather than accept any valid credential. Refer to the Certificate pinning section in the .NET Security Best Practices guide for a sample implementation.

remarks.para: You can obtain the remote hostname from the "sender" parameter. Depending on context, the "sender" may be either System.Net.Http.HttpRequestMessage or System.Net.Security.SslStream.
When the sslPolicyErrors parameter is , the certificate is considered valid by system's default validation. Otherwise, details about SSL errors can be obtained from of the certificateChain parameter.

Return true from the callback to accept the server certificate, or false to reject it. The returned value is not cached and the callback is invoked each time a new connection is made. The callback should be able to handle repeated calls for the same server, including parallel calls from multiple threads.

When targeting .NET 8+, secure WebSocket connections from will use this callback. When targeting .NET Framework, WebSocket connections go through System.Net.ServicePointManager.ServerCertificateValidationCallback instead. When targeting UWP, there is no way to customize certificate validation for WebSockets.

member.param: HTTP Configuration
The callback to use.
member.returns: HTTP Configuration

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.IApiKeyResource
member.summary: Represents a resource that supports access with an API key.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.IApiKeyResource.ApiKey
member.summary: Gets or sets the API key that allows your app to access ArcGIS location services and private portal items.

remarks.para: An API key is a unique long-lived access token that is used to authenticate and monitor requests to ArcGIS location services and private portal items. You can create and manage an API key using your portal when you sign in with an ArcGIS Location Platform account or an ArcGIS Online account with administrator access or a custom role that has the `Generate API keys` privilege. To learn how to create and manage API keys, go to the Create an API Key tutorial. You must ensure that your API key has the correct privileges to access secure resources.
Setting the API key on a specific resource overrides the default key set on .

In addition to setting an at a global level for your application, you can set it on any class that implements . This overrides the and enables more granular usage telemetry and management of ArcGIS location resources used by your app.
Classes that expose an API key property by implementing include:

Note: An API key created with a referrer is not supported. If you need service requests to include the referrer, call when initializing the . var myReferer = new Uri("http://foo.bar"); ArcGISRuntimeEnvironment.Initialize( config => config.UseApiKey("AAPK...") .ConfigureHttp( httpConfig => httpConfig.UseDefaultReferer(myReferer)));
For more information, see API key authentication and Types of authentication.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Ogc.KmlDataset
member.summary: Points to KML/KMZ content, either from a local file or the network. This object provides an entry point for traversing the tree of s that make up the file.

remarks.para: Use the property to obtain a to traverse the objects for examining/using individual KML features. The is populated after a KML dataset has finished loading successfully.
A typical KML document will have multiple levels of nodes. Consider using recursion to discover the full collection of nodes in a KML file. See the guide doc and samples for details.
If a KML file has s, the content can change after loading due to automatic refresh. You can use the event to detect changes and update your app's UI.

member.param: The to use as the first root node.

member.name: E:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Ogc.KmlDataset.NetworkLinkControlMessage
member.summary: Event that is raised when a network link control message is available for this dataset.
member.remarks: KML network links may specify messages, such as usage guidelines, to be shown once to application end-user. Applications are expected to deliver this message to the end-user, typically in the form of an alert. The event will be raised when a network link is first loaded, or when it is changed in the network link control. This event could be raised from an off-UI thread; you should dispatch any UI-related work (like showing the message) to the UI thread.

member.summary: Gets the location of the KML data from which this dataset was created.

member.value: The from which this dataset was loaded; or null if this dataset was constructed from a .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Ogc.KmlDataset.RootNodes
member.summary: Gets this dataset's root KML nodes as a .

remarks.para: Used to traverse the objects for examining/using individual KML features. is populated after a KML dataset has finished loading successfully.

It should be noted that a typical KML document will have multiple levels of nested KML nodes. To traverse through all of the KML node information in a KmlDataset, it is recommended to recursively test all KmlNodes to see if they contain additional child KmlNodes. When writing your recursive function pay particular attention to the properties: KmlDataset.RootNodes, KmlNetworkLink.ChildNodes, and KmlContainer.ChildNodes (inherited by and ).

member.value: A read-only collection of objects.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Ogc.KmlImageCoordinate
member.summary: Specifies an image coordinate with units.

remarks.para: According to the OGC 2.3 specification in section 16.26.2 (http://docs.opengeospatial.org/is/12-007r2/12-007r2.html#1254) "Specifies an image coordinate system. The x and y values may each be specified in three different ways - as pixels (pixels), as fractions of the icon (fraction), or as inset pixels (insetPixels), which is an offset in pixels from the upper right corner of the icon. They may or may not be specified in a consistent manner - for example, x can be specified in pixels and y as a fraction."
Use the to establish the KML image coordinate system. The and image coordinate systems are defined with device-independent pixels (DIPs).

member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether the app should zoom to the KML network link when it refreshes. NetworkLinkControl or the network link's top-level kml node upon refresh of the link.

remarks.para: When this is defined, the app should zoom to the viewpoint defined on the associated NetworkLinkControl or the network links' top-level KML node upon refresh of the link. The GeoView will not automatically animate its viewpoint to the refreshed node. It is the application developer's responsibility to implement this behavior, if desired.
You can listen for a PropertyChangedEvent on the viewpoint to detect when the geoview's viewpoint should be updated.
This is defined in the flyToView tag in the network link.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Ogc.KmlNode
member.summary: A single node within a KML document.

remarks.para: KML is a file format used to display geographic data (geometric shapes, symbology and attributes). KML uses a tag-based structure with nested elements and attributes and is based on the XML standard. The KML files are often distributed in KMZ files, which are zipped KML files with a .kmz extension.

A corresponds to the KML concept of a Feature. is an abstract type that describes properties common to all KML Features, such as: , , , , etc. The following table maps KML Features Types to ArcGIS Runtime types:

tr.td: Abstract type. Contains properties common to KmlDocument and KmlFolder.
Properties can be found on individual overlay subtypes instead.

remarks.para: Examining individual nodes may be useful for: viewing attributes of the KML data, seeing the organization/structure within the , toggling visibility of KML features, and navigating to specific KML features.

It should be noted that a typical KML document will have multiple levels of nested KML nodes. Therefore to accurately traverse through all of the KML node information in a , it is recommended to recursively test all KmlNodes to see if they contain additional child KmlNodes. When writing your recursive function pay particular attention to the properties: KmlDataset.RootNodes, KmlNetworkLink.ChildNodes, and KmlContainer.ChildNodes (inherited by and ).

member.remarks: All coordinates in KML are expressed in latitude and longitude (using decimal degrees) and are in the geographic coordinate system. May be null.

member.remarks: Snippets can be defined for KML nodes as an additional short description for UI display. If no snippet is specified, you can take the first few lines of the . The maximum number of lines to display is specified by the property.

member.summary: Gets the color used to tint the UX icon.
member.remarks: Pixels in the image are multiplied by this color channel-by-channel. Colors in KML are specified in ABGR order. When no icon is specified ( is null), you can use the icon color to draw a placeholder image in the UI (for example, the legend). UX icons are not pre-tinted; it is the application's responsibility to apply the tint color to the UX icon before displaying it.
