member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.TileCache
member.summary: A local on-disk cache of pre-rendered map tiles that can be used to create a layer.

remarks.para: A tile cache stores a collection of images at various scales. A client can request the tiles needed to display a particular map extent. You can use to generate and download tiles from a service, creating a tile package (.tpk/.tpkx). Alternatively, you can use ArcGIS Pro to create a map tile package and provision it to the device.
ArcGIS tiled layers do not support reprojection, query, select, identify, or editing.

Tiles are generated when the cache is created. Requests for tiles are made on multiple threads and handled asynchronously. The size of each returned tile increases as the resolution or complexity of the image in the tile increases. For example, high-resolution imagery tiles can be larger files than topographic mapping for the same area and map extent.

Local tile caches are ideal for providing basemaps, or for infrequent changes to contextual layers when network access is limited or non-existent. Use to create and download a local tile cache to a device. Alternatively, the cache can be provisioned directly to local storage.
The supported types of cache are:

list.item: Tile package (.tpk or .tpkx) - a tile cache of data, packaged into one convenient, portable file, ideal for offline sharing of complete tiled layers in a disconnected environment or via a portal.
Compact Cache - a directory structure where groups of tiles are combined into larger .bundle files, preserving performance and reducing copy times and the size on disk of the cache.
Exploded Cache - a directory structure where map tiles are stored as individual files.

remarks.para: A tile cache is often used as a basemap, but can also be used to create an that is added to a map as an operational layer. An offline can also be created from an appropriate tile cache and added to the of a .

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.VectorTileCache
member.summary: A local cache of vector tiles that can be used to create an .

remarks.para: Local tile caches are ideal for providing basemaps or less frequently changing contextual layers, where network access is limited or non-existent. You can create and download a vector tile cache onto a device by using an , or alternatively you can provision it directly to local storage. Currently the only supported type of cache is the vector tile package (.vtpk). This single file contains all of the tile data files, the source information, a style sheet, and the font and symbol marker resources required to display the map.
You can create an from a and then use it as a basemap or as an operational layer in a map or scene.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.WebTiledLayer
member.summary: A layer that requests image tiles based on a URL template, providing a simple way to display maps from non-ArcGIS web services or local tile caches.

remarks.para: This layer provides a simple way to integrate non-ArcGIS Services as a layer in a map. The URL template usually follows a pattern similar to https://{subDomain}.server.org/path/{level}/{col}/{row}.png, where:

list.item: "subDomain" is one of the strings in the subDomains list
"level" corresponds to a zoom level
"col" represents the column of the tile
"row" represents the row of the tile.

remarks.para: The URL should be arranged to reflect how the tiles are organized in the cache or on the server, and can point to a web server (https://) or to a local file cache (file://).
The tiles retrieved by this class are accessed directly by URL. Because the service is only providing images, you are responsible for manually setting the attribution text on the map or scene view.

To create a custom service tiled layer whose URL follows the above pattern (or a similar pattern), you can extend this class. Alternatively, you can extend the super class , in which you implement a required method to set the request information for a given LOD, column, and row. Extending WebTiledLayer, however, has no such requirement.
An will be thrown when attempting to load a layer with invalid URI template.
If is not specified, tiles are assumed to be in the OpenStreetMap tiling scheme, with 256x256 PNG tiles at 96 DPI, the WebMercator projection, and a FullExtent of [-180, -85.0511, 180, 85.051].

Tiles are fetched on demand using the specified URI template. Tiles are typically rendered and cached on the server but may be generated on demand by some services. Web tiled layers do not support identify, query, selection, or time.
Specifying sub-domains from which the layer will request tiles allows the load to be more evenly distributed among servers.
Web tiled layer requires a connection to the service at all times. Performance is similar to other raster tile layers.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.WebTiledLayer.#ctor
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class, that must be configured before use.
member.remarks: Since this constructor does not require an explicit argument, the max LOD will be 23 (approximate scale of 70 and resolution of 1.86 cm/pixel).

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.WebTiledLayer.#ctor(System.String)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class with the given URI template.
member.param: A URI template for tile requests.

remarks.para:
Use this constructor to create a to integrate non-ArcGIS Services as a layer in a map. Since this constructor does not require an explicit argument, the max LOD will be 23 (approximate scale of 70 and resolution of 1.86 cm/pixel). The URI template usually follows a pattern similar to https://server.org/path/{level}/{col}/{row}.png, where:
"level" corresponds to a zoom level.
"col" represents the column of the tile.
"row" represents the row of the tile.
The URI should be arranged to reflect how the tiles are arranged in the cache or on the server, and can point to a web server (https://) or to a local file cache (file://).
The tiles are assumed to be in the OpenStreetMap tiling scheme, with 256x256 PNG tiles at 96 DPI, the webMercator projection, and an extent of [-180, -85.0511, 180, 85.051].
and are set to OpenStreetMap-compatible defaults, and is left empty.

member.param: A URI template for tile requests.

remarks.para:
Use this constructor to create a to integrate non-ArcGIS Services as a layer in a map. Since this constructor does not require an explicit argument, the max LOD will be 23 (approximate scale of 70 and resolution of 1.86 cm/pixel). The URI template usually follows a pattern similar to https://{subDomain}.server.org/path/{level}/{col}/{row}.png, where:
"subDomain" is one of the strings in the subDomains collection
"level" corresponds to a zoom level
"col" represents the column of the tile
"row" represents the row of the tile

The subDomains collection must contain at least one string value to use the "subDomain" key in the URI template. The URI should be arranged to reflect how the tiles are arranged in the cache or on the server, and can point to a web server (https://) or to a local file cache (file://).

The tiles are assumed to be in the OpenStreetMap tiling scheme, with 256x256 PNG tiles at 96 DPI, the webMercator projection, and an extent of [-180, -85.0511, 180, 85.051]. and are set to OpenStreetMap-compatible defaults.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.WmsLayer
member.summary: A that displays data from OGC Web Map Services (WMS).

remarks.para: Web Map Service (WMS) is an Open Geospatial Consortium (OGC) standard for delivering map images from an online service. WMS layer displays data from a web service that provides maps in the form of server-rendered images. This API supports WMS versions 1.1.0, 1.1.1, and 1.3.0. The service may be hosted in the cloud on ArcGIS Online, on a third party server, or on-premises ArcGIS Server.

A WMS service can contain multiple layers in a hierarchy. A WMS layer can be constructed directly with a URL to a service and the uniquely identifying name of the desired layer. Alternatively, a can be used to programmatically explore the available layers and allow the user to choose layers at run time.

The maps provided by a WMS service use predefined symbology defined by the server. As a result, it is not possible to apply custom renderers or to visualize feature selection. WMS layers can have multiple style options. Your app can choose from the available styles.
Some layers can be marked as opaque, which means that they cover most of the map area and are good candidates for use as a basemap.

WMS layers support identify and time. They do not support selection or query. Note that due to the nature of WMS, it is not possible to retrieve feature geometry from WMS identify results. WMS layers support custom parameters, which can be specified for the service or an individual layer.
WMS servers render map images on demand, which can require more server resources than a similar tiled service. WMS requires a service connection at all times.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.WmtsLayer
member.summary: Displays data from a WMTS service by using pre-generated tiles.

remarks.para: Web Map Tile Service (WMTS) is an Open Geospatial Consortium (OGC) standard for delivering geographic data via raster tiles. The service may be hosted in the cloud on ArcGIS Online, on a third party server, or on-premises with ArcGIS Server. This API supports WMTS 1.0.0.
A WMTS layer uses pre-generated tiles to create a map instead of dynamically generating map images.
The maps provided by a WMTS service use predefined symbology defined by the server. As a result, it is not possible to apply custom renderers or to visualize feature selection.

A WMTS service can contain multiple layers in a hierarchy. A WMTS layer can be constructed directly with a URL to a service and the uniquely identifying name of the desired layer. Alternatively, a WMTS service can be used to programmatically explore the available layers and allow the user to choose layers at run time.

When creating a from URL: For some WMTS services it may be necessary to provide a direct URI to the getCapabilities resource, by appending either /1.0.0/WMTSCapabilities.xml or ?service=wmts&request=getCapabilities&version=1.0.0 to the root WMTS URI.

WMTS layer consumes raster tiles that were prerendered by a server. WMTS requires fewer server resources than WMS because the images are rendered and cached ahead of time. WMTS layer requires a connection to the service at all times.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.ArcGISTiledElevationSource
member.summary: An elevation source based on a tiled ArcGIS image service containing elevation data.

remarks.para: An ArcGIS tiled elevation source helps visualize maps and layers in 3D by providing a surface that layers may be draped or offset from.

You can create an ArcGIS tiled elevation source object from the URL, or , of an online ArcGIS image service, or a local generated from an ArcGIS image service. In all cases, the Image Service must support elevation that is stored in the Limited Error Raster Compression (LERC) tile format. If your app is online and connected to ArcGIS Online, you can utilize the cached elevation image service, WorldElevation3D/Terrain 3D.
To utilize an elevation source in a scene's surface, add it to the scene's collection.

It is recommended that you use the constructor for creating an elevation source with an associated URI.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.Basemap
member.summary: Represents the basemap, which holds layers that provide a geographic context and a visual backdrop for operational data. Layers in a basemap can supply imagery, place names, administrative boundaries, and elevation data.

remarks.para: A basemap provides a background of geographical context for the content you display in a map or scene. It provides a visual reference to help orient users and a framework on which you can overlay operational layers, perform analysis, and visualize geographic information. The content of a basemap is typically static and does not change frequently.

A basemap is composed of a collection of base layers () and reference layers (). Base layers are displayed at the bottom of a map or scene, and reference layers are displayed at the top, with the between them.

Typically, you construct a basemap using a basemap style:
Use image tile basemap styles for satellite imagery and hillshade.
Use vector tile basemap styles such as streets navigation, light gray canvas, and OpenStreetMap (OSM).
Create custom basemap styles with your own colors, glyphs, and fonts using the ArcGIS Vector Tile Style Editor.

See for the full range of the basemap styles provided by Esri. Basemap styles allow you to take advantage of geographically load-balanced services and monitor usage with API keys or authenticated users. See Basemap layers) for more information.
If your app works offline, you can construct a local basemap from a tiled layer using a , such as a tile package (.tpk, .tpkx, .vtpk) or a compact cache, stored on the device.

member.summary: Gets or sets the reference layers displayed in this basemap.

remarks.para: Reference layers are drawn on top all other layers in the map or scene to display information such as boundaries, labels, and traffic networks. The collection can be empty but it is never null.
This collection is specific to this basemap so you cannot reuse a collection coming from another basemap.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyle.OSMBlueprint
member.summary: An Open Street Map (OSM) vector basemap presented using a blueprint style.
member.remarks: This vector basemap is based on the Daylight map distribution of OSM data and is hosted by Esri. The map is presented in a creative cartographic style emulating a blueprint technical drawing. The default place labels are shown in the local language for where the label appears. For example, labels for places in France are shown in French.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyle.OSMHybrid
member.summary: An Open Street Map (OSM) basemap for the world.
member.remarks: A basemap version of Open Street Map (OSM) data hosted by Esri. This layer presents low-resolution satellite imagery for the world and high-resolution satellite and aerial imagery, typically within 3-5 years of currency, for most of the world. The default place labels are shown in the local language for where the label appears. For example, labels for places in France are shown in French.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyle.OSMHybridDetail
member.summary: An Open Street Map (OSM) detailed vector basemap for the world.
member.remarks: A basemap version of Open Street Map (OSM) data hosted by Esri. This vector tile layer provides a detailed reference layer for the world designed to be overlaid on imagery. The default place labels are shown in the local language for where the label appears. For example, labels for places in France are shown in French.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyle.OSMNavigation

It presents the map in a cartographic style similar to the Esri Vector Basemap Navigation map. The default place labels are shown in the local language for where the label appears. For example, labels for places in France are shown in French.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyleInfo
member.summary: Information about a given available from the Basemap Styles Service, including human-readable name, thumbnail url and customization options.

remarks.para: The Basemap Styles Service can be queried to access information about a given . The service provides information about each style, including the human-readable name of the style, the url for the style's preview image (a.k.a thumbnail), and different customization options available with each This read-only class collates the information available for each into a common data structure and makes the information easily accessible to API users.
Customization options include:

list.item: Specific languages (e.g. "Chinese (Simplified)").
Language Strategies (e.g. "local").

remarks.para: If a customization is not available for a given , the list for that parameter will be empty.

The class is intended to help a developer determine valid values for properties on the , which will be dependent on the style being used. A developer could use this information to present options to an application user, for example via UI elements, or to prevent errors occurring when loading a customized basemap.

member.remarks: If the collection is empty, the language of labels cannot be customized on this .
member.value: A list of objects.

If the list is empty, the representation of disputed boundaries cannot be customized on this .

member.summary: Gets the human-readable name of the language (e.g. "Portuguese (Brazil)").
member.remarks: To see the latest list of languages supported by the Basemap Styles Service, and the display name associated with each language, see the Languages section of the Basemap Styles Service documentation.
member.value: Human-readable name of the language (e.g. "Portuguese (Brazil)").

member.summary: Gets the CLDR code for this language.
member.remarks: The format is a string that uses a two letter language code (e.g. "en" for English) optionally followed by a two letter country code (e.g. "en-US" for US English). To see the latest list of languages supported by the Basemap Styles Service, and the code associated with each language, see the Languages section of the Basemap Styles Service documentation.
member.value: The CLDR code for this language.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyleLanguageStrategy
member.summary: Options for setting the language on a created from a .

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyleLanguageStrategy.Default
member.summary: Use the default language setting for the .
member.remarks: Currently, for ArcGIS styles, the default behavior is global labels for ArcGIS styles and local labels for OpenStreetMap styles.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyleLanguageStrategy.Global
member.summary: Use the global language (English) for basemap labels.
member.remarks: This is the default setting for ArcGIS basemap styles.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyleLanguageStrategy.Local
member.summary: Uses local place names for basemap labels.

remarks.para: This option uses the names of places in their local language - for example, "Lisboa" for "Lisbon" and "北京" for "Beijing".
This is the default setting for OpenStreetMap basemap styles.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyleLanguageStrategy.ApplicationLocale
member.summary: Attempt to use the system locale language for basemap labels.
member.remarks: Refer to for a description of the behavior if the system's locale is an unsupported language.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyleParameters
member.summary: Provides parameters for creating a from a .

remarks.para: allows you to set preferences, such as the language to use for basemap labels, when creating a that suits your application.
build upon a to adjust settings for how that style is displayed. For example, you can choose from a set of options that determine the language for displaying labels on the basemap.

list.item: Set to specify a desired language for basemap labels.
Set to use local language, use global language, or attempt to use the application locale.

remarks.para: Once you are finished configuring the options for your , they can be used with a to create a . When the is loaded, the parameters in the are evaluated.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyleParameters.#ctor
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class with default options.

remarks.para: You can change preferences, such as language, by setting properties.
A preference for using global, local, or the system locale language can be set using . To define a preference for a specific language, set the property to a culture with a supported language code.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyleParameters.LanguageStrategy
member.summary: Gets or sets the option for setting the language for basemap labels.

remarks.para: This property allows you to specify an option for the language to use for basemap labels. You can use global or local place names or attempt to use the system locale. If you want to use a specific language, set the property. Note that the property takes precedence over the . If the is not null, the will be ignored.
The default value is

member.value: The option for setting the language for basemap labels.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyleParameters.SpecificLanguage
member.summary: Gets or sets the culture that determines the desired language for place labels on the . Use null if no specific language is required.

remarks.para: The culture should represent a supported locale. Specifying the region is required for Portugese and Chinese, and optional for other languages.

The list of supported language codes is shown in the table below. To see the latest list of languages supported by the Basemap Styles Service, see the Languages section of the Basemap Styles Service documentation:

If a supported language code is supplied, the labels are displayed in the identified language. If an unsupported language is used, the label language falls back to the closest available language. For example, American English (en-US) or Canadian French (fr-CA) are not currently supported. These are interpreted as English and French respectively. If no suitable fallback language code is found, labels use the default language for the style.
If set, this property takes precedence over the .
The default value is null.

member.value: The desired language for place labels on the or null if no specific language is required.
member.exception: Given CultureInfo is ambiguous; use a region-specific culture instead.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStyleParameters.Worldview
member.summary: Gets or sets the desired representation of a boundary between countries on a where the location of the boundary is disputed.

remarks.para: This parameter defines the boundary lines and labels displayed on a . If no value is defined, the style will use the standard set of boundary lines and labels defined by the style. Note that there may be cases where labels for a given disputed area are not available in all languages. In that case, the language for the label may change by falling back to the default.
For the up-to-date list of supported world views used by the basemap styles service, see the documentation for the worldview parameter.
If customization of disputed boundaries is not required, leave this property set to null.
World views can only be displayed on some ArcGIS basemap styles and cannot be displayed on Open Street Map (OSM) styles.

member.value: The that defines how to represent boundaries between countries where the location of the boundary is disputed, or null if no specific representation is required.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStylesServiceInfo.StylesInfo
member.summary: Gets a list of objects.

remarks.para: Each object contains information about a style in the Basemap Styles Service, including:

list.item: Name of style.
URL to access the thumbnail for a style.
Customizations options available for a style (e.g. label languages).

remarks.para: The information in each object is retrieved from the service each time the is loaded, allowing any new customization options added to the Basemap Style Service to be automatically integrated into an application.

The ability to access the latest customization options available in the Basemap Style Service allows applications to be created that dynamically update to include the latest features. For example, if the language of labels on a basemap is a customization option available in an applications preferences, specifying the available languages using the results of a query to the Basemap Style Service ensures an application user has access to all of the available languages. If the service adds a new language, the list of languages in the preferences will automatically be updated.

member.value: A read-only list of objects.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.BasemapStylesServiceInfo.Version
member.summary: Gets the service's version.
member.value: The service's version.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.Camera
member.summary: Used for defining the perspective of a scene. It is a visual snapshot of an observation of the Earth with various layers displayed in a SceneView.

remarks.para: A Camera object can be thought of as a camera that you look through to see a viewable area of a scene. What you see depends on how you orientate the camera and how far it is above the . A camera has four main configurable properties:

list.item: Location - The 3D point in space where the camera is located.

Heading - The angle around the z-axis the camera is rotated. The angle is clockwise from north in the East, North, Up (ENU) ground reference frame. The value is between 0 to 360. 0 is looking North and 90 is looking East.
Pitch - The angle around the y-axis the camera is rotated. The value is between 0 to 180. 0 is looking straight down, 180 is looking straight up.
Roll - The angle around the x-axis the camera is rotated. The value is between 0 to 360. 0 is horizontal, 180 is upside down.

remarks.para: You can construct a Camera based on these values or you can obtain it from the scene view's current viewpoint using . The Camera is immutable which means that once it is created it you cannot modify it.

You can define the user's camera interactions using the scene view's camera controller. The default camera controller () allows users to freely move and focus the camera anywhere in the scene. Other camera controllers provide specialized behavior, such as:

list.item: The locks the camera to maintain focus on a (possibly moving) .
The locks the camera to orbit and to maintain focus on a fixed location.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.Camera.#ctor(System.Double,System.Double,System.Double,System.Double,System.Double,System.Double)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class with the specified latitude, longitude, altitude, heading, pitch, and roll.
member.param: The angular deltaDistance, measured in degrees north or south of the equator.

The angular deltaDistance, usually expressed in degrees, minutes, and seconds, of the location of a point on the earth's surface east or west of an arbitrarily defined meridian (usually the Greenwich prime meridian).
The altitude of the camera position in meters. If the altitude is below the and the is set to , the camera will be located at the . Note: that the default is .

The angle around the z-axis the camera is rotated. The angle is clockwise from north in the East, North, Up (ENU) ground reference frame. The value is between 0 to 360. 0 is looking North and 90 is looking East. Values are wrapped around so that they fall within 0 to 360.

The angle around the y-axis the camera is rotated in the East, North, Up (ENU) ground reference frame. The value is between 0 to 180. 0 is looking straight down and 180 is looking straight up. A negative value defaults to 0 and a value greater than 180 defaults to 180. If the behavior of a negative pitch is required, then the corresponding transformation with positive pitch can be set instead. For example, if heading:0 pitch:-20 roll:0 is required then heading:180 pitch:20 roll:180 can be used instead.

The angle around the x-axis the camera is rotated in the East, North, Up (ENU) ground reference frame. The value is between 0 to 360. 0 is horizontal, 180 is upside down. Values are wrapped so that they fall within 0 to 360.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.Camera.MoveTo(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Geometry.MapPoint)
member.summary: Returns a new with a new center position. Location is the point in space where the camera is located. Setting the location can be done using this Method or establish the location as part of one of the Camera Constructors.

remarks.para: A Camera object can literally be thought of as a camera that one would look through to see a viewable extent and perspective of the Earth. Depending on how you hold the camera and how far away from the ground will determine what you can see. These positions of holding the camera represent the various Properties that you can set on the Camera object.

The Location Property can be thought of physical position on/above the Earth that the camera is sitting when you look though it. To have a valid location for a camera, the MapPoint.X, MapPoint.Y, and MapPoint.Z Properties should all be set to have a valid 3D position. It is possible to have a MapPoint.Z elevation value be zero (0) and that would mean the camera is sitting directly on the surface of the Earth. The elevation Z value of the MapPoint is assumed to be in Meters. The Camera object assumes that MapPoint values are in the Latitude (Y) and Longitude (X) coordinate system.

member.param: The point in space where camera is located.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.Camera.RotateTo(System.Double,System.Double,System.Double)
member.summary: Returns a new with the specified heading, pitch and roll values.
member.param: The angle around the z-axis the new camera is rotated. The angle is clockwise from north in the East, North, Up (ENU) ground reference frame. The value is between 0 to 360. 0 is looking North and 90 is looking East. Values are wrapped around so that they fall within 0 to 360.

The angle around the y-axis the camera is rotated in the East, North, Up (ENU) ground reference frame. The value is between 0 to 180. 0 is looking straight down and 180 is looking straight up. A negative value defaults to 0 and a value greater than 180 defaults to 180. If the behavior of a negative pitch is required, then the corresponding transformation with positive pitch can be set instead. For example, if heading:0 pitch:-20 roll:0 is required then heading:180 pitch:20 roll:180 can be used instead.

The angle around the x-axis the new camera is rotated in the East, North, Up (ENU) ground reference frame. The value is between 0 to 360. 0 is horizontal, 180 is upside down. Values are wrapped so that they fall within 0 to 360.

member.summary: Gets the type of expiration for the given package.

it can no longer be used and will fail to load ().

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.FeatureForms.FeatureForm.EvaluateExpressionsAsync
member.summary: Asynchronously evaluates all form expressions.

remarks.para: An expression can include Arcade FeatureSet functions, which need to be evaluated asynchronously. Upon completion the task provides a set of s providing diagnostic information about errors encountered during evaluation of expressions. An empty list indicates no errors were encountered.
The are fully populated and each placeholder is replaced by its value.

This method must be called before displaying the feature form in a UI so you can use synchronous methods on form elements. It should also be called after every call to , in order to run any expressions which may depend on that value.
This method will load the if it is not already loaded.

member.returns: The task object representing the asynchronous evaluation of all contained Arcade expressions.

member.summary: Saves edits made using the to the database.

member.returns: The task object representing the save operation.

member.remarks: An empty dictionary indicates that all visible and editable field form element values are valid. Constraints are expressed by the and value of of the element. The constraints depend on the type of . A PropertyChanged event for this property is raised when the validation errors of visible and editable change. This includes validation errors arising from changes in the element's visibility or ability to be edited. This dictionary contains validation errors for visible and editable field form elements only. For other field form elements, use the property.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.FeatureTilingMode
member.summary: Feature tiling modes available to feature layers. This determines how feature tiling is handled for each layer. Using the default mode is highly recommended. As of now, only services based on service feature tables support feature tiling.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.FeatureTilingMode.EnabledWhenSupported
member.summary: Use feature tiling whenever a service supports it. Feature tiling retrieves more features per layer from a service by making multiple, smaller requests to said service. Since each request gathers a portion of the total features, this allows more features to be rendered in the extent than what the would normally allow.
member.remarks: When in this mode, all rendering requests to services will use feature tiling unless the following applies: is , the service does not support coordinate quantization, or if the service does not support tile result types. In general, Map Services do not support feature tiling, while all Feature Services do. This is the default mode.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.FeatureTilingMode.Disabled
member.summary: Feature tiling is disabled.
member.remarks: When in this mode, feature tiling will not be used even if a service supports it. This reduces the number of requests sent by each layer. Disabling feature tiling results in less features returned from a service.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.FeatureTilingMode.EnabledWithFullResolutionWhenSupported
member.summary: Use feature tiling and request full resolution geometries when supported by the service. If the latter is not supported, is used instead.
member.remarks: This mode requires the service to support the edit quantization mode, which returns all vertices of the source geometry in a high resolution. Use this mode for workflows that benefit from precise geometry editing, such as snapping feature geometries, without loading the feature. This mode may impact performance due to the increase in network traffic.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.Floor.FacilityLayerDefinition

member.remarks: The layer contains the footprints of managed facilities. A facility represents any occupiable structure, such as an office or campus building, retail setting, or industrial structure. Facilities have one or more floor levels and are optionally grouped into sites.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.Floor.FloorManager
member.summary: Manages the data displayed by a floor-aware map or scene, allowing filtering based on floor levels.

remarks.para: A must be loaded before you can access its site, facility, and level properties.

When a map's floor manager is loaded, floor-aware layers display only data associated with the ground floor by default. When a scene's floor manager is loaded, floor-aware layers display data from all floors by default. You can filter the data displayed by floor-aware layers by toggling the property of levels in the floor manager.
The will fail to load if:

list.item: The map/scene associated with the floor manager is not floor-aware.
The mandatory facility or level layer does not exist.
It is unable to get the facilities or levels from their respective layers.
It is unable to get the sites from a specified site layer.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel
member.summary: A base class for or , which contain layers and additional properties and can be displayed in a .

remarks.para:
The base class GeoModel represents the model in a model-view-controller (MVC) architecture, while represents the view. To display a map or scene to the user, pass to its appropriate geo view, as follows:
Pass a to a map view.
Pass a to a scene view.

has many properties that are common to both maps and scenes. For example:
Each map or scene typically has a to help orient the users.
The collection gives you access to the vector data (points, line, polygons), raster data, and attribute information provided by the or .
The determines how spatial data relates to real-world space.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.AllLayers
member.summary: Gets all layers in the order they are rendered (bottom-most first).
member.value: All layers.
member.remarks:
Layers are returned in the following order:
- Basemap Layers
- Operational Layers
- Basemap Reference layers

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.Basemap
member.summary: Gets or sets the basemap for this map or scene.

remarks.para: A basemap provides a background of geographical context for the content you display in a map or scene. It is an optional set of non-editable layers such as aerial imagery, roads, or landmarks, that help orient the user of the map or scene.

A basemap is composed of a collection of base layers () and reference layers (). Base layers are displayed at the bottom of a map or scene, and reference layers are displayed at the top, with the between them.
You can use ready-to-use basemaps, style your own basemaps with the ArcGIS Vector Tile Style Editor, or create and publish your own with ArcGIS Pro.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.Bookmarks
member.summary: Gets the collection of bookmarks defined for this map or scene.
member.remarks: Bookmarks allow end users to quickly navigate to a particular area of interest in a map or scene.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.InitialViewpoint
member.summary: Gets or sets the initial viewing state when the map or scene is first displayed.

remarks.para: The initial viewpoint value is available when the map or scene is loaded. If you want to change the initial viewpoint, you can do this before you add the map or scene to a geo view. At this point, the geo view's viewpoint is set to this initial viewpoint. Any subsequent changes to the are ignored.

If you want to change the viewpoint of a displayed map or scene, use geo view methods such as:
for instantly changing the visible area with no animation.
for instantly moving the perspective in 3D scenes.
for smooth animated viewpoint changes in 2D maps.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.Item
member.summary: Gets the ArcGIS Item associated with this map or scene.
member.remarks: This can be a (for web maps and webscenes loaded from a portal or saved to a portal), or a (for maps in a map package or scenes in a scene package), or null if no is associated with this map or scene.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.LoadSettings
member.summary: Gets or sets the properties that control the default loading and rendering behavior of layers in this map or scene.
member.remarks: For example, you can specify which tiling mode should be used when feature layers are added, or specify whether feature tables should use advanced symbology.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.OperationalLayers
member.summary: Gets or sets the collection of operational layers that can access geographic data from a file or a service.

remarks.para: The operational layers collection is used to display geographic data layers on top of a basemap layer in a or . For example, you can display a fleet of vehicles being tracked on a map or display a point cloud layer of a tree canopy in a scene.

This collection of layers is unique to the map or scene, it cannot be used by another . You can add and remove layers from the map or scene by adding and removing them from this collection. Ensure that the map has finished loading, otherwise you will replace the map's original operational layers with the newly added layers.

The first layer in the collection is drawn first (on the bottom) above the basemap layer. Each subsequent layer is drawn on top. Typically, imagery or tile layers are added to the collection first, and then polygon, line, and point layers last. When you pass the to the geo view it combines these layers to create the final display for the user.
See Data Layers) for more information about working with operational layers.
A instance can only belong to one map or scene at a time. To reuse a layer, either it or remove it from its previous map or scene first.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.SpatialReference
member.summary: Gets the spatial reference for this map or scene.

remarks.para: specifies how geometry coordinates relate to real-world space. It ensures that you can accurately view, query, and analyze the layers of a map or scene.
The spatial reference value is null until the or is loaded. You can set this value explicitly using the, use or constructors.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.Tables
member.summary: Gets the list of tables in the map or scene. Unlike , tables are not displayed by the geo view.
member.remarks: The collection of feature tables is specific to the map or scene. You can add and remove tables by adding or removing them from the collection. By default, the tables are not loaded. You can load them explicitly () or load them by calling methods such as .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.TransportationNetworks
member.summary: Gets the collection of transportation network datasets defined for this map or scene.

remarks.para: Map and scene authors can use ArcGIS Pro to create mobile map or scene packages that include transportation networks. If the map or scene is created from one of these packages, this collection will be populated with a read-only collection of objects.
A from this collection can be used to construct one of the network analysis tasks (such as , , or .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.Version
member.summary: Gets the version of the or , read when it is loaded. The version of a newly created or is empty.

remarks.para: When you load an existing map or scene, the version value is the version that it was created at, according to the ESRI web map specification or ESRI web scene specification, respectively.

You can make changes to a map (associated with a ) and save it back to the web map it originated from. Alternatively, you can save a map as a new web map on a specified with a given title and folder. In these cases, the version of the saved map will be the version of the ESRI web map specification supported by this API. You cannot save changes to maps in mobile map packages.
Saving changes to scenes is not currently supported.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.FloorDefinition
member.summary: Gets or sets the properties that allow a map or a scene to be floor-aware.
member.remarks: Floor-aware maps and scenes contain data representing floor plan and indoor features. The data displayed by floor-aware maps and scenes can be filtered based on floor levels using the . This property is null for maps or scenes that are not floor-aware.
member.value: The associated ; or null if this map or scene is not floor-aware.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.FloorManager
member.summary: Gets the object that manages the data displayed for a floor-aware map or scene, allowing filtering based on floor levels.

remarks.para: A must be loaded before you can access its properties and perform floor filtering.

When a map's floor manager is loaded, floor-aware layers display only data associated with the ground floor by default. When a scene's floor manager is loaded, floor-aware layers display data from all floors by default. You can filter the data displayed by floor-aware layers by toggling the property of levels in the floor manager.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.GeoModel.GeotriggersInfo
member.summary: Gets an object that presents information about the set of objects defined for the map or scene.

remarks.para: You must load the to populate the collection with any geotriggers defined by the author of the map or scene. If no geotriggers are defined this list will remain empty.
Use a to check each of the conditions defined in .
When you save the map or scene any objects that reference local data (for example, a ) will be omitted.

member.value: An object that presents information on the set of objects defined for the map or scene.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition
member.summary: Describes an object that defines the text, appearance, and position of labels for features within a given scale range via ArcGIS JSON.

remarks.para: A describes a class of labels.
A collection of label definitions can be attached to a , a , a , or an of a dynamic .
Each specifies:

list.item: Which features are covered by the definition, by applying the SQL constraint.
What text should be displayed, based on the individual feature's attributes and the chosen formula.
How the text should appear, using the label definition's .
Where the text should appear, with respect to its feature, using the .
How to move or avoid other labels or features that would overlap each label.

member.name: M:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.#ctor(Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.Labeling.LabelExpression,Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Symbology.TextSymbol)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class with the specified and .
member.remarks: The label expression may be a combination of literal text and attribute values from the feature being labeled. The syntax of the expression is determined by which label expression object is used: , or . Warning: If the expression attempts to use attributes that don't exist, then labels will not be produced. This is distinct from the attribute existing but having a null or empty value, which can form part of the produced text label. Likewise, if no has been set, then no labels can be created.
member.param: The expression script used to calculate the label text.
The text symbol which describes the overall appearance of label text (e.g. font and size).

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.DeconflictionStrategy
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for moving labels to avoid overlapping feature, annotation, dimension or graphic symbols or higher priority labels. In 3D is selected by default.
member.remarks: The default is (which will be interpreted as i.e. use preferred location unless it would overlap an obstacle).

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.Expression
member.summary: Gets or sets the expression script used to calculate the label text.

remarks.para: The expression may be a combination of literal text and attribute values from the feature being labeled.
The syntax of the expression is determined by which label expression implementation is used: , , or .
A label expression using one scripting language can be replaced by an expression using a different scripting language just by creating an object of the new subclass and assigning it to .
The default value is an empty , which will evaluate to an empty text label.
The expression needs to create a text string which will be used as the label text. The text string can be any legal UTF16 characters.

The text string can also include HTML-style formatting tags. Example: "Hello <BOL>World</BOL>". In this example, the angle-bracketed tags will not appear in the label, but may change the presentation of the text. In this example, the "World" will appear in bold.
The available tags are:

list.item: "<BOL></BOL>" for bold text
"<UND></UND$gt;" for underlined text
"<ITA></ITA>" for italic text
"<CLR red='256'></CLR>" for colored text
"<FNT name='Arial' size='18'></FNT>" for text using a different size or font (if available on client)

remarks.para: Tags can be upper or lower case, but not a mixture. Tags can be nested, example: "<ITA>Hello <BOL>World</BOL></ITA>".
Full documentation of the tag parameters is available at Text formatting tags.

Note that not all ArcGISPro tags are currently supported in this API. The "<CLR>" tag is currently only supported for text in 2D views. Any unsupported tags will be ignored and not written as part of the label text.

Warning: If the expression attempts to use attributes which don't exist then no label will be produced. This is distinct from the attribute existing but having a null or empty value, which can form part of the produced text label.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.FeatureBoundaryOverlapStrategy
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for whether other labels are allowed to overlap the boundary of polygon features/graphics being labeled by this . In 3D scenes, labels will be allowed to overlap the boundaries of polygon features.
member.remarks: The default is , which will give the same behavior as .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.FeatureInteriorOverlapStrategy
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for whether other labels are allowed to overlap the interior of polygon features/graphics being labeled by this . In 3D scenes, labels will be allowed to overlap the interiors of polygon features.
member.remarks: The default is , which will give the same behavior as .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.LabelOverlapStrategy
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for whether other labels are allowed to overlap labels created by this . In 3D, labels will not be allowed to overlap other labels. Overlapping labels will not be displayed until the user zooms in further so that the labels don't overlap.
member.remarks: The default is which will give the same behavior as .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.LineConnection
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for whether line features with the same label, and matching end vertices, should be joined before sharing a label. 3D line features will be treated as independent features with their own label.
member.remarks: The default is , which will be interpreted as .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.LineOrientation
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for whether above or below a line geometry will be interpreted as above or below on the "screen," or with respect to the "direction" of line's geometry (that is, the digitization order in which the vertices are listed).

remarks.para: If the is set to then above means the label will be offset perpendicularly from its line segment towards the top of the screen. If the is set to then above means the label will be offset perpendicularly left from its line segment. If the is set to then the label will be offset perpendicularly to whichever side of the line geometry has space (defaulting to above in the "screen" sense). "below" would have the corresponding interpretations.
The default is .

member.value: The strategy for interpreting above or below a line geometry.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.MaxScale
member.summary: Gets or sets the maximum scale at which labels will be visible. In 3D scenes, the scale range is between individual label and view plane instead of map and view plane.
member.remarks: Labels will only be visible when the viewing scale is smaller than (or equal to) the . For example, if the viewing scale is 1:12000 and the is 1:10000, then the labels will be visible (because 1/12000 <= 1/10000). A of 0, which is the default, indicates no upper limit on the viewing scale.
member.value: Scale denominator (e.g. `10000d` means 1:10000 scale).

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.MinScale
member.summary: Gets or sets the minimum scale at which labels will be visible. In 3D scenes, the scale range is between individual label and view plane instead of map and view plane.
member.remarks: Labels will only be visible when the viewing scale is larger than (or equal to) the . For example, if the viewing scale is 1:12000 and the is 1:20000, then the labels will be visible (because 1/12000 >= 1/20000). A of 0, which is the default, indicates no lower limit on the viewing scale.
member.value: Scale denominator (e.g. `20000d` means 1:20000 scale).

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.MultipartStrategy
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for how many labels should be placed on each polyline or polygon (multi-ring) feature.
member.remarks: The default is .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.Name
member.summary: Gets or sets the text name for the class of labels.
member.remarks: This name is assigned by the map author to clarify the purpose of this label definition. It is used for display or debugging purposes. If no name is explicitly assigned, then an empty text string is stored.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.OffsetDistance
member.summary: Gets or sets the distance (in points i.e. 1/72 inches) that text should be moved away from its features.

remarks.para: The distance is measured from the point symbol radius or line feature symbology edge. The distance is measured on the screen (i.e. not in the map) in points, comparable to the text font size. This can be a negative value, to pull the label closer to the feature if, for example, the average size for a point symbol is overly conservative for the actual graphic shown.
If no value is set, then 1 point is assumed.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.OverrunStrategy
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for whether a label will be visible at scales where the feature is too small for the label to fit. Not supported by 3D scenes.
member.remarks: The default is which will give different behavior depending on the type of feature that the label might overrun.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.Placement
member.summary: Gets or sets the preferred position of the text label, with respect to its feature geometry.
member.remarks:
The default is . The effect of this is the same as one of the following, depending on the type of feature geometry:
for point features.
for line features.
for polygon features.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.Priority
member.summary: Gets or sets a number that defines which labels are placed first, and can also supplant existing lower-priority labels (e.g. during panning).

remarks.para: Lower values indicate more important labels i.e.

list.item: 0 is the most important.
5 is high priority.
15 is medium priority.
Higher values indicate lower priority.

remarks.para: If set to -1 or not set at all, then default values are used, depending on the geometry types of the features:

list.item: 12 for point features.
15 for line features.
18 for polygon features.

remarks.para: This value does not have to be an integer because it is just a continuum of values for comparison. Users can use this to specify that a class of labels should have priority between two other classes with close priorities.

For example, PreferredRoutes might be given priority 13 to appear ahead of general line feature labels, but after general point feature labels. And HistoricSites might be given priority 12.5 to appear ahead of PreferredRoute labels, but still after general point feature labels.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.RemoveDuplicatesDistance
member.summary: Gets or sets a value that controls the duplicate thinning radius (in points i.e. 1/72 inches).
member.remarks: The distance is measured on the screen (i.e. not in the map) in points, comparable to the text font size. This value is only used if is enabled. The default value is 0. This indicates that duplicates should be removed from the entire extent.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.RemoveDuplicatesStrategy
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for whether labels are removed if other features have the same text label. This can reduce clutter and free up space.
member.remarks: The default is , which will be interpreted as indicating that all labels should be shown if possible. The distance within which to look for duplicates is controlled by .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.RepeatDistance
member.summary: Gets or sets The distance apart (in points i.e. 1/72 inches) that the repetitions should be. In 3D scenes, only one label will be placed per line feature.

remarks.para: This value is only used if is enabled.
The distance is measured on the screen along the line feature. As the user zooms in closer, two repetitions move further apart on the screen, until there is room for a new repetition between them.
The default is 216 points i.e. line labels should be repeated approximately every 3 inches along long features.
The distance is considered a guide for the approximate label separation and should not be used for precise distance markings.
A distance of 0 disables label repetition.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.RepeatStrategy
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for whether a label should have multiple copies created and placed along or across the same feature. In 3D scenes, only label will be placed per line feature.
member.remarks: The default is . The frequency of repetition is controlled by the property. This property is currently only supported for line features, but may be extended to polygons in the future.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.StackAlignment
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for how multi-row (stacked) text should be aligned.

remarks.para: Multi-row text labels can be horizontally aligned on the left, on the right, or at its center. This preference can be specified by the assigned to the . By using the property, the user can choose to use of the for all labels, or can choose to have the most aesthetic alignment calculated case-by-case for each label depending on its placement position around its feature. This is particularly useful for labels of dense point features, where labels may move above, below, left, or right of their point symbol, in order to fit on the display.
This property has no effect if is .
The default value is (which has the same effect on labels as ).

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.StackBreakPosition
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for whether a row of text should be broken before or after it exceeds the ideal length.

remarks.para: If stacking is turned on, label placement can insert a line break before or after the word that overruns the maximum number of characters per row. Using the option means rows will generally be shorter than the (although will overrun for individual words larger than this count).
This property has no effect if is .
The default is (which has the same effect on labels as ).

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.StackRowLength
member.summary: Gets or sets a value that limits the number of characters in a row of stacked text.

remarks.para: This length guides the decision on when to break long text strings into separate rows. The line break will be inserted between words, not in the middle of a word, so rows may be longer or shorter than the ideal. Depending on , the break may be inserted before the breaking word, or after.
The default value is -1. This means that a default length of 13 characters is currently used.
This property has no effect if is .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.StackSeparators
member.summary: Gets a collection of stack separators that should be used for automatic line breaking of label text.

remarks.para: Each specifies a code point to be looked for in the text. A code point is often thought of as a single character in the text, but may need several chars in a to describe it. So each is a intended to describe one code point.
For example:

list.item: To use a comma as a separator, the string would be just ",".
To use a Hebrew punctuation Paseq as a separator, the would need to encode the code point with utf16 value \u05c0.
To use higher range unicode code points such as Aegean Word Separator Dot which has utf32 hex value 10101, the would need to encode the code point with utf16 value \ud800\udd01.

remarks.para: If it is found, then the also specifies whether:

list.item: The text should always be broken here.
Whether the separator code point should remain visible.
Whether the separator code point should appear at the end of one row, or the beginning of the next.

remarks.para: Three default separators are provided: a comma, a space, and a hyphen. Custom separators can be added, and default separators can be removed if not required.
Note that a carriage-return (\n) in the label will always be considered a forced separator.
This property has no effect if = .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.StackStrategy
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for whether the text should be stacked or wrapped, rather than placed as long trailing labels across the map.
member.remarks: This property controls whether stacking is allowed, or not, or should be calculated automatically based on feature type. The default is , meaning that stacking will be enabled for point and polygon features and disabled for line features.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.TextLayout
member.summary: Gets or sets the layout of the text, which can be either horizontal, straight, perpendicular, or follow the line feature.

remarks.para: Optional specification of whether, once the text is positioned, it should be written:

list.item: Horizontally.
Straight (i.e.parallel) to the tangent to the line feature.
Straight in line with the point feature positioning angle.
Perpendicular to the tangent or positioning angle.
Following the geometry of the line feature.

remarks.para: The default is , which will be interpreted as:

list.item: for labels attached to point features.
for labels attached to line features.
for labels attached to polygon features.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.TextOrientation
member.summary: Gets or sets the orientation of the text, which can be either angle direction or screen-oriented. Not supported by 3D scenes.

remarks.para: Optional specification of whether text should follow the placement angle direction even if it means being rendered upside-down, or whether text should be flipped through 180 degrees to keep it screen-oriented. This setting will take effect whenever the feature geometry determines that the text is angled upside-down, or if the map is rotated within the screen to make the text upside-down (with respect to the screen).
The default is , which is interpreted as .

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.TextSymbol
member.summary: Gets or sets text symbol which describes the overall appearance of label text (e.g. font and size).
member.remarks: If no has been set, then no labels can be created.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.UseCodedValues
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether the data source should translate domain identifiers into meanings using a .

remarks.para: For any translation to occur, the data source must have one or more set up for the attribute fields.

This property only affects Simple label expressions. Arcade expressions can use functions to specify when code translations should be carried out. Coded value translation for web map expressions is not currently supported.
The default value is true, meaning translation will be done when possible.

member.value: true if the data source should translate domain identifiers into meanings using a , false otherwise.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.WhereClause
member.summary: Gets or sets the SQL WHERE clause expression that limits the set of features for which labels will be generated and placed.

remarks.para: This is a SQL where clause that can refer to the attributes of the feature, for example:

list.item: NAME LIKE 'A%' (Only label features with NAME attribute values starting with 'A').
(LENGTH > CAPACITY) and (TYPE = 1) (Only label features whose attributes satisfy the SQL condition).

remarks.para: The default value is an empty string, which will allow all features in the data source and extent to be labeled.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.Angle
member.summary: Gets or sets the that specifies angular positions and layout directions for labels on or around point feature symbols.

remarks.para: This optional property specifies how to position a label following the direction of an angle. The angular position may be different for each feature (driven by one or more feature attributes) or constant for all features (specified by a fixed number).

The value will still be used to indicate whether offset or centered positioning is required, but the exact position will be overridden by the angle calculated for the feature. Once the position has been determined, and are used to specify the layout of the text at that position.

Warning: If the expression attempts to use attributes which don't exist then no label will be produced. This is distinct from the attribute existing but having a null or empty value, which will be interpreted as a zero angle.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.GeometryStrategy
member.summary: Gets or sets the strategy for how to use the geometry of the feature when calculating the label position.
member.remarks: Only applicable to labels for lines or polygons.
member.value: The strategy for how to use the geometry of the feature when calculating the label position.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.UnknownJson
member.summary: Gets a collection of unknown data from the source JSON.
member.remarks: Unknown JSON is a dictionary of values not defined in the ArcGIS specification used to create this object but found in the source JSON. If the object is written back to JSON, any unknown JSON data is not persisted. The ArcGIS specification may be for a web map, web scene, REST API, and so on.

member.name: P:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelDefinition.UnsupportedJson
member.summary: Gets a collection of unsupported data from the source JSON.
member.remarks: Unsupported JSON is a dictionary of values defined in the ArcGIS specification used to create this object but not currently used in this API. If the object is written back to JSON, any unsupported JSON data is persisted. The ArcGIS specification may be from a web map, web scene, REST API, and so on.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelGeometryStrategy
member.summary: The strategy for how to use the geometry of the feature when calculating the label position.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelGeometryStrategy.Automatic
member.summary: Labeling will use the default depending on the feature type.
member.remarks:
This behavior depends on Feature type:
Polygon labels will use
Line labels will use

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelGeometryStrategy.UseGeometry
member.summary: The feature's entire geometry will be used to calculate the position of the label.
member.remarks: This may result in a label not being visible because the preferred location is outside the extent. For example, a line label placed at the start or end of the line might be off-screen if the start or end of the feature geometry are off-screen.

member.name: F:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.LabelGeometryStrategy.UseClippedGeometry
member.summary: The subset of the feature's geometry within the screen extent will be used to calculate the position of the label.
member.remarks: The label is more likely to be visible on-screen, if there is room. However, the label may move position as the user pans the screen and different parts of the feature geometry are used to calculate the label position.

member.name: T:Esri.ArcGISRuntime.Mapping.Labeling.ArcadeLabelExpression
member.summary: An expression script using the Arcade language.

remarks.para: The Arcade label expression property must contain a syntactically correct Arcade script expression. An example expression that combines text with a field value is "State: " + $feature.State_Name.
Note that quotes are needed around the literal text. See ArcGIS Arcade for full documentation on the Arcade scripting language and its function library.

Any Arcade functions allowed for the Labeling profile can be used in a label arcade expression. Other functions (for example, FeatureSet functions) are potentially too slow to be used in a dynamic label evaluation.

Warning: If the expression attempts to use attributes which don't exist then no label will be produced. This is distinct from the attribute existing but having a null or empty value, which can form part of the produced text label.

member.remarks: An empty script is a valid Arcade expression that will evaluate to an empty string.

member.summary: How the placement angle should be interpreted.

member.summary: The angle uses the default interpretation ().
